title: Izzet NPC Tactics
content_text:
If you’re of the opinion that mad scientists are generally less desirable than your common or garden-variety scientist, then the Izzet League is not for you. Its stable of technomages and arcane engineers is supplemented by the scorchbringer guard and a variety of magic weapon field testers called “blastseekers.” Those names could have used a few more rounds of field testing, honestly. Izzet should encourage its members to take more credits in the humanities.

The scorchbringer guard is a short-range shock attacker with high Dexterity and above-average Strength and Constitution. Its ideal positioning is at least 10 feet from its nearest foe but within 30 feet of at least two of them, in a firing line with its allies spaced 15 feet apart. This formation confers two key benefits:

A single scorchbringer guard can reliably use their scorchbringer—a flamethrower with a 30-foot range—against two foes at once. Since two points define a line, a scorchbringer guard can always hit two enemies as long as they’re within range, so they focus chiefly on doing that. Three or more is exceptional good fortune; a scorchbringer guard will seize the opportunity, but they aren’t going to go out of their way to make it happen.

Fifteen-foot spacing means that a scorchbringer guard doesn’t cause a chain reaction if their fuel tank explodes. Being chaotic neutral in alignment, they’re not what you’d call exceptionally disciplined, but they do know what happens when their weapons go boom. When they break formation, which happens often, it’s always to move farther apart, not closer together.

Scorchbringer guards aren’t overly concerned about being charged by melee opponents, since their main weapon isn’t used to make ranged attacks and therefore isn’t penalized for use at point-blank range. They simply circle around their foes, staying within reach, until there’s another foe directly behind the immediate one, then fire their scorchbringers again. If that weapon is still recharging, a scorchbringer guard switches briefly to their Light Hammer melee attack. But any guard whose scorchbringer is recharged and ready to go will prioritize an opponent engaged in melee with one of their allies.

The minimum size of a scorchbringer guard patrol is four. This number makes it at least two-thirds likely that after the first-round scorchbringer volley, at least one guard’s scorchbringer will recharge right away, so that they can keep advancing as a line. A scorchbringer guard doesn’t rush ahead simply because their scorchbringer is on cooldown. They wait their turn.

When every member of a scorchbringer guard patrol either is seriously wounded or dead or has lost its scorchbringer to a fuel tank explosion, the patrol withdraws. Any guard still carrying a working scorchbringer retreats walking backward, facing the enemy, and continues to fire that weapon to cover their allies’ withdrawal. On turns when their scorchbringer doesn’t recharge or all their foes are out of range, they take the Dash action, or Dodge if they’re within melee reach of an enemy (they lack the discipline to Disengage).

Blastseekers are mostly spellslingers, lobbing magic from long range. The ability contour of the counterflux blastseeker, for instance, is defined by exceptional Intelligence and very high Dexterity. They carry a rapier, but they should rarely find themself in a situation where they need to use it. Instead, they rely primarily on scorching ray for damage dealing.

They have a strange trait called Counterflux Overcast that allows them to create a bubble around themself extending 15 feet outward that randomly imposes either advantage or disadvantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects until the end of their next turn. Their inability to control which effect occurs means they have to react to it after the fact. If the bubble grants advantage on saving throws, they reposition to include as many of their allies in it as possible. If it imposes disadvantage, they have a problem—two, actually. The first is that they need to get away from their allies to avoid making their side more vulnerable to enemy magic. The second is that they don’t have a lot of great ways of exploiting an enemy’s disadvantage. Scorching ray, their only damage-dealing spell, calls for an attack roll, not a save.

Under 2014 fifth edition D&D rules, the only way the counterflux blastseeker can exploit a disadvantage bubble is by casting enlarge/reduce to shrink an enemy combatant—specifically, a melee combatant engaged with an ally of the blastseeker. However, the blastseeker doesn’t want to impose disadvantage on the friendly as well as the foe, so they wait until their next turn to rush forward and cast the spell with the enemy inside the bubble. (At the end of their turn, the bubble goes away, so the opposition can’t use it against the blastseeker’s ally.)

The 2024 fifth edition rules offer one other use case: counterspell, which in 5E24 requires a Con save. If a counterflux blastseeker creates a disadvantage bubble, one of its cannier plays is actually to run into the middle of the enemy formation. This act is going to draw all kinds of aggro, of course, so the blastseeker’s allies have to make themselves very distracting to take the heat off. What the blastseeker is hoping to do is to bait out a spell from an enemy caster, which they can then counterspell while that caster has disadvantage on the save to hold it together. But it helps if at least one of the counterflux blastseeker’s allies can take advantage of the situation as well, casting some other spell that the other side will have to save against.

Either way, it’s not a super coherent or effective combination of abilities, and it forces the counterflux blastseeker to behave in ways that aren’t especially compatible with their ability contour and combat roll. Being a mage, of sorts, the counterflux blastseeker is wise to withdraw from combat when they’re only moderately wounded (reduced to 27 hp or fewer); staying in the fight longer betrays a degree of recklessness that’s unbecoming even for the Izzet League. That being said, all blastseekers are excellent at spotting enemy weaknesses, and they cooperate to strike hard and fast at whatever weakness they spot. Since they can cast mage armor three times per day, assume they have the sense to keep it on all the time.

The cosmotronic blastseeker is an exception to the usual blastseeker combat role, their very high Constitution making them more of a short-range support caster than a long-range artillerist. Their Empowered Spell trait lets them reroll up to four dice of damage anytime they cast a damaging spell, and they have to use the new roll; since their damaging spells all use d6s and d8s, and they want to have at least a two-thirds chance of rolling something better, they simply reroll all their 1s and 2s for the first three rounds. It might seem like leaving damage on the table to use Empowered Spell to reroll one damage die on a scorching ray or a thunderwave when fireball lets them roll eight damage dice at once, but since combat often doesn’t last longer than three rounds, it would be leaving more damage on the table to wait too long to use Empowered Spell and not get to reroll any of those four dice.

The cosmotronic blastseeker casts fireball whenever they can catch at least four enemies—and only enemies—within the 20-foot radius of the blast. Under 5E24 rules, they seek to immolate all its enemies at once, or at least 10 of them, whichever is less. If they have allies tangled up among their enemies, fireball is too blunt an instrument, and they fall back on the more surgical scorching ray, targeting foes with lower Armor Classes. Thunderwave is for when the cosmotronic blastseeker is rushed by multiple enemies and there’s no ally in the area of effect. They don’t worry about a lone enemy, and they don’t risk friendly fire, because they also have shield to (hopefully) deflect incoming hits. They cast shield whenever an enemy rolls a 15 to 19 on an attack roll against them or targets them with magic missile. When fireball would cause too much collateral damage and a too-close-for-comfort opponent imposes disadvantage on scorching ray, that’s when the cosmotronic blastseeker sighs and hauls out their warhammer, which they wield two-handed for the extra average point of damage on a hit.

Cosmotronic blastseekers have good saving throws in most of their abilities, but not Wisdom or Charisma. In the first two rounds of combat, they hold onto Tides of Chaos unless they’re targeted by a spell or effect that requires them to make a Wisdom or Charisma save. If they still have it in round 3, having seen what their foes are capable of, they might use it to gain advantage on an attack roll, if they’re making one. Still, its highest and best use is probably gaining advantage on one of those two saves.

A cosmotronic blastseeker commences withdrawal when they’re moderately wounded (reduced to 25 hp or fewer), backing off while continuing to face the enemy and lob ranged attacks if they still can. When seriously wounded (reduced to 14 hp or fewer), they turn tail and Dash away.

The flux blastseeker—not to be confused with the counterflux blastseeker—has extraordinary Intelligence paired with high Dexterity, so they’re another long-range spellslinger. In addition to scorching ray, which they can cast three times per day, they get one casting each of banishment, cone of cold, fireball and ice storm, conveniently allowing them to choose whichever of these will have the greatest effect. And thanks to that Intelligence score, they know which one that is.

Banishment is best against extraplanar entities—fiends, celestials, fey, elementals and many aberrations, plus monstrosities native to the Shadowfell and modrons—with low-to-midrange Charisma and without Charisma save proficiency, because when these creatures go, they stay gone. But even against a creature native to the material plane, it can be handy as long as all the blastseeker’s other enemies are fairly easy to clear away while the banished foe is absent.

Cone of cold requires a clear line of fire, all one’s enemies appropriately arrayed within a 60-foot cone (under 5E14 rules, six enemies are enough if there are more than that), and an opposing side that’s not great at Con saves and not resistant to cold damage.

Fireball calls for at least four (5E14) or 10 (5E24) targets clustered in a 20-foot-radius sphere, who aren’t resistant to fire and aren’t great at Dex saves.

Ice storm requires the same number of targets in a 20-foot-radius cylinder (they can be airborne up to 40 feet off the ground) who aren’t resistant to cold and, again, aren’t great at Dex saves.

There’s also one daily use of dimension door, whose obvious application is evacuation, but the blastseeker’s Fluxbending Overcast trait adds another wrinkle or two.

Like the counterflux blastseeker’s Counterflux Overcast trait (woof, the technobabble is strong today), Fluxbending Overcast immediately follows the casting of a spell, and the blastseeker can’t control which effect they get. But Fluxbending Overcast’s effects are even more questionably useful, and being unable to choose between them doesn’t improve matters at all.

One effect swaps the blastseeker in space with a creature of their choice within 30 feet of them, so to gain benefit from that, either the blastseeker must want to be where the target is (and be OK with the target’s ending up where they are), or they must want the target to be where they are (and be OK with ending up where the target is). The other effect is a sonic boom with a 10-foot radius, centered on the blastseeker—who takes the same amount of damage from it. The damage can be reduced by a Constitution save, which the flux blastseeker isn’t proficient in. There are use cases for each of these effects, but those use cases have very little overlap with each other.

The flux blastseeker is very smart. It knows that none of this makes sense. But it’s going to find a way to make it make sense.

The likelihood of the sonic boom effect means that the flux blastseeker never wants to use Fluxbending Overcast while they’re surrounded by their own allies. Ideally, they want to be surrounded by enemies when they use Fluxbending Overcast. But the flux blastseeker is a spellslinger. If they’re surrounded by enemies, they’re out of position. Badly.

Therefore, Fluxbending Overcast is for use in one of two situations: Either the Izzet side has been thrown into positional disarray, or the flux blastseeker is deliberately breaking formation to take advantage of it.

In the first case, suppose that several enemies have rushed the flux blastseeker’s position. This scenario is an ideal one for Fluxbending Overcast. The blastseeker casts scorching ray at the charging enemies, not overly concerned with whether or not they have disadvantage on their attack rolls because an enemy is too close. Because the point isn’t whether the rays hit—if they do, it’s brûlée on the crème. The point is that the flux blastseeker has to cast a spell (it doesn’t matter which one) to trigger Fluxbending Overcast, and this situation is one of the tiny handful in which both effects are to their benefit. Either they get to swap places with another creature—friend, foe, pigeon on a rooftop—or they set off a sonic boom that, yeah, hurts them … but hurts all the charging foes, too.

In the second case, suppose that the flux blastseeker is accompanied by a blistercoil weird. They cast dimension door, teleporting themself and the weird smack into the middle of an enemy formation. Then they invoke Fluxbending Overcast. There’s a 50 percent chance that they get to teleport back out immediately by swapping places with another creature—one they don’t care about too much (sorry, pigeon)—leaving the blistercoil weird behind to wreck face. There’s also a 50 percent chance that they go boom. Yeah, it hurts. But it hurts all the enemies around it, too. And if the flux blastseeker can get far enough away, they can then lob a scorching ray spell at their weird. Three rays. The first ray to hit it causes it to grow to Large size. The second causes it to grow Huge. The third causes it to grow Gargantuan … and overload at the start of its turn, dealing a fireball’s worth of damage over a 30-foot radius, rather than the usual 20. And the flux blastseeker can do that again on their next turn. And, theoretically, again. Three uses of scorching ray per day, eh? Let’s make the most of them.

Lest you think that the flux blastseeker is a lunatic with no sense of self-preservation … well, you’re not far off, but they are in fact a lunatic with some sense of self-preservation. Once they’re seriously wounded (reduced to 22 hp or fewer), they flee the scene, using dimension door if they still have it and otherwise just Dashing. Until then, if none of their once-per-day spells suggests itself as a fitting solution to the problem in front of them, they just throw scorching rays. The quarterstaff they carry is dead weight.

But boy, though, are they good at identifying key targets and their weaknesses. If there’s a linchpin figure on the enemy side without which the opposition collapses, the flux blastseeker says, “Get that one,” and the entire Izzet side makes sure that the fingered enemy goes down first and fast. The flux blastseeker also keeps their mage armor on 24/7.

Last among the blastseekers is the galvanic blastseeker, almost as smart as the flux blastseeker and a lot more dexterous, but with a spell repertoire that doesn’t match its combat role particularly well. On the one hand, they can cast lightning bolt three times per day, which is a lot. On the other hand, it’s hard to maximize the value of lightning bolt, which requires a minimum of four (5E14) or eight (5E24) targets arranged in a neat line. Casting it against just two targets at a time is easy, but it leaves so much damage on the table. Sadly, though, it’s the galvanic blastseeker’s only ranged spell. Its only other offensive magic option is thunderwave.

Consequently, the galvanic blastseeker is not an all-purpose faction agent, useful in any combat situation. They’re someone you send in when their particular skill—electrocuting many enemies at once in a straight line—is exactly what’s needed. For instance, to shoot at enemies down a narrow alley, or the neck of a barbican, or some other situation in which the enemy is forced into enfilade.

They also benefit from cooperation with a fluxcharger, in the same way a flux blastseeker benefits from working with a blistercoil weird. (Izzet, get your nomenclature straight, I’m begging you.) The fluxcharger flies, allowing it to drop into the midst of the blastseeker’s enemies, where it will also be hit by their lightning bolt. Thanks to their Amplify Lightning trait, their presence will boost the spell’s damage by an extra 2d8 lightning per target.

The blastseeker can use their Galvanic Overcast trait to boost the damage even further, at some risk to themself. But the odds favor them. They may take the damage themself half the time, but the other half of the time, the damage is dealt to every target that fails its Dex save against lightning bolt. It’s swingy as heck, but in the long run, it will result in more damage dealt to enemies than to the blastseeker—cold comfort to the blastseeker when battles typically last only three to five rounds, so there may not be a long run. But in the Izzet League, by Niv-Mizzet, we respect the sciences, and no one ever said math was safe.

No one wants to stand too close to a galvanic blastseeker, because when they cast their signature spells (lightning bolt is one, thunderwave is the other, but I’ll get to the latter in a moment), they throw off arcs of electricity like a Van de Graaff generator, dealing lightning damage to everyone else within 10 feet—not a lot of damage, but entirely unnecessary. And while their combat role would normally dictate placing them at the back of a formation, no one wants to be standing in front of them, either. So they’re usually way up front, with their fluxcharger partner floating overhead and all their other allies a good 15 feet back. They do their job, then they get out of the way and let everybody else do theirs.

OK, but what about the rest of their kit? Thunderwave is for when they get blitzed by enemies who aren’t forced into single file by having to run down a narrow corridor. Heart of the Storm adds 3 lightning damage to the spell’s effect, and as long as it’s not on cooldown, the blastseeker can amp it up further with Galvanic Overcast. Then, if any of their foes succeed on their saves and stand their ground against thunderwave, they use their Gust-Propelled Leap to fly 10 feet upward to a convenient rooftop, balcony or parapet. (Backward wouldn’t suffice: Their enemies would easily close the distance again on their turn.) If the galvanic blastseeker’s enemies are daft enough to charge them while confined to single file, the blastseeker laughs maniacally and simply unloads another lightning bolt on them.

Stoneskin is useful, but it’s not so useful that the galvanic blastseeker is going to take time to cast it when fighting has already begun. It’s a preparatory measure taken just before the fight begins, and it goes away if and when the blastseeker’s concentration is disrupted. As for levitate, it’s incompatible with stoneskin, the blastseeker doesn’t want it (because it lifts them up out of the plane in which their enemies are lined up for lightning bolt), the fluxcharger doesn’t need it, and they’re not going to waste time chancing it on an enemy who might succeed on their Con save against it. But it is an entertaining way for the galvanic blastseeker to declare that its work is done when it’s spent its three uses of lightning bolt and is getting out of its allies’ path so that they can charge.

A galvanic blastseeker begins to withdraw when they’re moderately wounded (reduced to 37 hp or fewer); when they’re seriously wounded (reduced to 20 hp or fewer), they drop everything and run, using Gust-Propelled Leap for extra speed.

Next: Izzet weirds.

title: Sunder Shaman and Wurm Tactics
content_text:
The sunder shaman is a Gruul-affiliated giant that would more logically be considered an NPC if it weren’t a giant but rather a humanoid. Also, despite its name, it’s not a spellcaster, just a giant berserker with extraordinary Strength and Constitution and merely humanoid-average mental abilities.

It’s got good defenses: Armor Class 20 and proficiency in all the “big three” saving throws. It’s proficient in Athletics as well, for whatever that’s worth; few people would ever try to grapple it. However, I can imagine situations in which a sunder shaman gives up its Multiattack to try to grab somebody, and in that case the escape DC would be 20. Additionally, its Multiattack increases its offensive damage output immediately after it takes damage itself.

While it’s not proficient in Stealth, Stone Camouflage gives it advantage on Dexterity (Stealth) checks in rocky terrain, so it has some ambush potential on its home turf. Most often, though, it’s not going to be encountered on its home turf. The sunder shaman is an offensive weapon, as evidenced by the Siege Monster trait. Yep, it’s another creature that goes after the shelter first and the sheltered afterward. (Which does a lot to explain why a world-spanning city contains so many ruins.)

Beyond that, there’s little to think about. The sunder shaman favors Slam over Rock, not just because its ability contour tends that way but also because it can’t make Rock attacks as part of its Multiattack, only Slam attacks. Slam has a 15-foot reach, but if a sunder shaman can’t get that close using its full movement, only then does it opportunistically throw a rock. Creatures hit by a sunder shaman’s Rock attack are knocked prone, but since it can make that attack only once on its turn, it falls to its allies to exploit the condition; by its next turn, the target will have gotten back up.

Suppose, though, that a group of the Gruul Clans’ enemies are holed up in a fortification of some kind. The sunder shaman is a living catapult, hurling rocks at the walls as it charges forward, dealing double damage with every hit. Rock deals 28 bludgeoning damage on average; Siege Monster makes that 56. A large, resilient object has 27 (5d10) hp, which means that one boulder hurled by a sunder shaman can easily take out a 10-by-10 section of whatever wall its foes are cowering behind (see 2014 Dungeon Master’s Guide chapter 8, “Objects: Huge and Gargantuan Objects,” or 2024 Player’s Handbook appendix C, “Breaking Objects”), making a hole large enough for it to squeeze through.

Unlike the typical berserker, a sunder shaman has just enough Intelligence and Wisdom not to use its Reckless trait whenever it’s making Slam attacks. Initially, it saves its uses of this feature for when it’s attacking an enemy with AC 17 or higher, although once it’s moderately wounded (reduced to 96 hp or fewer), it flies into a rage and begins attacking Recklessly regardless. Perhaps surprisingly, it’s good at picking its targets, taking aim at support spellcasters, sharpshooters and flying creatures. The Gruul have a particular loathing for the Azorius Senate and the Boros Legion, and a sunder shaman will preferentially target Azorius and Boros spellcasters as well as the mounts of those guilds’ air cavalry.

Sunder shamans have enough of a self-preservation instinct to withdraw when they’re seriously wounded (reduced to 55 hp or fewer), and since they’re chaotic neutral and Huge, there’s no one who can tell them not to except Borborygmos himself.

Wurms are monstrous, unaligned chaos agents with animal-level Intelligence which the Gruul Clans pit against their enemies. They can burrow through solid rock, and if they’re within 30 feet of the surface, they create tremors that damage buildings and cause creatures to fall prone. The wording of the Earth Tremors trait is wacky: It refers to “[a]ny structure or object anchored to the ground that comes within 30 feet of the moving wurm,” as if the structure or object were moving rather than the wurm, and the same is true for creatures. Read these as “ … that the wurm moves within 30 feet of.”

Also, it’s unclear whether or not the force damage dealt by Earth Tremors to objects and structures should be doubled by Siege Monster. I would not double it, and here’s why: Siege Monster says, “The wurm deals double damage to objects and structures” (emphasis mine), so one should of course double the damage of an attack hit or any other ability that uses the phrase “deals x damage.” But Earth Tremors simply says that an object or structure within 30 feet of the wurm “takes 10 (3d6) force damage” (emphasis mine, again), without specifying any agent that deals the damage. It’s akin to when the bleeding target of a hit with an infernal weapon “loses hit points,” and analogous to the difference between “restoring” hit points (active) and “gaining” (or “regaining”) hit points (passive).

Wurms appear to lack eyes, but their blindsight and tremorsense extend 60 feet around them. They’re creatures of instinct, and when they sense movement, they zero in and chomp. This behavior is the full extent of their “tactics”: If it moves, eat it. If there’s something solid between wurm and moving thing, destroy it. If there are no more moving things within 60 feet, go back underground.

Despite their lack of sophistication, wurms have perfectly well-developed survival instincts, and since few things are capable of hurting them, it takes only moderate injury (reduction to 140 hp or fewer) to drive them off.

Gangs of Gruul anarchs make use of the tunnels left by burrowing wurms to launch surprise attacks from deep within enemy territory.

Next: Izzet NPCs.

title: Gruul NPC Tactics
content_text:
What do you do if you’re a wild hunter-gatherer type who abhors sedentary living, dense development, abstruse legal codes and advanced technology, hemmed in by a city that’s grown to fill all the space available to it, including the lands where your people once subsisted? You live your life as best you can, when it lets you. And when it doesn’t, you start busting stuff up.

“A loose affiliation of bands that squat on the fringes of Ravnican society,” according to the Guildmasters’ Guide to Ravnica, the Gruul Clans bring subgiants, goblinoids, reptilians, centaurs, minotaurs and antisocial humans together under one ragged banner. Most Gruul are already represented in the good ol’ Monster Manual, but chapter 6 of Ravnica lists three nonplayer character archetypes that aren’t—four if you count the sunder shaman, but the book groups that one among monsters rather than NPCs, so I will, too.

Anarchs are rank-and-file Gruul—or would be if the Gruul ever organized themselves into ranks and files—who form the body of the clans’ busting-stuff-up forces. At first they look like simple melee brutes, charging their enemies with the Aggressive bonus action and whaling away with their spiked clubs, but there are two subtle aspects that set them apart.

The first is their ability contour. Strength is their highest physical ability score, but second is Dexterity, not Constitution. That’s a shock attacker contour, which means that anarchs aren’t rushing in and fighting until they win or die. They’re rushing in, doing as much damage as they can in two rounds, maybe three, and then rushing off again, considering their work done. (They don’t carry shields, so they wield those clubs two-handed for the larger damage die.)

The second is the Siege Monster trait. Gruul’s beef isn’t necessarily with any other particular clan—it’s with civilization itself. They don’t attack others out of brutality. Their way of life has been under assault for as long as they can remember, and they’re lashing out against the wrongs done to them. Thus, they’re just as likely, if not more likely, to attack buildings instead of people. In fact, an anarch encounter will often follow this script: A band of anarchs appears out of nowhere and begins inflicting structural damage on everything within reach. Some other faction—probably Azorius or Boros, maybe Izzet or Orzhov—attempts to intervene, at which point the anarchs turn their attention to them and fight back for a round or two. But as soon as each individual anarch is severely wounded (reduced to 4 hp or fewer) or two rounds of combat with the new foes have passed, whichever comes first, they cut and run.

Anarchs are indiscriminate in their target selection, attacking the nearest foe who’s caused them harm, or at least tried to, and they don’t give any thought to whether the fights they pick are winnable. If they’re the stronger side, it’s an easy victory; if they’re the weaker side, even defeat is glorious. The point is that they fought.

For awesome hilarity, give the smallest anarch on your side gauntlets of ogre power, increasing its attack modifier to +6 and its damage to 9 (1d10 + 4) piercing.

Rubblebelt stalkers are most often encountered in areas of uncleared urban ruins, such as the Red Wastes of District 10, Precinct 4, where they pick through the debris looking for anything of value. They’re ambush attackers and skirmishers, with keen senses, proficiency in Perception and Stealth, and the ability to climb as fast as they can walk. Although the stat block gives their size as Medium, they have the Nimble Escape trait, so they might just as easily be goblins.

Unlike anarchs, rubblebelt stalkers take care not to start any fight they can’t finish. They have the Wisdom to intuit when they’re outmatched, even if they lack the Intelligence to be able to put their finger on why. They’re more reactive than strategic, but they do know from experience that surprise attacks win battles.

The Ambusher trait gives them advantage on attack rolls against any creature that hasn’t taken a turn yet. Under the 2014 fifth edition rules, that includes anyone who’s surprised. (Under 2024 rules, they’re likely to attack before an enemy who’s surprised, but it’s not guaranteed, and they won’t ever get to do it two rounds in a row.) They also have advantage on ability checks to hide in ruins, thanks to their Ruin Dweller trait, and that applies whether their foes are surprised or not. That gives them an effective +8 on Dexterity (Stealth) checks on their home turf, and an opponent needs a passive Perception of 19 or better to spot them lurking without actively searching for them.

They don’t deal a lot of damage with their swords, but with three attacks per Multiattack action, they can make it add up, especially with advantage on the attack rolls. Thus, a band of rubblebelt stalkers can dish out a lot of damage at once, and they aren’t shabby judges of who the weak link in a party might be. Rubblebelt stalker ambush parties don’t cluster up but rather spread out around the easiest (relatively speaking) paths through their broken territory, so that they can use the sweet, sweet advantage of their ambush attacks to overcome front-liners’ heavier armor while hitting frailer foes from behind at the same time.

When a rubblebelt stalker takes a hit, they use Nimble Escape on their next turn to Disengage from melee, scamper back into the ruins and Hide again once they’re out of line of sight. (Whether they use their action to Disengage and their bonus action to Hide or vice versa doesn’t matter.) They get advantage on this Hide action, but unfortunately they don’t get it on their subsequent attack rolls: Ambusher applies only in round 1. However, if they remain hidden and an enemy is foolish enough to come looking for them, they can exploit being unseen to deliver one attack with advantage on the roll when the enemy comes within reach. Under 2014 rules, I allow hidden creatures (PC or not) to cross open terrain unnoticed if they succeed on another Stealth check, but I wouldn’t give the rubblebelt stalker advantage on this one, since it doesn’t count as “hid[ing] in ruins”—it’s trying to remain unseen and unheard while emerging from ruins.

In any event, as long as a rubblebelt stalker isn’t severely wounded (reduced to 4 hp or fewer), they keep attacking, retreating and hiding, attacking, retreating and hiding, until their enemies are defeated. Only once they’ve taken that much damage do they withdraw from combat completely.

Note that Ruin Dweller also lets rubblebelt stalkers move at full speed over “difficult terrain composed of rubble.” This trait gives them a substantial situational advantage in such terrain which they can and should exploit to the fullest. The same goes for verticality, since they have a 30-foot climb speed: Give them multistory ruins to clamber around in as well as ground covered in layers of unstable bricks and stones. Let them not just run away from foes but also climb away.

Also, like anarchs, rubblebelt stalkers have the Siege Monster trait. Ruins being full of crumbling structures and whatnot, this trait presents a great opportunity for rubblebelt stalkers to turn environmental hazards against their foes. Per the 2014 Dungeon Master’s Guide, chapter 8, “Improvising Damage,” 4d10 (on average, 22) is an appropriate amount of bludgeoning damage for falling rubble to deal. In lieu of its melee Multiattack, a rubblebelt stalker might instead strike or topple a weakened part of a ruined structure, causing it to fall on an opponent below. Reducing the rubble damage to 3d10 bludgeoning (or 2d10 bludgeoning while dealing half damage on a successful DC 12 Dex save) keeps it on par with the rubblebelt stalker’s normal damage per round … not that you have to pull your punches that way if you don’t want to. (Alternatively, you could have the falling masonry deal 1d10 bludgeoning damage on a failed save to each PC in a 5-foot-radius circle beneath the crumbling portion of the structure.)

Anarchs are suitable for any encounter with Gruul, while PCs of any level who venture into the rubblebelts may encounter rubblebelt stalkers. These foes are fine for tier 1 PCs. However, a druid of the Old Ways is way more powerful, a threat to the entire district whom PCs shouldn’t encounter until they’re at least level 5. When the Gruul send one of these characters out, they’re not messing around. When they send out more than one, they’re not chipping away around the edges of civilization anymore: They’re declaring all-out war.

Druids of the Old Ways have extraordinary Wisdom and very high Constitution, along with high Dex and Charisma. They’re spellcasters who are nevertheless sturdy enough to take a few hits, so they don’t need to hang back far from the front line. They, too, deal double damage to objects and structures—I’ll deal with some of the nastier implications of this trait in a moment—and they wield pointless quarterstaves. Seriously, these weapons are really pointless: Druids of the Old Ways don’t even have shillelagh. That cantrip must be too newfangled an innovation for them.

The druid of the Old Way’s spell repertoire is … incoherent, to put it kindly. As a spell list for high-fantasy druids, it’s great. As a spell list for druids who live in bombed-out urban precincts, it’s less apropos.

Transport via plants requires mature trees at both the origin and the destination. When a tree grows in Brooklyn, it’s newsworthy enough to name a book after it. When a tree grows in Ravnica, the Selesnya Conclave probably has something to do with it, and it happens far away from the rubblebelts. That being said, if a druid can get a mature tree to grow in a Gruul-controlled neighborhood, the spell provides an interesting way to launch a special ops assault against other factions through Selesnya territory. But the druid of the Old Ways must have paid that Selesnya-grown tree a visit, and while the Selesnya are sympathetic to the Gruul’s desire to get back to nature, they tend to find the Gruul themselves off-putting. In fact, if I were a Selesnya arborist, I’d put a ring of wrought-iron fence around each mature tree to make sure that Gruul druids couldn’t use my precious urban forest for such a distasteful purpose.

Wall of thorns is always a solid combination of damage dealing and area control, its only drawback being that it requires concentration. If I were converting this spell list to 5E24 uses-per-day format, I’d make wall of thorns a 1/day spell and dispense with transport via plants so that the poor Selesnya wouldn’t have to build fences around all their trees.

Commune with nature is for social encounters, not combat encounters.

Conjure elemental is a banger under 5E14 rules but a damp squib in 5E24. A conjured earth elemental can go around smashing things and provide a splendid distraction from everything else the Gruul are doing. A conjured “Large, intangible spirit that appears in an unoccupied space within range” and never leaves that space, doing nothing but immobilizing and somehow hurting (in an intangible way) anyone dumb enough to go near it, is boring. It’s not Gruul. Gruul smash!

Scrying is mainly for social encounters, not combat encounters, although it does provide a legit excuse for how a druid of the Old Ways might know what the PCs are up to.

Dominate beast relies on the opponents’ having exactly one beast worth dominating. Verrrry situational. The most common version of this situation is when one PC is a Beast Master ranger with a companion animal, and turning a ranger’s companion against the ranger is a mean thing to do. A Gruul druid would totally do it, but as a Dungeon Master, you shouldn’t.

Freedom of movement is more of an ideological aspiration than a useful spell. It does allow a druid of the Old Ways to traverse rubble without being slowed down, since they don’t have the Ruin Dweller trait. But rather than put it on the spell list, it makes more sense to me either to simply give the druid that trait or to have them pre-cast freedom of movement before every encounter.

Wall of fire is even more useful to a druid of the Old Ways than wall of thorns … if you take a liberty or two with it. RAW, wall of fire damages creatures but has no effect on objects or structures. It makes total sense, though, that a wall of fire should burn flammable things as well as living beings—other spells that deal fire damage do, including meteor swarm, fire storm, fireball, scorching ray, flame blade and fire bolt, and so do fire elementals—and the Siege Monster trait doubles damage to objects and structures. More to the point, if it doesn’t deal fire damage to objects and structures, wall of fire is largely redundant to wall of thorns. Follow your own code.

Conjure animals, under 5E14 rules, is less interesting than conjure elemental and often far more cumbersome, while under 5E24 rules, conjuring spells are never interesting at all. But you can give this spell a specifically Gruul flavor—and keep it non-cumbersome—by having a druid of the Old Ways conjure a single batterboar (giant boar) or maaka (saber-toothed tiger), both of which are CR 2.

Dispel magic is sometimes handy, though not particularly Gruul. A member of the Gruul Clans is more likely to dispel the magician. By force.

Plant growth is a trap option. It slows all ground creatures’ movement by a factor of 4, including rubblebelt stalkers who might otherwise be able to benefit from Ruin Dweller. A druid of the Old Ways with freedom of movement in effect can disregard the movement penalty, but none of their allies can. I guess that makes it an appropriately chaotic neutral thing to do, but I also think the druid’s Wisdom score argues against it.

Beast sense is interesting from a flavor perspective but has no combat application that I can see, other than letting a druid of the Old Ways watch a battle without participating in it.

Flame blade is superior to the druid’s Quarterstaff attack and really ought to replace it, except that it does require concentration to keep active.

Pass without trace is for exploration encounters, not combat encounters, except for the fact that it can guarantee an allied group of rubblebelt stalkers a surprise first strike.

Cure wounds … why? The druid of the Old Ways isn’t that kind of druid.

Faerie fire is useful and effective but not at all thematic.

Thunderwave, on the other hand, is very thematic, and given what the druid will and won’t use its higher-level spell slots for, I think it would be fun to give it a massive boost—to 3rd, 4th or even 5th level, dealing 4d8, 5d8 or 6d8 thunder damage. (If you’re converting the druid’s spell list to uses-per-day, make it 2/day at 3rd level or 1/day at 5th level.) Also, since the spell pushes unsecured objects 10 feet away, I would treat a single 10-foot-by-10-foot section of weakened wall as an “unsecured object” and require anyone behind it to make a DC 15 Dex save against flying rubble (4d10 bludgeoning damage, half damage on a successful save, and not reduced this time!). RAW, thunderwave deals thunder damage only to creatures, but I think it would be very Gruul to apply this damage to objects and structures as well. That’s up to you, though.

Druidcraft makes little wildflowers grow up through the rubble, engendering sympathy for the druid of the Old Ways before they tell their ogre buddies to beat you to a pulp.

Produce flame (my first-printing copy of Ravnica erroneously says “produce fire”) does allow the druid of the Old Ways to target objects as well as creatures under 5E24 rules, and because the druid is a 12th-level caster, it deals 3d8 fire damage to targeted creatures and 6d8 fire damage to targeted objects and structures. Yes! Under 5E14 rules, only creatures are targetable, and only within 30 feet, as opposed to the 60-foot range under 5E24. Boo.

Resistance … no. Not at the cost of an entire action plus concentration. Thorn whip isn’t particularly Gruul, but at least it deals 3d6 damage and can be used to pull an enemy into reach of a Gruul-style clobbering.

In short, here’s how I’d structure the druid of the Old Ways’ spell list in uses-per-day form:

At will: produce flame, thorn whip2/day: dispel magic, flame blade, freedom of movement, pass without trace (optional: conjure animals, 5E14 version)1/day: conjure elemental (5E14 version), thunderwave (as 5th-level spell), wall of thorns (optional: wall of fire)

Wall of thorns is the druid’s first choice when controlling movement on the battlefield is more important than demolition, whereas conjure elemental is the first choice when the druid simply wants to wreak havoc. If and when its concentration on either of these spells is broken, it switches from wall of thorns to wall of fire (if you’ve chosen to give them that spell) or from conjure elemental to conjure animals (again, if you’ve chosen to give them that spell), or to flame blade from either one. Assume that it has freedom of movement already cast at the start of the battle, with one chance to cast it again if it’s dispelled by the enemy. Dispel magic is for use primarily against abjuration magic—anything that might protect someone from harm or shield a building from destruction—but common sense also indicates using it to negate sustained buff and debuff spells of 3rd level or lower such as haste, slow, enlarge/reduce, invisibility, hypnotic pattern and spiritual weapon.

For extra fun, you might give a druid of the Old Ways a Gruul keyrune, which on command can be transformed into a ceratok (use the rhinoceros stat block) for 1 hour. The druid can use the ceratok as an independent mount, allowing it to charge and gore enemies while carrying the druid to places on the battlefield they might not be able to reach easily otherwise. Alternatively, a horn of blasting is great for a druid facing off against a massed opposing force.

The druid of the Old Ways is a fanatic, unwilling to withdraw even when they’ve taken severe damage. However, they’re careful about which battles they pick in the first place, attacking only when they’re confident of victory. Thus, a combat encounter involving one or more druids of the Old Ways is always Deadly (5E14, per DMG chapter 3, “Creating a Combat Encounter”) or High Difficulty (5E24, DMG chapter 4, “Combat Encounters”). Druids may be backed up by any combination of CR 5 or lower creatures listed in the Gruul Creatures table in Ravnica chapter 6.

Next: sunder shamans.

title: Undercity Medusa and Devkarin Lich Tactics
content_text:
Undercity medusas occupy a peculiar place in the Golgari Swarm. Although they live beneath the city, they don’t seem to share the Swarm’s ideology. Their alliance with the Golgari is a matter of convenience, a buffer that lets them live unmolested by the other guilds. The Golgari, in turn, benefit from their power to petrify opponents. Given the chance, though, the medusas would happily use the other denizens of the undercity for their own ends alone.

Nimbler and stealthier than ordinary medusas and much stronger and smarter, undercity medusas are dangerous ambush attackers. They can cast fog cloud once per day and often use it to conceal themselves, but the moment they’re ready to strike, they have to drop it in order to make use of their Surprise Attack trait, which grants them advantage on attack rolls against surprised foes. Why? Because as long as everything is heavily obscured by the fog, everyone in it is effectively both blinded and invisible, and the combination of advantage and disadvantage nullifies the undercity medusa’s own attack roll advantage. Truth be told, the fog is likely to be a tip-off to suspicious player characters; much of the time, an undercity medusa is better off merely using its Stealth proficiency to hide in the shadows and save fog cloud for emergency disengagement.

As for its other spells, expeditious retreat and misty step, their usefulness depends on how much space an undercity medusa has to work with. Misty step’s usefulness is limited in tight underground tunnels where there’s little maneuverability and tightly constrained line of sight, while expeditious retreat is handy for running away but can only be used to run rings around one’s opponents when there’s room to run them—in an abandoned undercity mansion, for instance. It doesn’t make sense, by and large, for the Golgari to wage offensive campaigns against rival guilds: They have every advantage on their home turf and little or no advantage anyplace else, so their conflicts are overwhelmingly defensive in nature. However, that being said, if any Golgari are going to take the battle to the enemy, they’re going to be a rogue faction within the guild, or even just a handful of renegades—and an undercity medusa is a good candidate for the renegade role.

Undercity medusas’ cunning makes them good judges of others’ weaknesses, but their Wisdom, though above humanoid average, is just low enough that they can sometimes be careless about choosing their battles. Thus, they will often pick a fight and inflict a lot of pain in the first round or two but then find the tide of battle swinging against them. That’s where expeditious retreat and misty step come in handy, allowing them to get away when they realize they might otherwise be hosed. Failing that, they can make use of their Deception proficiency to attempt to snooker their way out of trouble.

While they have high ability scores across the board, the nearest thing they have to a weakness is their Wisdom. Their Petrifying Gaze is strongest against a target with a low Constitution save modifier. Thus, they make it a priority to petrify bards, wizards and more fragile warlocks. They’d like to take out sorcerers, too, but since sorcerers have Con save proficiency, the better tool for that job is their Multiattack (which also happens to be excellent against wizards). They’re also strategically minded, however, and they know that the best time to take out a foe with more hit points and a higher Armor Class is that first round of combat, since Surprise Attack grants advantage on their attack rolls and an extra 3d6 damage on every hit. That’s the moment to go after a front-liner: Two good hits deal an average of 46 damage total, which probably isn’t enough to bring a tough warrior down but is definitely enough to strike fear into them. And about one time in 16, an undercity medusa does deal enough damage to bring that warrior down.

Thus, the medusa’s first-round attack pattern is to ambush a front-liner with two Claw attacks, use Petrifying Gaze to give a bard or wizard a calcium supplement and finally use its bonus action to misty step well away from the front-liner and into melee reach of someone less protected—a back-line marksman, perhaps. After that initial assault, the undercity medusa instead aims its Claw attacks at targets with ACs that are easier to overcome while continuing to use its Petrifying Gaze against the targets it favors for that.

When an undercity medusa is seriously wounded (reduced to 48 hp or fewer), three combat rounds have elapsed, or the medusa is simply disappointed by the amount of damage it’s dealing, it cuts and runs, using misty step to disengage from melee if necessary before Dashing away.

Undercity medusas don’t mesh well with most other Golgari assets, but a medusa may occasionally be accompanied by a Devkarin warrior (drow elite warrior) or two. The medusa’s attack pattern remains the same, but any Devkarin warrior present maintains engagement with the enemy’s front line to monopolize its attention while the medusa wrecks the squishies.

A Devkarin lich is a close but slightly less powerful relative of the standard lich, differing primarily in its spell list (notably, it lacks counterspell and dimension door but can cast create undead) and its melee spell attack, Noxious Touch, which merely inflicts the poisoned condition (as opposed to the standard lich’s Paralyzing Touch, which, of course, paralyzes). In lieu of Rejuvenation, it has Regeneration (which works identically to a troll’s) and Undead Fortitude (which works identically to a zombie’s). It has no Frightening Gaze legendary action, but its other LAs work the same way. It has no lair actions.

Its opening play depends on whether it’s fighting many enemies on wide open ground or a handful of enemies in tighter quarters. Against a hostile horde out in the open, circle of death affects a ridiculous number of enemies at once, letting the Devkarin lich rack up damage numbers like a pinball machine with bonuses lit. But in smaller spaces, the Devkarin lich can’t cast this spell without hurting itself as well, so its best opening play is the same as that of a regular lich: cloudkill. The reason is simple: Being immune to poison, it can cast the spell at ground zero and let it gradually wash over its foes from there, maximizing the damage. This spell is slightly better than insect swarm, which stays in one place but to which the Devkarin lich isn’t immune. The latter spell is preferred if and only if the Devkarin lich’s enemies refuse to come close enough for the lich to share the area of effect with them.

While the Devkarin lich is concentrating on either cloudkill or insect swarm, all its other concentration-required spells are unavailable to it. As it works its way down through its damaging spells, finger of death is next, followed by blight. Black tentacles deals slightly more damage on average than blight­—and has the virtue of targeting Dexterity instead of Constitution—but it requires concentration; the Devkarin lich casts it only if it’s been forced to drop cloudkill or insect swarm. Even then, with two 5th-level spell slots, it gets to try one of those spells again before having to resort to black tentacles.

Meanwhile, it’s been using legendary actions all the while. As long as there are three or more foes within 30 feet of the Devkarin lich, it’s worth burning all three legendary actions to use Disrupt Life. Otherwise, it uses its legendary actions to lob cantrips—poison spray is the natural default, but acid splash is good against two targets standing side by side, while chill touch deals slightly less damage but has the benefit of suppressing healing. If the Devkarin lich sees an enemy cleric rubbing their hands together and getting them warmed up, it may be worth it to pop three different opponents with chill touch just to keep them all from receiving any healing—assuming you’re using the 2014 rules, since chill touch is, in fact, a touch-range spell under the 2024 rules. (Note that since the Devkarin lich is a 14th-level spellcaster, its cantrips all deal three dice of damage, not just one.) Spending two legendary actions on Noxious Touch is never worth it: It calls for a Con save, but most of the time, the only targets within reach will be tough front-liners.

Getting back to the lich’s own turn, after blight, we’re starting to scrape the bottom of the barrel, because its best spells all compete for its concentration. Unless the Devkarin lich is having trouble landing hits with attacks against its opponents’ ACs, chromatic orb is preferable to magic missile, especially in 5E24, which gives it a chance to bounce to a second target. At that point, it’s down to cantrips, which at the Devkarin lich’s level are still better than acid arrow. Animate dead and create undead aren’t getting used in combat. There’s also the Noxious Touch attack, but honestly? 5E24 chill touch is probably better.

Bestow curse and web are superb spells, and vampiric touch isn’t half-bad either, but it’s hard to justify casting any of these spells when the alternative is the enormous ongoing damage of cloudkill or insect swarm. It may make sense to cast one of them instead of black tentacles, though, if it happens to solve a very specific problem that the Devkarin lich is facing. For instance, a particularly troublesome rogue is an excellent target for bestow curse, because as long as they have disadvantage on all their attack rolls, they will never get to use Sneak Attack. Or, if there’s a front-liner who’s proving doggedly resistant to all the lich’s spells that target Con, it can use bestow curse to target a poor Wisdom score and exploit that weakness to impose disadvantage on Con saves. The difficulty here is twofold, though: First, the target has to get within the lich’s reach, and second, it could be using its action to deal damage instead. Like the standard lich, the Devkarin lich can only do so much so fast. Time is not its friend.

However, there are two things that are its friends: Its extraordinary Intelligence, which lets it instantly spot every enemy’s every weakness, and … its actual friends. It may not be casting create undead during combat, but you can bet it’s been casting that spell in its free time, so you can give that Devkarin lich an entourage comprising a dozen or so ghouls, or a handful of ogre zombies or Erstwhile wights. These undead minions, also conveniently immune to poison, block for the Devkarin lich while it hangs back, casting spells.

A Devkarin lich has invested too much in its own immortality to let it be taken away by a stupid sword blade. It retreats when it’s seriously wounded (reduced to 38 hp or fewer), allowing any allies it has to stay behind and delay pursuers. It doesn’t care whether they’re destroyed; it can always make new friends.

Next: Gruul NPCs.

title: Coming Oct. 6: Remonstered!
content_text:
Times have changed. The old rules no longer apply. Our foes have grown in strength. We must adapt.

Don’t worry. I gotchu.

In Remonstered! The Monsters Know What They’re Doing, every monster’s tactics are updated to reflect the recent changes to their stat blocks in the 2025 Monster Manual and accompanied by brand-new tips to help you prepare the battlefield, choose allies and minions, and take your combat scenes in exciting, unexpected directions. (Not to mention another banger cover by Lio Pressland and all-new interior illustrations by Jen C. Marshall!) This revised edition hits shelves Oct. 6, but you can preorder it now from Bookshop, Barnes & Noble, Amazon, Indigo or your favorite independent bookstore in the United States or Canada.

And if you’ve decided to stick with the original 2014 fifth edition Dungeons & Dragons rules, fret not: The original The Monsters Know What They’re Doing will still be available alongside the revised edition.

title: Golgari NPC Tactics
content_text:
From the legalistic Azorius Senate, the zealous Boros Legion and the scheming House Dimir to … our first bunch of weird little guys, the Golgari Swarm. Azorius, Boros and Dimir go about easily understood human goals in easily understood human ways. The Golgari, on the other hand, share a mentality from another phylum of life altogether, if not another kingdom. When they say “I hope you rot,” they mean that in the best possible way.

The Golgari rank and file comprises drow, fungus zombies, vegepygmies and kraul warriors. Kraul are a eusocial insectoid folk with an instinct for teamwork; their warriors have hard exoskeletons and wield spears. With high Strength and above-average Constitution and Dexterity, they have a slight bias toward toe-to-toe melee engagement but aren’t averse to the occasional hit-and-run maneuver. Some possess wings and act as “scouts and shock troops,” but a unit including scouts comprises only scouts; a unit of shock troops comprises only shock troops.

Kraul have 60 feet of darkvision and thus prefer to engage at night or underground, but there’s nothing stopping them from engaging in daylight beyond this preference and the fact that they have no particular skill at stealth. When they attack, it’s a direct, open assault. They do, however, have the Spider Climb trait, so if clinging to the ceiling confers any kind of advantage upon them, that’s what they’ll do. But it’s probably of greater concern that high walls and towers constructed to keep others out are no obstacle to them: They can walk right up the side.

They move as one, never straying more than 30 feet from their nearest ally because of their Hive Mind trait, which immunizes them against being charmed or frightened as long as they remain close together. They also have Pack Tactics, so at least half of any group of kraul warriors always moves quickly to engage in melee with the enemy, if not all of them. Since they don’t carry shields, they wield their spears two-handed and deal the greater amount of damage. But also, since they have six limbs, they can carry two more spears in their hands at the same time as long as they don’t have a wall to scale (which I assume requires at least four limbs), and a kraul ground assault may involve hurling two volleys of spears from 50 and 20 feet away before the melee free-for-all commences.

Because of their Hive Mind, an individual kraul warrior never retreats, regardless of how much damage they’ve taken, but a unit of kraul warriors retreats when reduced to 40 percent of its original number, Disengaging if necessary before Dashing away.

Kraul warrior scouts, especially those with wings, keep at least 60 feet between themselves and the targets of their observation. Usually, they withdraw as fast as they can when they’re spotted, holding onto their spears in case their pursuers catch up and they need to fight. But they may sometimes engage in aggressive reconnaissance to get an idea of what their foes are capable of and how they behave in battle. This kind of operation consists of approaching to a distance of 60 feet, hurling a volley of spears, then hightailing it back to base. A shock attack is much the same, except half of the unit closes all the way and engages in melee, so that—thanks to Pack Tactics—their buddies 60 feet back don’t have disadvantage on their attack rolls when they hurl their spears. After two rounds of hostilities, the rear group Dashes away as fast as it can, while the front group stays behind to delay pursuit, fighting to the death if it comes to that.

A kraul death priest occupies a lofty role in kraul society. On the battlefield, they’re more than a mere captain—something on par with a major, perhaps, commanding a battalion of several hundred kraul warriors. (Yes, that’s right, I said “several hundred.” If you don’t have the brass to throw that many enemies at your player characters at once, don’t pit them against a faction called the Golgari Swarm.) However, commanding troops isn’t their primary role. They’re priest-kings first and foremost, and in that role, they’re more likely to be encountered along with a smaller security detail—say, between half a dozen and two dozen kraul warriors, plus up to four fungus drudges created with animate dead.

Even when commanding a battalion, however, a kraul death priest leads from the front—or, at least, no more than 15 feet behind the front. Their primary defensive ability is their high Con, not their merely above-humanoid-average Dex, and their Feed on Death trait requires them to be within 30 feet of where the dying is happening. The more of their own allies they’re within this distance of, the better, as they can use casualties from both sides to bolster them. They share the Hive Mind, Pack Tactics and Spider Climb traits with the kraul warrior as well as their darkvision, but unlike kraul warriors, they can communicate in Common, not just in the clicks and buzzes of kraul speech. This linguistic ability, combined with their high Wisdom, makes parley feasible, though not by any stretch of the imagination pleasant.

Despite their clerical role, the kraul death priest’s primary offensive ability is Strength; their Wisdom is secondary. Fortunately, their shrewdly designed Multiattack allows them to both make a melee attack and cast a spell (other than animate dead, which takes a full minute to cast), so that they’re never forced to choose between the two. Like the kraul warrior, the kraul death priest carries no shield, so their Quarterstaff attack always deals two-handed damage—and their extra limbs mean they don’t have to let go of the staff to cast their spells, which is cool to describe.

Sadly, there’s no spell in their repertoire that would give them advantage on their Quarterstaff attack. On the contrary, at least two three of their spells are ranged spell attacks (ray of sickness and poison spray, plus ray of enfeeblement and chill touch if you’re using 2014 rules and poison spray if you’re using 2024 rules), which means they have disadvantage on these rolls if they’re within 5 feet of an enemy who’s alive and kicking. Thus, they’re not going to be casting these spells as long as they’re engaged in melee.

The kraul death priest isn’t especially good at assessing their enemies’ weaknesses, but they choose their targets mindfully nonetheless, and one of their top priorities is casting blight against a key back-line enemy, particularly a squishy spellcaster—but not if every such enemy is more than 30 feet away, which would be beyond the range of the spell, and not against a nearer enemy who doesn’t look at all squishy, which would be a waste. The Golgari waste nothing. Vampiric touch is a melee spell attack, but the kraul death priest has the prudence and patience to wait to cast that one until they’re reduced to 47 hp or fewer, because they don’t want to lose a single one of the hit points the spell might restore. As long as they remain under that threshold, they keep the spell going, but since that means forgoing their Quarterstaff attack (since maintaining the spell as an action means they can’t take the Multiattack action), once they’re sufficiently topped up, they’re better off dropping the spell.

Beyond these two, no other spell in the kraul death priest’s repertoire suggests itself as an obvious default. Under 5E14 rules, all of them but poison spray are ranged spell attacks, which means the KDP must make all those attack rolls with disadvantage if they’re in the thick of things. Given that, their best bet is to cast poison spray against their melee opponent: The damage isn’t great, but they can cast the cantrip at will, so if it misses because their foe is up in their face succeeds on their Con save, at least they haven’t blown one of their limited daily uses.

Under 5E24 rules, ray of enfeeblement is not a ranged attack but rather requires a Con save. Unfortunately, even the revised spell suffers from the same Catch-22 as the original spell: Its primary utility is against front-line warriors who’ll have high Con and therefore an easier time resisting the spell. However, it does at least nerf all weapon damage, not just damage from Strength-based weapons, so the kraul death priest can use it with a clear conscience against a skirmisher or marksman wielding a finesse or ranged weapon. It also nerfs Strength-based ability checks and saving throws, making it useful against a grappled and restrained shock attacker who gets caught in a Golgari shaman’s entangle spell. It does require concentration, though, and as long as the kraul death priest is keeping it going, the only other sensible option for their Multiattack spell while they’re in melee is chill touch (which is a melee attack, not a ranged attack, in 5E24).

Ray of sickness is a groovy enough spell, but since it’s a ranged spell attack regardless of which 5E rules you’re using, it makes sense for the kraul death priest only when they have no active opponent within 5 feet.

The kraul death priest is both eusocial and a fanatic, so they never retreat from combat, no matter how much damage they take. If they die, so be it: Their body will feed another generation of life. However, if they’re seriously wounded (reduced to 26 hp or fewer), any kraul warriors accompanying them will gang up on any foe engaged in melee with them, then close ranks around their priest once that foe falls and fight to the death themselves to keep them from coming to any further harm.

Kraul death priests don’t lead their followers into doomed battles. The presence of a KDP in a fight of the Golgari’s choosing invariably means it’s a Deadly (according to the 2014 Dungeon Master’s Guide, chapter 3) or High Difficulty (according to the 2024 DMG, chapter 4) encounter. If such a battle should nevertheless start to turn against the Golgari—never before the Golgari have lost less than 10 percent of their forces, but usually before they’ve lost more than half—a kraul death priest will signal a willingness to parley, though in oblique terms such as, “The new life has been well fed this day! We need not feed it further.”

However, depending on what faction they’re parleying with, coming to a mutually satisfactory arrangement may be difficult. Under no circumstances are the Golgari willing to let Azorius extend its laws into the Undercity or Boros fight its battles there. They detest Orzhov’s brand of necromancy, seeing it as a desecration of death, and won’t permit it where they hold sway. And even with guilds they’re more ideologically simpatico with, they just can’t help but be weird. They may insist on their right to extend a fungal mycelium beneath another guild’s overground territory or demand a supply of fresh corpses as a concession for some other point of contention, and their communitarian mentality may make requests for individual favors or exceptions impossible for them to comprehend.

Golgari shamans are not kraul but elves—most likely Devkarin, according to the lore—so you may want to give them a drowish 120 feet of darkvision rather than the 60-foot range given in the stat block. They’re long-range spellslingers, with very high Wisdom and Charisma and high Dexterity. Although they carry staves, their Fungal Rot melee attack is superior to their Quarterstaff attack in every way, even if they cast shillelagh. But if they ever find themselves in melee, something has gone drastically wrong, and they’ve probably stopped fighting to parley long before that point, on the same terms and in more or less the same manner as a kraul death priest.

A significant different between Golgari shamans and kraul death priests is that the shaman’s Feed on Death feature is a reaction, usable only once between turns and obviating the use of their reaction for other purposes, whereas the KDP’s can happen over and over again as many times as necessary. Also, the KDP can bolster any of their allies, but the shaman’s Feed on Death reaction bolsters only the shaman. Thus, it’s not necessary for a Golgari shaman to be positioned any further forward than the ranges of its spells dictate. Kraul death priests lead from the front; Golgari shamans hang back.

Their spell repertoire is deeper than a kraul death priest’s, and they cast their spells using spell slots. (If you’re using 5E24 rules and prefer the uses-per-day schema of the 2025 Monster Manual, then change the Golgari shaman’s spellcasting repertoire as follows: At will: cure wounds, dispel magic, entangle, thorn whip; 2/day: animate dead, pass without trace, plant growth, spike growth; 1/day: blight, giant insect; bye-bye: ray of enfeeblement, shillelagh. The 5E24 Way is to change ray of sickness and poison spray into ranged attacks, but you can also leave them as at-will spells if you prefer. See also Lionheart261’s comment below regarding cure wounds.) Of these spells, giant insect, pass without trace, ray of enfeeblement, spike growth and entangle all require concentration, so casting one obviates the rest.

But there is one combination of spells that’s slow but potentially devastating, especially when a Golgari shaman is backed up by winged kraul warriors. That’s to cast plant growth in round 1 and spike growth in round 2, resulting in an enormous area of ground that costs four times as much movement to traverse and automatically deals 2d4 piercing damage for every 5 feet of it an opponent marches through. Under 5E14 rules, the area of effect of plant growth is a circle 200 feet in diameter; under 5E24 rules, the AoE is spherical, and the point can be centered anywhere within range, including up in the air, allowing a circle of growth of any smaller size desired, and I’d say just do that if you want to even if you’re using 5E14 rules. The diameter of spike growth, however, maxes out at 40 feet, so if you want perfect overlap, just make both of them 40-foot circles (that is, circles with a 20-foot radius). Kraul warrior ground troops can then fan out at a distance of 5 feet around the perimeter of the overgrowth area, ready to perforate anyone who steps out of it. Why 5 feet away? To get that last 2d4 of piercing damage as opponents move out of it. Golgari waste nothing.

Once a Golgari shaman has cast plant growth and spike growth, a delightful option is to advance to between 10 and 20 feet from the edge of the circle, then cast thorn whip at a target within range inside. The whip itself deals 2d6 piercing damage (the Golgari shaman is a level 8 caster), and in addition, the target must take another 4d4 piercing damage as they’re dragged across 10 feet of spikes. Just like the kraul death priest, the Golgari shaman would love to be able to cast blight on a spellslinger who’s likely to lob Dex-save evocation spells, but since its range is only 30 feet, it’s not likely to be able to reach; this spell is therefore better used against rogues, monks and other shock attackers who’ve managed to escape the Circle of Giant Thornbushes and are making their way around the perimeter. If spike growth is somehow dispelled (or counterspelled) twice, the Golgari shaman can instead cast giant insect and summon five giant wasps (or, in 5E24, one humongous wasp) to torment foes stuck inside the circle. Ray of enfeeblement has the same drawbacks and the same limited situational applications described above, while entangle is always a great way to pin down a melee opponent (either of oneself or of an ally) but far down the list given all the other things the shaman could be concentrating on instead.

If they have nothing better to do, the Golgari shaman may cast dispel magic to get rid of a buff of 3rd level or lower on one of their enemies—haste is the top priority here—or cure wounds on a wounded fellow leader of at least CR 2, or fire an opportunistic ray of sickness, since they generally don’t need to worry about being embroiled in melee. If perchance they do end up in melee, they fight back with their Fungal Rot attack.

A fanatic to the core, the Golgari shaman never retreats, no matter how much damage they take. Unfortunately for them, they’re not part of the kraul hive mind, so kraul warriors won’t close ranks around them the way they do around a wounded death priest. Ah, well. The next generation of life will be fed.

Next: Undercity medusas and Devkarin liches.

title: Nightveil Specter and Gloamwing Tactics
content_text:
House Dimir values discretion and usually goes about its business with great care … but accidents happen. A clandestine mission encounters chance interference, an undercover agent lets the wrong word slip, or an interrogator applies a little too much pressure and strips away their subject’s sense of self. Rather than release an amnesiac husk back onto the streets of Ravnica, though, House Dimir repurposes the hapless captive as a Nightveil specter, a tool of terror protecting the guild’s secrets from snoops. Waste not, want not.

With extraordinary Dexterity and exceptional Strength, along with proficiency in Perception and Stealth, the Nightveil specter waits silently in the shadows, then strikes hard and fast. Its Intelligence marks it as dim-witted compared with its former self—we’re talking ape-level cognition—but its very high Wisdom makes up for it, powering not just its senses but its intuition. It can’t speak, but it understands Common, allowing it to eavesdrop and glean others’ intentions. Ordinary weapons are weak against it, and it can’t be charmed or frightened. It has 120 feet of darkvision, indicating that it’s active almost exclusively at night and in dark places far from the light of the sun: dungeons, vaults, and other secret dens.

Its attack pattern isn’t complicated. Much like a beholder, a Nightveil specter lying in ambush positions itself where it has a security-camera view of the approach to the place it’s guarding; if it’s pursuing an interloper who’s learned too much, it often overtakes them, then wheels around to cut off their path of escape. It manages these feats by bestriding a flying mount called a gloamwing—more on that in a moment. For now, suffice it to say that gloamwings are quiet and fast and grant Nightveil specters an elevation advantage over their opponents, and a gloamwing is almost never encountered without its rider.

Whether a Nightveil specter takes a lethal or nonlethal approach to combat depends on what its quarry has discovered—and when. Suppose that someone has seen something they weren’t supposed to see or heard something they weren’t supposed to hear. Chances are, House Dimir knows when and where it happened: Even if they couldn’t deter the incursion, they had ways, or at least one way, of detecting it. (Detection, deterrence and response are the three pillars of building security, which I discuss at greater length in How to Defend Your Lair.) If it happened within the last 24 hours, the guild can send out a Nightveil specter and have it wipe the snoop’s memory of the appropriate hour. Similarly, if the snoop is merely on the verge of discovering something they shouldn’t, or of trespassing someplace off limits, the specter can erase their recollection of how they got there, then drop them off somewhere else. However, if the leak is more than 24 hours old, the snoop is marked for death.

Regardless of the scenario, the Nightveil specter’s opening play is the same: Mind Twist, a 5–6 recharge ability, targets everyone in a 60-foot cone with a chance of dealing psychic damage and imposing the stunned condition. This area of effect is generously large, and with the gloamwing’s help, the specter can easily position itself where it can affect every target it needs to. (Conic sections being what they are, it can even soar overhead and turn a 60-foot cone into a 30-foot-radius circle, although it won’t often need to do that.) Once a target is stupefied by Mind Twist, the Nightveil specter can use Reap Memory upon them, choosing an hour to erase from their recollection. It can use this ability up to three times in one day, which, if it’s targeting just a couple of hapless spies or trespassers, is plenty.

It gets more complicated, though, if the Nightveil specter has to deal with a group of four or more would-be trespassers in House Dimir territory—first, because of the three-use-per-day limit on Reap Memory, and second, because in a group that size, at least one target is bound to succeed on their saving throw against Mind Twist, putting the Nightveil specter in a bind: Blank the memory of the one(s) knocked out, or deal with the ones who aren’t affected?

A major liability of Mind Twist is that a target who succeeds on their save against it not only isn’t stunned but also takes no damage at all, leaving them fully capable of continuing to interfere. But also, even a target who fails gets to repeat the save at the end of each turn. They may fail once, even twice, but they’re probably not going to fail three times in a row. The Nightveil specter doesn’t have time to dither. If it’s going to Reap, it has to Reap immediately, or it risks losing the opportunity.

Additionally, Reap Memory doesn’t work forward in time. It erases memories of the past, but it doesn’t prevent new memories from being formed from new experiences. So if you have a group of strangers who’ve discovered someplace that House Dimir doesn’t want them to know about, you can’t wipe their memories there, then take them somewhere else. They may not remember how they got there, but they’ll remember being taken away, and where they’re taken away from, and that’s bad enough.

So I guess I can’t put off discussing the gloamwing any longer. This Large undead mount (animated from Krokt knows what—some kind of dragon, maybe?) possesses exceptional Strength, very high Dexterity and Constitution, and the Intelligence of a lizard, and it operates poorly in daylight, having the Sunlight Sensitivity trait. It, too, is proficient in Perception and Stealth, allowing it to glide with owl-like silence, while its size and Strength allow it to carry up to 600 pounds at a time. Nightveil specters aren’t incorporeal, but being undead, they can’t weigh all that much, can they? So let’s say that a gloamwing can snatch two Medium humanoids in its clawed feet and carry them off without difficulty, assuming it can win the grappling contests. And if a target is stunned or unconscious, it’s no contest at all—which is good, because each of those grappling contests would be an action if the target were able to resist.

What has to happen when opponents venture into House Dimir territory, then, is that the Nightveil specter and its gloamwing companion knock them out, then carry them away, then wipe their memories of everything, including both the arrival and the ensuing fight. Leading with Mind Twist is still an efficient way to accomplish the knocking-out part, since the 22 psychic damage it deals on average to each target who fails their save comes very close to the 24 total damage the specter’s Scythe attack deals on a single hit, and its Multiattack lets it attack with Scythe twice. In a group of more than three, at least two targets will most likely fail, and they’ll be stunned to boot.

Meanwhile, the gloamwing has attacks of its own.
