To use these attacks, it has to be an independent mount, since a controlled mount can only Dodge, Dash or Disengage, so it takes its turn on its own initiative account. It makes its attacks against non-stunned targets, flying wherever it needs to and ending its movement out of opponents’ reach. Its Flyby trait exempts not only itself but also its rider from opportunity attacks, since the rider isn’t moving out of foes’ reach under its own power. The gloamwing attacks to knock its targets out, not to kill them, and after its initial Mind Twist, the Nightveil specter does the same with its own Scythe attacks. It doesn’t use Mind Twist again after its initial blast, because it can’t make this damage nonlethal, and it doesn’t want to inadvertently kill anybody if it hasn’t been ordered to. What it wants to do is knock everybody out.

Here’s why: As I mentioned above, a target of Mind Twist, even if they fail their first save, gets to repeat it each turn. But a target who’s reduced to 0 hp by attacks to subdue stays unconscious (and therefore, by definition, incapacitated) for 1d4 hours. That’s much better! Once everyone’s gone nappy-bye, the gloamwing can grab two of them at a time and transport them to an inconspicuous neutral location up to a mile away. And once that’s done, the Nightveil specter can Reap Memory on the unconscious saps at its leisure, deleting all memory of their encounter with House Dimir. If there are more than three, it can send its gloamwing off to fetch another specter or two to finish the job (the only situation in which a gloamwing might be encountered without its rider).

Or … they can just kill the others. The living will wake up, see their friends dead and remember only that they were going somewhere together, but not remember where. However, this measure, if taken against members of another Ravnican guild, is likely to provoke tit-for-tat retaliation, so House Dimir discourages it.

The strategy of relocating enemies before mind-wiping them additionally solves the problem that a Nightveil specter’s Intelligence allows it no way to determine who’s likeliest to snap out of a Mind Twist first. Even its Wisdom 17 plus Insight proficiency, I’d argue, isn’t enough to allow it to intuit this information, although it does suffice to intuit whether trespassers are trespassing innocently or with espionage in mind. Even against spies, though, the Nightveil specter’s standing orders are to do no more harm than necessary to deter curiosity. That’s House Dimir’s way: Deter … but don’t escalate. Getting repelled, minus your memories, should be message enough. Try it again, and we won’t play nice next time.

No amount of damage will force a Nightveil specter or a gloamwing to retreat; their compulsion to obey their Dimir masters is too strong. But killing a Nightveil specter destroys its gloamwing, and killing its gloamwing drives a Nightveil specter to vengeful frenzy.

Next: Golgari NPCs.

title: Mind Drinker Vampire Tactics
content_text:
Ravnican blood drinker vampires are creatures of the night—primarily. It’s not because they have to be: They lack the Sunlight Sensitivity and trait of off-the-rack Dungeons & Dragons vampires. It’s simply that their darkvision makes it advantageous for them to operate after sundown, so they prefer to do so. In theory, however, they might be encountered anytime. Mind drinker vampires, on the other hand, do have Sunlight Sensitivity, along with Shadow Stealth (like the shadow horrors I examined last time). They’re strictly nocturnal.

With extraordinary Intelligence and exceptional Dexterity, mind drinker vampires are psionic spellslingers, capable of melee (they also have very high Strength) but somewhat averse to it, preferring to strike physically only when they’re fairly sure they can finish an enemy off in one strike, two at most. They’re proficient in Perception and Stealth, the skills of an ambush attacker, and it’s worth noting that their Mind Siphon action is neither an attack nor a spell, so a hidden mind drinker vampire can use it without immediately giving its position away. In fact, since only spells with a verbal component give away a hidden creature’s position, the mind drinker vampire can use any of its psionic “spells”—even message!—without making itself a target. Consequently, if a mind drinker vampire can accomplish its mission without ever coming out of hiding, it does.

Mind Siphon is the mind drinker vampire’s key ability, combining the more superficial portion of detect thoughts with a jolt of psychic damage. It can’t be used to dig deeper, the way detect thoughts can, so it’s not especially useful as an interrogation tool. By House Dimir’s standards, this feature is a very blunt instrument. So what’s it for? Clandestine commando raids, basically. Rapid operations in which the guild’s agents must physically penetrate a location, are using the mind drinker vampire to tell them where to go and what to watch out for, and aren’t concerned about whether they leave a trail of bodies behind them.

A small problem: A target of Mind Siphon makes their saving throw with disadvantage if the MDV has consumed their blood, but the only way that’s going to happen is if the MDV Bites them first. If they’re hidden in the shadows, of course, they can make that attack with advantage, then follow up with Mind Siphon, a very effective double play. But I have to ask myself, does the mind drinker vampire want to come out of the shadows?

Well, it may not have a choice. DC 14 is respectable, but it’s not going to give the MDV a two-thirds chance of success on any target with a zero or positive Intelligence save modifier—and I don’t see House Dimir as being especially tolerant of failure. So I think the mind drinker vampire has to Multiattack from concealment, first Biting (advantage is essential to ensuring that the MDV gets its drinky-drink), then Unarmed Striking (grappling the target is nice but less mission-critical). Ceteris paribus, this order would make sense, but as veteran reader Idan bhk reminds us below, the MDV can Bite only a grappled, restrained or incapacitated target, which means Unarmed Strike must come first. Given this requirement, it’s fortuitous that a hidden mind drinker vampire gains advantage on its Unarmed Strike attack from concealment, since the whole sequence falls apart if this attack doesn’t hit.

A thing to keep in mind is that Mind Siphon is for use against key targets, not just anybody. It takes a few rounds, on average, to recharge, so if the mind drinker vampire makes the error of choosing a target who’s lost in thought about something irrelevant, it may not be able to try again on its next turn, or on the turn after that—or, if it’s unlucky, the turn after that. Also, since it needs to Bite first to undermine its target’s ability to resist Mind Siphon, it has to take care that its Bite doesn’t kill the target before it can extract any useful information from them. For the mind drinker vampire to have two-thirds certainty that this won’t occur, the target must have at least 17 hp; for 90 percent certainty, at least 19 hp; and for 95 percent certainty, at least 20 hp. And that’s if it only Bites. If it plans to use its entire Multiattack, the numbers are 26, 29 and 30 hp. Fortunately, the mind drinker vampire’s Intelligence is high enough that it can judge its target’s hit points accurately at a glance.

“The most suspicious vampires might even siphon thoughts from their subordinates to detect any hint of betrayal,” the Guildmasters’ Guide to Ravnica suggests in the mind drinker vampire’s flavor text, but note that a failed save against Mind Siphon results in an average of 28 psychic damage, and spies—including thought spies—typically have only 27 hp. So it’s not a simple loyalty test that the MDV can administer any time it feels a bit mistrustful of its companions. If a mind drinker vampire is using Mind Siphon this way, it’s made up its mind about its fellow agent’s loyalty already. A doppelgänger is a different story: It’s got enough hit points that it could take the drain and survive. No mind mage would ever consider themself—or allow anyone else to consider them—a mind drinker vampire’s subordinate.

Anyway, enough about Mind Siphon. Let’s look at the mind drinker vampire’s psionic “spells”:

Gaseous form is an escape hatch, but since it slows the mind drinker vampire down significantly, the MDV uses it only when it’s within 10 feet of an aperture that its enemies can’t follow it through.

Major image is rather situational. A mind drinker vampire might use it to lure targets into its reach, but it might also use it to distract targets away from where it wants to go, so that it can sneak past without getting caught. Not every circumstance calls for messy murder.

Charm person is for situations that call for a social scalpel rather than a brutal psionic sledgehammer. Combined with the mind drinker vampire’s heretofore undiscussed Deception and Insight proficiencies, plus a Dimir-appropriate magic item such as a hat of disguise (which lets the wearer cast disguise self) or house signet ring (which does the same), it can elicit valuable intel from a target without having to resort to bloodsucking at all. The mind drinker vampire casts this “spell” from hiding, stepping out into the open with a jaunty greeting if the target fails their saving throw and switching to plan B if the target succeeds (fortunately none the wiser).

Hold person is a gold-star spell in so many other circumstances, but for the mind drinker vampire, it’s almost a trap choice. The error is to use it to try to paralyze a target as setup for a Bite attack an Unarmed Strike attack. It’s an error because the DC of hold person is the same as Mind Siphon’s, but with no way to impose disadvantage on it. The whole point of Biting first is to impose saving throw disadvantage, and the whole point of Unarmed Striking first is to enable Bite. Now, since it’s delivered psionically and therefore silently, one might argue that a successful save against hold person is no big deal; the target still has no idea that the mind drinker vampire is there. But an unseen mind drinker vampire doesn’t need hold person to grant it advantage on its Bite attack roll; it’s got that already from being unseen. The benefit of hold person is that it increases the damage of the attack, and the mind drinker vampire may not even need that. After hits with Bite and Unarmed Strike Unarmed Strike and Bite and a successful Mind Siphon, it’s dealt an average of 50 damage already, enough to knock a bottom-quartile level 8 player character out. One more Multiattack will take down the second quartile as well. And remember, the MDV isn’t necessarily traveling alone. The chief argument against hold person as setup for an attack, however, is that it wastes precious time. Leaping out of the shadows to Bite seize and Bite a target comes as a shock. Seeing the target suddenly freeze in place without any explanation puts the target’s allies on their guard. There are other reasons why a mind drinker vampire might want to cast hold person—to prevent pursuit, perhaps, or to immobilize a lone guard—but this particular application isn’t one of them.

Mirror image adds a funny acid-trip quality to the mind drinker vampire’s attack: Instead of one MDV leaping out of the shadows, four identical ones leap out, all swirling around each other. It’s also practical prophylaxis. But when every second counts, does the MDV really want to spend six of them on this parlor trick?

Sleep verges on pointless under 2014 rules, but under 2024 rules (am I supposed to be calling them 5.5E rules now?—I’m so confused), it has an exceptionally nasty application. With one failed Wisdom save, it can impose the incapacitated condition. Who can make excellent use of the incapacitated condition? Intellect devourers, which are listed in the Dimir Creatures table in chapter 6 of the GGR, and whose Body Thief ability targets incapacitated humanoids. Normally it has to stun its victims with Devour Intellect, which can be resisted with an easily achievable DC 12 Intelligence save. The mind drinker vampire’s DC 14 save is 10 percentage points less easily achievable, and the 5-foot radius of its area of effect can include a second target standing alongside the first, giving it two chances to incapacitate at least one of them.

Since Shadow Stealth allows the mind drinker vampire to Hide as a bonus action in dim light or darkness but doesn’t grant any special ability to Hide when it otherwise couldn’t, it doesn’t fit well into the MDV’s attack sequence. It’s quite useful, however, for vanishing if and when a mind drinker vampire decides to run away (or rather, fly away—incidentally, the fact that it never has to touch the ground if it doesn’t want to is a great enhancement to its stealth).

The fact that mind drinker vampires are valued assets, which might bend them toward caution, is balanced out by their undead nature and evil alignment, which makes them enjoy hurting their enemies more, so they don’t retreat from combat unless and until they’re seriously wounded. However, they’re fragile compared with other CR 4 monsters, with nothing to compensate for their lack of hit points but three saving throw proficiencies (one of which isn’t even in the big three) and resistance to necrotic damage (they’re the ones dealing the necrotic damage, not taking it). So rather than calculate “seriously wounded” based off their own hit points, I’m going to calculate it against the low end of the normal hit point range for CR 4 (per Sly Flourish’s Lazy 5E Monster Builder Document) and say they withdraw when reduced to 25 hp or fewer, or about 45 percent of their hit point maximum, rather than the usual 40 percent. Alternatively, you might decide to give them between 64 and 84 hp to begin with, which fits within their 10d8 + 10 distribution, and have them withdraw at 40 percent of whatever hit point maximum you choose.

Next: nightveil specters.

title: Horror Tactics
content_text:
Horrors are barely sentient living nightmares pressed into service by the guilds of Ravnica to stalk and, occasionally, kill their enemies—and no guild makes greater use of horrors than House Dimir. Horrors come in three varieties: flying horrors, shadow horrors and skittering horrors.

With their extraordinary Dexterity putting all their other abilities to shame, flying horrors are shock attackers that terrify their targets, maul them, then skedaddle. Their 120-foot darkvision and Sunlight Sensitivity inform us that they’re active exclusively at night. They use their impressive Stealth skill to get within 90 feet of their targets, then on their next turn use their full 60-foot flying movement to get close enough to let loose a Frightening Screech against those targets, along with any and all allies present.

This maneuver would be more impressive if the DC on it were higher: Given that House Dimir doesn’t send out flying horrors willy-nilly but rather aims them at specific enemies, it seems uncharacteristically sloppy for them to use an asset against which a target will succeed on their saving throw at least one-third of the time and in many cases even more often than that. Not only that, but the target gets to repeat that saving throw at the end of each of its turns, so even if it fails the first time, it will probably succeed on the second or third. But it’s what the flying horror’s got, so we may as well use it.

While using Frightening Screech, a flying horror remains airborne, out of reach of its foes’ melee attacks. On its next two turns, it swoops down and strikes with its claws. Given its Challenge Rating and saving throw DC, a flying horror is likely to be sent only against low-level player characters—specifically, levels 3 and 4. Such characters typically have between 20 and 39 hp; since Claws deals an average total of 22 damage on a hit, two rounds of attacks should be enough to get the target rolling death saves. Regardless of whether it succeeds at that or not, at the start of round 4, the flying horror departs, Dashing while flying (and possibly provoking one or more opportunity attacks as it does). If it should happen to succeed early, it may depart early, or it may go ahead and make a second attack against its unconscious target. It’s not smart or flexible.

With luck, three rounds of combat will be enough for the flying horror to do its job. It’s got a high Armor Class for its CR, but its hit points are on the low end, and it has no damage resistance. It’s counting on imposing disadvantage, via the frightened condition, on enough of its foes’ attack rolls that when they swing on it, they’ll miss. That’s a reasonable bet to make, but it’s not a safe one.

A flying horror is driven off by being severely wounded (reduced to 19 hp or fewer) or by taking any amount of radiant damage, which horrors abhor. It also flies away if it downs its target in round 2 but then takes 15 damage or more before it can attack the unconscious target again in round 3.

Flying horrors are the size of an adult human; shadow horrors are much larger, equal to or greater than a grizzly bear in size. Their standout ability score is Charisma, followed closely by Wisdom and Dex, indicating a preference for spooky action at a distance—but their stat block supports this preference inconsistently. Their Lashing Shadows action deals significant necrotic damage from up to 60 feet away, but it’s on a 5–6 recharge; in between uses, shadow horrors have to find other things to do, and these things consist mostly of melee.

Let’s examine what they can do, then figure out how these features fit together best.

Shadow Stealth is a common feature that allows a creature to Hide as a bonus action while in dim light or darkness. Note that, contrary to a widespread misreading that I’ve been guilty of myself, this does not mean that it can Hide anytime it’s in dim light or darkness. Rather, it means that if it’s in dim light or darkness and the other conditions for hiding are met, it can Hide as a bonus action. It’s an action economy enhancer, not a power that lets it hide in any shadow.

Shadow Stride allows the shadow horror to teleport from one shadow to another, as long as they’re within 60 feet of each other and cast by a creature or object of sufficient size. This ability is also a bonus action, so it’s not happening on the same turn as Shadow Stealth. It’s also not much good if the shadow horror isn’t already in a shadow to begin with. Note that it doesn’t specify that the shadow horror must be able to see the destination shadow—or even that its path must be unobstructed.

Incorporeal Movement lets the shadow horror move through solid objects. Thus, on any turn when it doesn’t have anything better to do, it could simply duck through a wall so that nobody could attack it until it came back, but shadow horrors aren’t smart or flexible, either. Such a move demands more creativity than they’ve got.

Multiattack is self-explanatory. The order of the shadow horror’s attacks doesn’t matter, because even though Claw has a chance of imposing the frightened condition, that only imposes disadvantage on its target’s attack rolls; it doesn’t confer advantage on the shadow horror’s own attacks. Therefore, the effects of the condition will be felt only after the shadow horror’s turn is over.

One thing that the shadow horror does have working in its favor is the combination of proficiency in Perception and expertise in Stealth. It’s a consummate ambush attacker. Also, there’s a subtle and intriguing implication in the wording of Lashing Shadows: “Each creature within 60 feet of the horror, except other horrors, must succeed on a DC 16 Dexterity saving throw … ” (emphasis mine). This wording suggests that shadow horrors don’t always attack alone but rather are often accompanied by the lesser flying horrors. That keeps them threatening all through tier 2 and into tier 3—up to about level 12. And that’s assuming you’re limiting yourself to just one shadow horror.

All right, suppose House Dimir has sent a shadow horror to deal with an obnoxious Selesnya-affiliated tree hugger. The mission takes place at night, of course, because the shadow horror, like other horrors, has 120 feet of darkvision and the Sunlight Sensitivity trait. The shadow horror can’t fly, but on the other hand, walls can’t stop it. As long as it knows where its target is—something that has to be explained to it very simply and slowly, because with Intelligence 2, it’s going to get lost after the second instruction—it can make a beeline toward them, get within 60 unobstructed feet of them and let loose with Lashing Shadows against the target and anyone else in the room. Shadow horrors are not precision weapons; House Dimir doesn’t send one when it’s concerned about what happens to bystanders.

Then what? It seems at first that it would make sense for the shadow horror to use whatever movement it has left to get into its target’s shadow. But I think it would keep its distance for as long as it could—remember, it doesn’t like melee as much as it likes lashing out from across a room. Rather, I think that as soon as it’s established line of sight to its target, it heads for the nearest shadow it can use Shadow Stride with, then uses Lashing Shadows, then stays put. If any enemy wants to engage it in melee, that’s their problem; it’s not going to make it any easier for them.

What it does on its next turn depends on what its opponents do and whether Lashing Shadows happens to recharge. If it does, the shadow horror uses it again, obviously. Otherwise, it wants to attack its intended target directly. If the target has engaged it in melee, great; it doesn’t have to go anywhere. But if a different enemy has engaged it, that’s an unwanted inconvenience. In that instance, the shadow horror uses Shadow Stride to teleport into its target’s shadow, then lays into them with its Multiattack. It keeps making melee attacks until Lashing Shadows recharges. At that point, if other enemies have engaged it in melee, it Shadow Strides out of reach before using Lashing Shadows again; if they haven’t, it stays put and uses Lashing Shadows where it is.

It’s hard to work Shadow Stealth into this attack sequence, because there are only two things the shadow horror really wants to do: Lashing Shadows and Multiattack. Neither of these actions leaves it a good chance to move anyplace it will be able to Hide, even as a bonus action. As long as it’s standing next to its target, its position is obvious, and there’s no good reason for it to physically move away and risk an opportunity attack when it can teleport instead—but doing that consumes its bonus action.

I think Shadow Stealth is more likely something the shadow horror uses when it’s trying to get away, e.g., when it’s severely wounded (reduced to 48 hp or fewer) or after it’s taken a hit dealing 12 or more radiant damage. In this instance, it hustles away through the nearest wall or closed door, makes for the nearest spot that isn’t brightly lit, takes the Hide action and uses its remaining movement to keep going, sticking to the shadows as closely as it can.

With the skittering horror—a tier 3 opponent, elephantine in size—we get more of the same sort of thing, except that this horror has extraordinary Strength and isn’t shy about melee confrontation at all. With Spider Climb and a 40-foot climbing speed, the skittering horror comes in through the bathroom window and across the ceiling, positions itself directly above its target and Multiattacks. First, it gives them a little Vir Cotto wave or a jaunty whistle, exposing them to its Maddening Presence. Next, it drops down to attack with its Bite and Claws—or simply chills out on the ceiling, since these melee attacks have a 10-foot reach.

Really, there’s nothing more it needs to do—and not much more that it can do. If anyone comes to its targets’ aid, it can use its Maddening Presence on them, since it has no effect on anyone the skittering horror has used it on already, but it confines its melee attacks to the target it was sent to bedevil, and it doesn’t come down from the ceiling if it doesn’t have to.

An average total 76 damage (from one Bite and two Claws) will knock out most PCs of level 8 or lower in one round and PCs of level 16 or lower in two. When the skittering horror’s work is done, it leaves the way it came in. It can be driven off by seriously wounding it (reducing it to 91 hp or fewer) or dealing it at least 23 radiant damage before it decides to leave on its own.

Next: mind drinker vampires.

title: Dimir NPC Tactics
content_text:
House Dimir is a guild of secret agents, assassins and other shadowy horrors; if its members ever find themselves in a head-to-head fight, they’ve done something wrong. I began my posts on Azorius and Boros NPCs with discussion of their soldiers, but Dimir doesn’t use soldiers. When other guilds’ soldiers are on the march, Dimir’s forces are nowhere to be seen. Their best defense is not to be found.

Instead of soldiers, the bulk of House Dimir’s active assets are spies. As I discuss in How to Defend Your Lair and in my upcoming revised edition of The Monsters Know What They’re Doing, due out later this year, the spy stat block in the Monster Manual is an unrealistic, idealized notion of a spy that isn’t even particularly good at what spies do. It’s better to think of spies as falling into two categories:

Snoops, unremarkable “gray people” whose job is to observe, report and, most important of all, not be noticed.

Plants, who go undercover to observe but also to conduct clandestine activities under their targets’ noses. They can’t help but be noticed, so they strive instead not to be doubted.

As I state in this excerpt from The Monsters Know Revised:

The spy stat block in the Monster Manual is much closer to the second category of spy, although it still misses the mark a bit. Intelligence and Wisdom are more important to a spy than Dexterity: Above all, a spy must have a detailed memory and the ability to immediately notice anything out of the ordinary. This quality is reflected in the Monster Manual spy’s proficiency in Insight and expertise in Investigation and Perception, at least, but the spy’s Intelligence and Investigation modifiers should probably both be a point higher. Deception is an essential part of a spy’s job, and Charisma is important for the second category of spy, but to truly represent what’s referred to in tradecraft as a “plant,” they should have not just proficiency in Deception but expertise (+7).

Gray people, meanwhile, engage with the targets of their observation as little as possible; they simply report what they see and hear in the course of their ordinary activities, and the only pretense they need to engage in is that they’re not watching and listening. Skill in Stealth or Sleight of Hand can certainly be helpful, but being unseen and unheard isn’t the ultimate goal. Being unnoticed is, and the best way to be unnoticed is to be unimportant. None of the spy personas suggested in the Monster Manual is suitable for a gray person: They all call too much attention to themselves. Better a taciturn bartender, server, housekeeper, or regular patron at a well trafficked inn; a market-square beggar, from whom everyone looks away; or a ragpicker, gathering overheard rumors along with scraps. They could make do with a much lower Charisma, since self-control, not force of personality, is the deciding factor in whether they succeed in seeming perfectly ordinary. That’s Wisdom.

A gray person isn’t going to engage in combat unless they’re truly desperate. If they’re busted, they run, using Cunning Action to Disengage if they must to get out of an enemy’s reach, to Dash to put distance between themselves and their pursuers, and to Hide once they’re out of sight. If they succeed, they may never be seen again in the same city, town, or village. Whomever they’ve been reporting to will set them up someplace else. You can’t easily hide a sword or a crossbow under a tavern dress or a beggar’s cloak, so they don’t carry these weapons, only a dagger … which they use only as a weapon of last resort.

A busted plant will deny everything, act wounded by the accusation, propose a believable alternative explanation of the ugly facts before them, and reluctantly but warmly forgive their accusers when they apologize—anything to avoid a direct violent confrontation long enough to disappear. If they must run, though, they run. They may not carry a weapon on their person—or if they do, it may be just a dagger—but they have the weapons in their stat block stashed where they can retrieve them if their life is in danger. At that point, they move cautiously, stay hidden, and do nothing to give their presence away. Taking a potshot at their pursuers, even with Advantage from being unseen, would accomplish nothing beyond exposing their own position. Escape is always the paramount goal. Cornered spies, however, fight to the death. Captured spies are nearly always killed, or at least tossed in nasty dungeons to think about what they’ve done for a long time, and the reputations of the organizations they belong to hang on their members’ unwillingness to give up the secrets they’ve learned. This combination of factors produces a kind of zealotry that overrides their self-preservation impulses.

Modifications: Planted spies have an ear for deceit. Attempts to Influence them with Deception are made with Disadvantage, and you, the DM, should roll the second die. On a failed check that would have succeeded by the player’s die roll alone, the spy takes the desired action anyway but is aware of the attempt to deceive them. Also, give plants expertise in Deception (+7) along with proficiency in up to two extra skills necessary for them to maintain their false identities, plus one Ability Score Improvement feat for every four levels of the party average, rounded down, and a Proficiency Bonus equal to the PCs’ (adjusting modifiers accordingly). For example, a spy embedded with a level 9 party would have two ASIs and a +4 Proficiency Bonus. These extras allow spies to monitor more experienced adventurers without raising suspicion.

Unique to House Dimir is the thought spy, a variant spy with slightly less Charisma, significantly more Intelligence, double proficiency in Deception (thank you) but only regular proficiency in Investigation and Perception (what?), darkvision (OK, makes sense for Dimir) and psionic “spellcasting” in lieu of Sneak Attack (good—spies are spies, not assassins). The nerfing of Investigation and Perception is perhaps the most perplexing thing about this stat block, since more than anything else, these skills are what spies are for. Since it has no effect whatsoever on the thought spy’s challenge rating, I’d raise their Investigation modifier to +7 and their Perception to +5. Alternatively, use the snoop or plant stat block from HTDYL appendix B and add the thought spy’s Innate Spellcasting skill.

Probably the most useful “spell” in the thought spy’s psionic arsenal is gaseous form, because it allows them to “vanish” when they hear someone coming while they’re someplace they don’t want to be found. Although it reduces their speed to 10 feet, casting gaseous form as an action, then using their Cunning Action trait to Dash as a bonus action and their full movement to keep going lets them slip away through any available crack or crevice. Alternatively, if they’re trapped someplace with no way out, they can use Cunning Action to Hide instead, so that nobody notices the odd mist lingering in the room. On a subsequent turn, they can then use their movement, action and bonus action to slip out by whatever way their pursuer(s) came in.

Next most useful is disguise self, since it allows them to assume any guise that helps establish their false identity. For a snoop, disguise self is even more useful than gaseous form, because it allows them to remain unnoticed by adopting a completely unremarkable appearance—or, even better, the appearance of someone that most people don’t want to look at.

But a thought spy isn’t just a spy. It’s right there in the name: “thought spy.” Any spy would love to have an ability like gaseous form or disguise self, but detect thoughts is the thought spy’s sine qua non. The thing is not to get greedy with it. In most cases, surface thoughts are enough to give the thought spy something good to go on. Probing deeper into a target’s mind, however, tips the target off. Pay attention to the spell’s wording (in both its 2014 and 2024 versions): “The target knows (that) you are probing into its mind.” They aren’t merely aware that someone is trying to read their mind—they’re aware that you are. Only the most inept House Dimir thought spy would give themself away in this manner while out in the field. This application of detect thoughts is an interrogation technique, used on captives by agents who aren’t trying to hide who and what they are.

Then there’s charm person. This power plus disguise self is a potent combination, because they both last an hour and neither of them requires concentration. The thought spy uses disguise self to cook up an entire look from scratch—a “burner” identity, if you will—and then casts charm person on whomever it wants to schmooze. When charm person ends, the target knows they were charmed, and they know who did it … or rather, they think they do. But the person they know charmed them doesn’t actually exist.

Encode thoughts is clever but risky. On the one hand, it produces a tangible record that can be taken back to House Dimir’s vaults and kept for use as evidence in a trial or blackmail material. On the other hand, it’s also proof that the possessor is a spy, and if a spy is busted with one, it can be taken and used against them—or against House Dimir. Therefore, a thought spy uses this power only when House Dimir must have material proof of someone’s thoughts (either a target’s or those of the thought spy, such as their firsthand observations), and they wear clothing that gives them plenty of places to hide any thought strand(s) they’re carrying. Of course, the best way not to have your clothes rifled is to make your getaway before you’re captured or killed. (And let’s be blunt: For a Dimir spy, capture almost always means death.)

According to the stat block, the thought spy carries a dagger and a rapier. But they never carry the rapier unless they’re disguised as the sort of respectable citizen who’d wear one out and about, and any dagger they have is carefully hidden and drawn only under extreme duress. A thought spy’s first tactic is to be gone before they’re found; their second is to Disengage (using Cunning Action) and make tracks. Speed plus disguise self plus a city the size of a continent is the perfect formula for vanishing into a crowd.

The mind mage rarely ventures out into the field, and when they do, they rarely disguise their identity. On the contrary, they often flaunt it, because people are terrified of them. But when they’re on the move, they’d prefer not to be tracked, and since they can’t magically disguise themselves the way thought spies can, they rely on simpler devices, such as a good old hooded cloak or curtained carriage.

Mind mages specialize in the rough stuff: interrogations, crowd control, mind-wiping and turning folks into Manchurian candidates (for the 1-minute duration of dominate person, anyway). If you’re going to conduct interrogations, pick up HTDYL and read chapter 7, “Taking Prisoners.” Mind mages are good at what they do and aren’t going to make rookie mistakes like challenging or belittling interrogation subjects right out of the gate. They’re slick. They’ll get their subjects wanting to talk—just to set the record straight, mind you, just to make sure you don’t have the wrong idea about them. Most of the time, they don’t even need magic to get the information they’re looking for. But it helps. (That said, if a mind mage is on the case, they’re looking for specific information, and you should know what specific information they’re looking for. Open-ended interrogations are fruitless interrogations.)

While interrogating a subject, a mind mage activates their spies’ murmur, an eavesdropping device that lets a confederate listen in on the proceedings so that House Dimir always has someone else in possession of whatever information is gained. They also make much more extensive use of encode thoughts: Unlike the garden-variety snoop or the tightrope-walking plant, they have little need to hide who they are and what they’re up to. However, depending on how much rapport the mind mage has or hasn’t established with their subject, creating a thought strand in the middle of questioning might cause the subject to clam up. Therefore, a mind mage generally refrains from using encode thoughts during an interrogation, except when the subject is eager to provide proof of what they’re saying (i.e., when the answer to a question like “Would you testify to that in court?” is an emphatic “Yes!”) or when using detect thoughts to probe the subject’s mind in tandem with charm person to stay on their good side (“I’m sure you won’t mind if I just jot this information down”). Otherwise, the mind mage uses encode thoughts immediately after the interrogation is over, to record what they’ve learned out of the subject’s presence.

In any encounter with a mind mage, the mind mage is in control of the situation. That’s how it’s supposed to be, at least. When a mind mage is no longer in control of the situation, the mind mage leaves, and a different mind mage is sent to take over—or the situation is such that mind mages are no longer what’s needed. Thus, combat encounters involving mind mages are rare: By the time combat breaks out, the mind mage is no longer there. Certainly, they don’t want to be part of any physical altercation. Depending on the depth of the trouble they’re in, a mind mage who’s in danger of being attacked directly tries to preempt the situation with mass suggestion or dominate person and manipulate their foes into letting them go free. And if that doesn’t work? They surrender. Unconditionally, without hesitation or even resentment. Mind mages are valuable to House Dimir, which is more than happy to pay a ransom to get an asset back alive—and, more important, to get its hands on the information that asset has acquired.

Next: flying horrors.

title: A Small Quality-of-Life Improvement
content_text: I’ve been dutifully tagging all my posts on monsters from the Guildmasters’ Guide to Ravnica with the “ravnica” tag, but aside from my post–Monsters of the Multiverse updates to the monsters I analyze in MOAR! Monsters Know What They’re Doing, I haven’t been so conscientious with monsters from other books. That changes now. I’ve just gone through all my posts from Eberron: Rising From the Last War, Ezmerelda’s Guide to Ravenloft, Fizban’s Treasury of Dragons, Spelljammer, Planescape and Mythic Odysseys of Theros and tagged them accordingly. Now you can use the tag cloud in the sidebar to go straight to monsters from each of these books. It’s something I’ve been meaning to do for a long time, and today I discovered just how easy it was, so … presto! I hope readers find these new tags to be helpful.

title: Guardian Giant Tactics
content_text:
Guardian giants are gatekeeping sentinels with longstanding beef against the Gruul Clans, to which most other giants owe their allegiance. Once in a while, they also venture out on more active operations against exceptional threats.

Absolute tanks, guardian giants possess extraordinary Strength and Constitution, but their exceptional Wisdom also stands out. They can’t be surprised, and they’re extremely difficult to slip past unnoticed. They’re also far more agile than their size would suggest. They have proficiency in Dexterity and Wisdom saving throws, so not only are they hard to manipulate using magic, they’re also surprisingly good at dodging incoming projectiles, both magical and mundane.

Tactically, there’s nothing remarkable about them: They’re front-line brutes that hold their ground when defending and move directly to engage in melee when attacking, using their spears one-handed since they carry shields. Although their speed is 40 feet, if they’re advancing alongside a unit of Boros soldiers, they slow their movement so that they don’t get more than 10 feet ahead of their comrades. This way, they can use their Protection reaction to hinder incoming attacks against important allies—“important” being defined first and foremost by rank, since while the overall Boros ethos is lawful good, guardian giants themselves are lawful neutral. However, if all allies within reach are of the same rank, they’ll defend whoever happens to be nearby and need it.

What makes guardian giants interesting is their strategy. Not lavishly endowed with Intelligence, they’re not stupid, either, and they recognize immediately when a particular foe is key to an enemy’s battle plan. If they’re not in a position to take on that foe themselves, either alone or with assistance—usually, because there’s another responsibility they can’t abandon—they direct their allies to do so. Moreover, their Wisdom gives them a keen sense of the tide of battle. Thus, while they’re not flexible attackers or planners, they have an unerring instinct for when everyone else should change plans.

Generally speaking, creatures with Wisdom 14 or higher choose their battles carefully, fight only when they’re sure they’ll win, and refrain from combat in favor of parley when they see they’re outmatched. Guardian giants have Wisdom 18. They might not be able to articulate it, but in their gut, they know whether a matchup is Easy, Medium, Hard or Deadly (per the 2014 Dungeon Master’s Guide’s encounter guidelines) or Low, Moderate or High Difficulty (per the 2024 DMG’s).

If the encounter is Deadly (or High Difficulty) for their own side, or if it’s Easy or Medium (or Low Difficulty) for the other side, they advise against engagement. When defending a position, that means they tell everyone to take shelter, get behind walls (they’re the last inside, after everyone else is safe) and prepare for siege. When planning an attack, it means they state in no uncertain terms that the operation is likely to fail, and they refuse to budge unless and until the odds can be swung back in their favor—or unless they’re under strict, direct orders to sacrifice their unit for the sake of victory. If an attack is under way already and is going poorly, guardian giants delay the enemy to allow their allies to withdraw safely; ditto if they’re under siege and their side begins to take unanticipated losses.

Sometimes this delay takes the form of requesting a halt to hostilities in order to talk terms. They’re not natural diplomats, although their Charisma is modestly above humanoid average, but they’re reasonable and willing to talk turkey. Thanks to their Insight proficiency, they’re also good at discerning when an enemy is trying to put one over on them, and they call out such bad-faith bargaining without hesitation. They’re aware of what their best alternative to a negotiated agreement is, and if their parley isn’t heading in a direction that’s at least as good as that or better, they’ll say so. Play them as loyal, forthright and shrewd.

Guardian giants will usually recognize that a battle is going against them and order a retreat long before they’re seriously wounded (reduced to 54 hp or fewer), but if it comes to that, their first priority is to get their smaller allies away safely. Saving themselves matters, but it’s secondary.

Next: Dimir NPCs.

title: Boros Angel and Skyjek Roc Tactics
content_text:
Notwithstanding the reckoner’s ability to cast levitate (which I maintain is of limited utility—if melee is your preferred mode, neither floating up away from your foes nor causing them to float up away from you serves you well), the Boros Legion’s humanoid forces are basically infantry, with some heavy-weapon and artillery capability in the form of their mages.

The Skyjek roc turns a Boros knight into Boros air cavalry. Despite the name, it’s a horse-size avian, not jumbo jet–size like the Monster Manual roc. The flavor text in the Guildmasters’ Guide to Ravnica indicates that Skyjek rocs are used for reconnaissance, bombardment and dogfighting, each of which comes with its own set of tactics.

But first the basics: Skyjek rocs are strong and fast brutes, with extraordinary Strength, high Constitution and little else to distinguish them ability-wise. Proficiency in Dexterity saving throws makes them better than average at dodging area effects, and proficiency in Wisdom saves gives them a bit of added resistance to confusion, hold monster and the like. They’re proficient in Perception, too, and they have advantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on sight (if you’re using 2024 rules, ditch Keen Sight and boost the Skyjek roc’s Perception modifier to +4), but it’s their riders whose eyesight recon missions depend on, because Skyjek rocs lack the ability to communicate their observations.

When it comes to reconnaissance, smaller is better. Recon patrols are just large enough that if they’re spotted and attacked, at least one member is sure to escape and make it back alive. Their duty is to observe and report, not to engage. The longer they can stay and gather intelligence, the better, but as soon as they’re threatened, they rush back to base. In this kind of operation, the Skyjek roc is a controlled mount (see 2014 Player’s Handbook chapter 9 and 2024 Player’s Handbook chapter 1, “Mounted Combat”), using its action to Dodge while its rider is observing (this doesn’t mean it’s literally juking all over the place in the air, just that it’s alert and ready to react) and to Dash while withdrawing. With a 90-foot flying speed, the Skyjek roc can rocket back to base at a speed of 180 feet per turn. (“Rocket” is a relative term: 180 feet per turn equals a little over 20 mph. But it’s faster than most things that are going to chase after it.)

Bombardment missions are essentially the same: The Skyjek roc, as a controlled mount, Dodges while its rider drops … something … on targets from above. The knight wields a heavy crossbow, but that doesn’t really count as “bombardment,” does it? However, chapter 5 of Ravnica gives us a tantalizing option: the Sunforger warhammer, which can be thrown up to 120 feet, where it explodes in a blast equivalent to three-fourths of a fireball spell!

The wielder can magically retrieve the now magically unexploded hammer on their next action, but they can’t make it explode again until they’ve taken at least a short rest. However, there’s also no stated range limit on how far they can be when they retrieve the hammer, so a bombing run can look something like this: The Skyjec roc flies its rider to within 120 feet of ground zero, then takes the Dodge action. Next, the rider hurls a Sunforger hammer. The following round, the rider Dodges, while the mount Dashes away. Once they’re both safely out of range of any kind of reprisal, the rider uses their action to retrieve the Sunforger.

A modern bomber flight most often consists of two to four aircraft but may comprise as many as 10 or 12. These are reasonable numbers of Skyknights to include in a single encounter, but keep in mind that a CR 3 knight mounted on a CR 2 Skyjek roc is equivalent to—actually, slightly better than—a single CR 4 creature, and these XP will add up fast, especially if you’re using 2014 Dungeon Master’s Guide encounter multipliers. Don’t send Skyknight bombers against low-level PCs; these are encounters for upper tier 2 and tier 3. Targets of bombing runs include enemy bases, workshops, construction yards, supply caches, equipment and, of course, combatants.

Dogfighting is a different story. In this case, whether the Skyjek roc is controlled or independent depends on the strength of the adversary and whether the mounted knight needs to take advantage of the roc’s natural weaponry. If the knight can handle their foe all by themself, all the mount needs to do is make sure it doesn’t fall unconscious and, by extension, out of the sky. In that case, the knight keeps their roc controlled, and it takes the Dodge action to reduce its likelihood of taking a hit. On the other hand, if the knight needs the extra damage from the roc’s beak and talons to be sure of defeating their foe, they allow the roc to move and act independently.

Boros legionnaires are zealous, but they’re not fools, and the Skyknights are an elite force not to be squandered. Consequently, they fight until either the rider or the mount is seriously wounded—that is, when the knight is reduced to 20 hp or fewer or the roc is reduced to 14 hp or fewer. At that point, if the Skyjek roc is controlled, it Disengages and flies away. However, if it’s independent, and its rider is the one who’s seriously wounded, it’s not smart enough to make that call by itself, and it Disengages and withdraws on its first turn after its rider tells it to.

A battleforce angel occupies a position in the Boros Legion roughly equivalent to a major—that is, it doesn’t command a regiment or battalion, but it outranks a captain and can tell them what to do. Its primary offensive ability is its exceptional Charisma, presumably the source of the radiant damage its Longsword attack inflicts, and it also possesses very high Wisdom and Strength. (Oddly for a supposed strategist, its Intelligence is barely above average. I’d give it at least a 14, increasing its Investigation modifier to +5.) Its Con is slightly higher than its Dex, but neither is anything to write home about: This celestial is a shock-and-awe combatant, relying on flight (including the Flyby trait), a high Armor Class and ferocious damage output to make up for a lackluster ability to either avoid or absorb damage.

There’s an interesting pattern in the battleforce angel’s features: proficiency in Perception and Investigation, plus 120 feet of darkvision and truesight. I read this as saying that the battleforce angel interprets anyone and anything that’s trying to conceal its true nature or position as ipso facto inimical, and therefore its primary target. But also, every once in a while, if something seems fishy, the battleforce angel will pause a moment and use its action to study the battlefield (or, if you’re playing under 2024 rules, to Study the battlefield) and try to discern the nature of its misgivings, rather than Multiattack.

The other thing that leaps out of the battleforce angel’s stat block is Battlefield Inspiration, an action that’s included in its Multiattack. Effectively a limited-duration bless spell that doesn’t require concentration, this feature lets the battleforce angel to designate up to three allies to receive a 1d4 bonus to their attack rolls and saving throws until the end of its next turn. Like bless, it has a 30-foot range, which incentivizes the battleforce angel to stay relatively close to its allies. Putting this together with its animus against foes that employ illusion, it’s not a stretch to imagine the battleforce angel, upon identifying a concealed enemy, immediately deputizing three of its allies as an ad hoc task force, ordering them to come along with it to deal with the problem—and buffing them to increase their effectiveness.

In the absence of such a specific threat, the battleforce angel helps its troops take down their toughest foes by flying in, striking twice with Longsword (always two-handed, since there’s nothing to indicate that it carries a shield), using Battlefield Inspiration to bless a few allies, then flying back out—provoking no opportunity attack, thanks to Flyby—and possibly baiting that foe into chasing it instead of continuing to pick on the mortal soldiers. It’s not to the battleforce angel’s benefit to land; as far as possible, it always remains airborne.

The battleforce angel has Magic Resistance, so it doesn’t have a lot to fear from spellcasters. But it does know that its troops, by and large, don’t have that resistance, so it’s cognizant of the threat that spellcasters pose and will help its forces neutralize them forthwith. With Intelligence of only 11, a battleforce angel is unsophisticated in its tactics and largely lacking in strategy, which is why I’d increase that Intelligence to at least 14, so that it can pick out strategic targets and accurately assess its enemies’ weaknesses. Either way, however, its exceptional Wisdom means it chooses its battles carefully and doesn’t lead its forces into fights without excellent chances of victory. That means that if they’re picking a fight with your player characters, the encounter must be Deadly (under 2014 encounter building guidelines—see DMG14 chapter 3) or High Difficulty (under 2024 guidelines—see DMG24 chapter 4).

A true believer, the battleforce angel fights to the bitter end. Even if it’s seriously wounded (reduced to 26 hp or fewer), it may order its troops to withdraw, remaining behind to cover their withdrawal, but it doesn’t withdraw. Then again, depending on the nature of the enemy, it may order its forces to fight to the death, too. That’s especially likely against Rakdos cultists, and possibly against Dimir and Golgari forces as well.

The firemane angel stands outside the regular chain of command. It’s a solitary champion, sent to confront unique solo adversaries too strong for ordinary troops to handle—like, say, Thanos, who can knock a whole company flat with a wave of his hand.

With extraordinary Charisma and Strength and very high Constitution, the firemane angel seeks close encounters of the martial kind, but it uses its magic to give itself an edge first. Specifically, it uses its blazing 120-foot flying speed to get within 30 feet of its foe, then uses its bonus action to cast compelled duel, so that the target can’t hurt anyone else. Since compelled duel is a bonus action spell, the firemane angel can Dash if necessary to get close enough to its foe to cast it. It doesn’t have to attack the same turn, as long as it can prevent its enemy from attacking anyone else. But if it can, the firemane angel gets within 5 feet of that enemy so that it can’t withdraw without provoking an opportunity attack. The firemane angel’s spell save DC is 18, which is excellent but far from unbeatable for the kinds of enemies it’s going to confront, so it wants to have a foe’s full attention whether its saving throw succeeds or fails.

When its foe is ensnared by compelled duel, the firemane angel uses its movement to jerk it around. After Multiattacking (it always makes its two Longsword attacks with two hands, since it doesn’t carry a shield), it withdraws 30 feet into the air—no farther, because withdrawing any farther ends the spell—away from its own forces. Its dueling companion, if it can’t fly, is in a bind: It can’t move away without succeeding on a Wisdom saving throw, but even if it can move toward the firemane angel, it may not be able to reach it with an attack. To attack anyone else, it has to get to them first, which means succeeding on a Wisdom save to pull away, and it still has disadvantage on any such attack. And the firemane angel’s flying speed means it can easily pursue its foe and rope it back in.

Ironically, considering that Charisma is its primary offensive ability, the situations in which the firemane angel’s other spells make sense are few. It can cast fireball at 6th level, but the chief value of that spell lies not in its damage but in its ability to deal that damage to several foes at once. Also, casting it at anyone other than its designated foe breaks compelled duel. The time to cast this spell is when a number of weaker minions of the firemane angel’s primary enemy are trying to come to its aid. (“I strongly suggest you reconsider.” *fwoom*) Fortunately, since the firemane angel gets three uses of compelled duel per day, it can try to reestablish the special relationship on its next turn after casting fireball to deter backup—not on the same turn, alas, since they’re both leveled spells.

Daylight doesn’t conjure sunlight, so its only real use case is neutralizing an area of magical darkness. And guiding bolt, even as a 5th-level spell, doesn’t deal enough damage to compete with the firemane angel’s melee Multiattack; it’s suitable only when the firemane angel can’t get close enough to swing its sword at a target.

A firemane angel isn’t a tremendously flexible fighter, beyond the above, and it’s not uniquely good at picking out its opponents’ weaknesses; by default, it deals radiant rather than fire damage with Longsword, but that’s just playing the odds, since more creatures are resistant to fire than to radiant damage. The likelihood that a creature is resistant or immune to radiant damage but not fire damage is remote, and when that turns out to be the case, it generally catches the firemane angel by surprise. (However, if it receives intelligence through a plausible information vector that tells it about a foe’s radiant damage resistance in advance, it arrives prepared.)

That said, like battleforce angels, firemane angels do know better than to initiate a losing battle. If firemane angel itself isn’t a Deadly or High Difficulty encounter for the opposing side, it brings enough troops along to make up the difference. A firemane angel is absolutely a zealot, and it fights to the death no matter whom or what it’s fighting.

Next: guardian giants.

title: Boros NPC Tactics
content_text:
The Boros Legion consists of zealous warriors devoted to “justice, not merely law enforcement” (emphasis mine), meaning that they’re not going to concern themselves with victimless crimes—and they’re also not going to let themselves be hemmed in by legal constraints when dealing with the real bad ’uns. They’re basically good, and they’re basically lawful … but if there’s a conflict between them, “good” takes precedence over “lawful” every time.

Thus, the Boros soldier, while their stats are identical to those of an Azorius soldier, functions slightly differently. They still carry shields and wield their swords one-handed. They still move in close ranks to take advantage of Formation Tactics. However, they don’t always attack to subdue rather than kill. If they’re merely helping to apprehend someone deemed to have broken the law by the Azorius Senate or the Selesnya Conclave, then sure, Boros will deliver them to the defendant’s box in one piece (in some cases eagerly, in others begrudgingly). But if they’re independently pursuing a foe they consider an incorrigible threat to justice and wholesomeness, one that in their eyes has already established its own guilt in plain sight of everybody—perhaps one affiliated with House Dimir, the Golgari Swarm or, especially, the Cult of Rakdos—they don’t hold back.

They’re also motivated by a common fervor, so while Azorius troops are leery of tolerating losses, Boros troops will fight on. Depending on the nature and degree of the threat their enemies pose, Boros soldiers may be willing to fight to the death, or at least until they’ve lost half their number or everyone in their unit has suffered serious injuries. An Azorius commander might deem it wiser to withdraw to fight another day, but a Boros commander is more likely to declare, “The line must be drawn here!”

Soldiers in smaller Boros patrols may be accompanied by mastiffs, which allow the soldiers to split up and take advantage of their Formation Tactics trait without being shoulder-to-shoulder with their fellows. They may also be accompanied by frontline medics, soldiers who also possess some divine spellcasting ability. The word “frontline” in the name is significant. These warriors don’t hang back behind the front line, ready to run up and cast healing spells as needed. They’re right up front with everyone else, and when they’re not healing, they’re stabbing. In fact, they’re stronger, tougher and better armored than the average soldier; they just don’t put out as much damage.

Frontline medics—there’s generally one for every six soldiers in their unit—are there to keep their buddies in the fight. How they go about that depends on what kind of intelligence they have on their opponents. If they know that their enemies are capable of imposing the blinded, deafened, paralyzed or poisoned condition, they hold back on using their 2nd-level spell slots in case they need to use those slots to cast lesser restoration. If their intelligence tells them that their enemies don’t have the power to impose any of these conditions but are especially dangerous—for instance, if a fight against them would constitute a Hard or Deadly encounter under 2014 encounter building rules (see Dungeon Master’s Guide chapter 3) or a High Difficulty encounter under 2024 rules (see Dungeon Master’s Guide chapter 4)—they use both their 2nd-level slots to cast aid on the soldiers in their unit before the encounter begins, so that they all enter the fight with 5 hp extra.

(If you’re playing under 5E24 rules, the standard way to convert this stat block would be to give the frontline medic one use per day of aid and one of lesser restoration, but this is an inelegant half-measure that doesn’t suit the medic’s role. What’s the point of being able to cast aid on only half your unit, or to save one blinded or paralyzed comrade but not a second one? Conversely, does it make sense to have one medical specialist for every three grunts rather than every six? Reader, I submit that it does not. Rather than make both aid and lesser restoration 1/day spells, choose one or the other in advance and let the medic cast it twice.)

As for their 1st-level slots, they don’t cast sanctuary on their troops, because the troops need to be able to fight. Instead, they cast it on hostages, bystanders and any other noncombatant threatened by their enemy. Otherwise, they spend all their 1st-level slots on cure wounds, using it to top up soldiers who fall to half their hit point maximum. (Under 5E24 rules, there’s no point in casting cure wounds on a soldier until they’re seriously wounded, i.e., at 6 hp or fewer; any sooner, and all that extra healing is wasted. It seems absurd to let the frontline medic cast cure wounds at will, but it seems equally absurd to limit them to just 2 uses per day. Unfortunately, 5E24 monster design requires us to choose one or the other. I say make it at-will. The medic can cast it only once per turn; that’s enough of a limitation.)

Spare the dying is for PCs and NPCs you’ve decided to let make death saving throws rather than die immediately upon reaching 0 hp. Resistance allows one target to get a 1d4 bonus to one saving throw under 5E14 rules and to subtract 1d4 from a single type of elemental or mystical damage under 5E24 rules; either way, I think this spell, like sanctuary, is more for special noncombatants than it is for other Boros troops.

The frontline medic is a fighter for Boros and thus continues to fight for as long as Boros soldiers do.

A company of 50 to 250 Boros soldiers is commanded by a knight or veteran captain. There should be only one of these per company. Everyday neighborhood-level threats are dealt with by these troops alone, as are purely mundane threats that span whole districts. But more unusual threats at the district level, especially arcane threats, may call for higher-level intervention.

That’s where reckoners come in. There’s a large jump in power level from a soldier to a reckoner, and a single reckoner may suffice to deal with a threat that’s beyond the power of several soldier patrols to handle. The reckoner is a front-line brute, with very high Strength and high Constitution, but it also has high Intelligence and can cast wizard spells. It has proficiency in Perception and expertise in Arcana, equipping it to observe and assess magical threats, and the First Strike trait, which lets it get the jump on its opponents.

Lightning Backlash is a reaction to an incoming attack, with a 5–6 recharge. This recharge usually indicates an ability that’s preferable in all instances to a creature’s main attack, but I’d make an exception in this instance. The reckoner isn’t going to waste this ability on a threat it considers insignificant or one it can do other things to. Since they’re a front-line melee combatant by nature, with a Longsword attack of their own, I’m going to say they reserve Lightning Backlash for two specific instances: when they’re hit by a ranged attack, and when an incoming melee attack deals so much damage that halving it is still better than taking a swing with their sword.

Lightning bolt, of course, requires one’s opponents to be lined up just so, although saying the reckoner should cast it only when they can hit at least four foes with it (per Targets in Areas of Effect, DMG14 chapter 8) seems a bit extreme, since they have nothing else to spend their 3rd-level slots on. Even if they were to hit only one opponent with it, it would still inflict more damage than they could deal with anything else they’ve got, even a thunderwave against three opponents.

The reckoner’s 2nd-level spells are much less impressive. Blur takes an action to cast, making it a subpar choice once combat is under way, while levitate, a.k.a. attract arrows, lifts the reckoner up out of melee when melee is exactly where they would like to be. Meanwhile, at 1st level, they have guiding bolt and witch bolt, both ranged attacks; shield, a solid reaction spell for use while Lightning Backlash is on cooldown; and thunderwave, the first really practical melee-oriented spell we’ve seen in the reckoner’s toolkit. Among their cantrips, blade ward is pretty useless because of its opportunity cost, but shocking grasp deals 2d8 lightning damage thanks to the reckoner’s spellcaster level, making it slightly superior to Longsword, and also grants advantage on the attack roll if the reckoner’s target is wearing metal armor, which they very often will be.

So what do we make of this weirdo with a natural predilection for melee but a toolbox that’s so biased toward attacking at range? Interestingly, I think there’s a clue in their skill proficiencies. Look, right there. Intimidation +2. The reckoner is scary. You see them coming, and you want to get away. They want to get closer. But if you don’t, that’s OK. They have ways of hurting you anyway.

First Strike moves the reckoner up in the initiative order, and the first thing they do is advance. They don’t wait until you’re within their melee reach to make their first attack: They begin with guiding bolt, boosted to 2nd level because, seriously, you’re not going to waste either of those slots on blur or levitate, are you? The reckoner already has an exceptionally high Armor Class for their Challenge Rating and wants to move forward, not upward. But as soon as their target is within reach, they switch—not to Longsword, but to shocking grasp, for the slightly greater damage and, possibly, advantage on the attack roll. They cast lightning bolt if ever the stars (or rather, the opponents) align, but mostly they stick with shocking grasp. Thunderwave is for clearing out pesky brawlers—at least three of them—who stand between the reckoner and someone else they’re trying to reach. Shield is for use while waiting for Lightning Backlash to recharge, and witch bolt never quite measures up to guiding bolt, even when it’s boosted to 2nd level, because its range is much shorter, and only the first round’s damage is increased by the higher spell slot.

Now, all of this presumes you’re playing 5E14. If we bring a 5E24 design sensibility to the reckoner, we might be inspired to make some changes, viz.:

Lightning Sword. Melee Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 8 (1d10 + 3) Slashing damage plus 9 (2d8) Lightning damage, and the target can’t take reactions until the start of its next turn.

Presto: Shocking grasp and Longsword combined into a single attack that deals more CR-appropriate damage. (We can assume the two-handed sword damage, since the reckoner doesn’t carry a shield. Shocking grasp doesn’t gain advantage against metal armor in 5E24, and it shuts down only opportunity attacks, not all reactions—but we’ll keep this last effect, because the reckoner’s foes are going to be trying to get out of the reckoner’s reach, not the other way around.)

As for the reckoner’s spell repertoire, we might simplify it thus:

At will: Blade Ward, Light, Message, Thunderwave2/day: Blur, Levitate [maybe, just in case, but you could easily leave these out]1/day: Lightning Bolt

Shield would be treated as a reaction, in a manner similar to many stat blocks in the 2025 Monster Manual, and guiding bolt would be converted into a ranged attack, with the upcasting damage bonus already figured in:

Radiant Bolt. Ranged Attack: +5 to hit, range 120 ft., one creature. Hit: 17 (5d6) Radiant damage, and the next attack roll made against it before the end of your next turn has Advantage.

A seriously wounded reckoner (reduced to 20 hp or fewer) may pause to talk, but since their only social skill is in Intimidation, that talk will consist largely of warning about the wrath their foes are going to bring down upon themselves and how they’d better repent before it’s too late. And that’s a pause in hostilities, not a cessation: If and when their foes fail to say, “Aw, man, you’re right, I do need to reevaluate my life choices,” they go right on fighting until they fall.

The greater the threat to a district (or to Boros’s interests within it), the more reckoners will be called upon to deal with it. But once you’re up to sending out three reckoners at once, it might be time to send in the crème de la crème of Boros military spellcasters, the firefist. This one’s a divine rather than arcane spellcaster, and despite wielding a greatsword, they prefer to deal with their problems using magic first.

Their spell repertoire is a greatest-hits collection of divine wrath:

Flame strike brings heavenly combustion down upon at least two foes standing within 10 feet of its center point, dealing an average of 14 fire damage and 14 radiant damage—28 damage in total—to each.

Banishment sends inconvenient enemies away temporarily and inconvenient extraplanar enemies away permanently.

Wall of fire cuts off escape. (The firefist doesn’t have immunity or even resistance to fire damage, so the ring-of-fire trick used by efreets and pit fiends doesn’t work for them.)

Blinding smite is a bonus action spell that enhances the firefist’s next Greatsword attack, adding an average of 13 radiant damage and potentially imposing the blinded condition. Used at the start of the firefist’s turn just before a Multiattack, it can result in advantage on the second of their two attacks and potentially on subsequent attacks as well. It does, however, require concentration, precluding the casting of banishment, wall of fire, crusader’s mantle, heroism and shield of faith—and precluding the casting of this spell if the firefist wants to sustain any of those others.

Crusader’s mantle is useful if and only if the firefist has six or more allies willing to stay within 30 feet. That can greatly enhance the effectiveness of, say, a small unit of Boros soldiers, but it’s not going to do much if the firefist shows up accompanied only by a pair of reckoners.

Revivify is for protecting important NPCs (or PCs, if the firefist is on their side). A nearby frontline medic can slap a sanctuary spell on the revivified character to make sure the job isn’t undone immediately.

Lesser restoration is discussed above.

Scorching ray deals an average of 7 fire damage to each of three targets, or 21 in total. If boosted to 3rd level, it deals this damage to four targets (28 total damage), and if boosted to 4th level, it deals this damage to five (35 total damage). But the firefist’s 3rd- and 4th-level spells are useful enough that the firefist shouldn’t assume those slots are available for this purpose.

Guiding bolt deals an average of 14 radiant damage and grants advantage on the next attack roll against the target.

Healing word is useful only because it’s cast as a bonus action; otherwise, its opportunity cost would make it a laughable inclusion. Even so, the amount of healing it deals is small, even under 5E24 rules, and it’s by no means certain that it’s worth the cost of a spell slot that the firefist could spend on guiding bolt instead. That equation changes a bit, however, when the firefist is embroiled in melee and any guiding bolt attack roll would have to be made with disadvantage.

Heroism is worth the spell slot, it’s worth the action, but it’s not worth the firefist’s concentration. There’s too much else they could be doing with that.

Shield of faith isn’t even worth the action. All these Boros guys are wearing heavy armor already. What difference is another +2 going to make? OK, so it’s a bonus action, not an action. Precluding any other concentration-required spell is still a problem, as is the fact that the firefist can’t cast any other leveled spell on the same turn. Are you sure the firefist isn’t going to want to cast banishment or wall of fire? Then shield of faith might be worth it. Otherwise, meh.

Fire bolt and sacred flame are for when the firefist has run out of spell slots altogether. That’s not happening today.

Spare the dying is the same as it is for the frontline medic.

The firefist’s Multiattack grants two Greatsword attacks, which together deal a total of 20 damage on average. Thus, in descending order of average potential damage, we have flame strike, scorching ray (4th), blinding smite plus Multiattack, scorching ray (3rd), scorching ray (2nd), Multiattack alone and guiding bolt. I think we can conclude from this that the firefist casts guiding bolt only when it can’t get close enough to a foe to make melee attacks and the foe has also demonstrated resistance or immunity to fire damage.

The firefist also has a reaction it can use once per encounter, Guided Attack, which adds +10 to a single attack roll—its own or that of an ally it can see within 30 feet. Because it gets only one shot, that attack roll has to be important. Boros being Boros, you’re not likely to see it conferred on a rogue making a Sneak Attack. You might, however, see it conferred on a ranger who’s trying to land a lightning arrow or a shot with hunter’s mark or ensnaring strike, or a paladin who’s just cast a smite spell.

Under 5E24 rules, here’s a plausible spell use distribution:

At will: Healing Word, Lesser Restoration, Light, Revivify, Spare the Dying2/day: Blinding Smite, Scorching Ray1/day: Banishment, Flame Strike, Wall of Fire

Like other Boros NPCs, the firefist is willing to fight to the death, but they’ll pause to monologue when seriously wounded (reduced to 46 hp or fewer).

Next: air support.

title: Felidar and Archon of the Triumvirate Tactics
content_text:
I would love for the felidar to be more interesting than it is. Unfortunately, from a tactical point of view, it’s just a big celestial lion. Its Pounce trait gives it an incentive to move 20 feet straight toward an opponent before making its first attack, which on a hit knocks the opponent prone on a failed Strength saving throw and grants an extra claw attack as a bonus action. Since the felidar has a 40-foot move and a fairly high Armor Class, it has an incentive to permit an opportunity attack in order to move 20 feet away, then come back and attack with Pounce again. Worst-case scenario, it takes an OA hit and gains nothing, but best-case scenario, it knocks the foe prone and gets to make one Claws attack normally and a follow-up Claws and Bite, both with advantage.

Bonding is a ribbon trait that does nothing for the felidar once initiative has been rolled, while Keen Hearing and Sight helps the felidar and any ally or allies it has from being surprised. A sidebar notes, “Some felidars boast huge, feathered wings,” giving them a flying speed equal to their land speed, which simply means that they pounce from above rather than ahead. That’s pretty much it.

So let’s move on to the archon of the Triumvirate, a likely partner of a felidar according to the latter’s flavor text. Despite its high Challenge Rating, the archon plays a support role first and foremost: Although it has extraordinary Strength, its Wisdom is even greater. For defense, it relies on its superhuman Constitution. Its other three stats are all pretty wowzers as well.

I’ll focus first on the archon’s Wisdom-powered features, the most interesting of which is Pacifying Presence. This ability, with a 120-foot range, forces all targets the archon chooses to make a Wisdom save; on a failure, targets are not only charmed for up to 1 minute but drop their weapons, along with any sustained spells they’re concentrating on. DC 18 is high but not insurmountable, and affected targets get to repeat their saves at the end of each turn, so you can figure that player characters with proficiency in Wisdom saves—which is to say, five classes out of 12—will be affected for a round or two at most. The archon and its allies have to take advantage of the situation quickly.

The legendary action Detention is banishment reskinned, but lasting only until the end of the archon’s next turn and not requiring concentration. I suppose that last item is the reason why it costs three whole legendary actions, but the timing is such that, at best, Detention will take its target out of the fight for a single turn, after which they’ll come back mad. And that’s assuming they fail their save! That’s a big cost and a big risk for not a lot of payoff.

Speaking of spells, the archon can also cast calm emotions, command and compelled duel at will. Calm emotions and compelled duel both require concentration.

Calm emotions affects creatures in a 20-foot-radius sphere, which can be expected (per Targets in Area[s] of Effect in 2014 Dungeon Master’s Guide chapter 8 and 2024 Dungeon Master’s Guide chapter 3) to affect four targets under 2014 rules and 10 targets under 2024 rules. These numbers are the minimum necessary for the spell to be worth casting. If the archon is trying to wipe the targets’ minds clean of effects that are causing them to be charmed or frightened, that’s an easy call. But if it’s trying to turn hostile creatures indifferent—say, to pacify an angry mob—it lasts only until “the target is attacked or harmed by a spell or if it witnesses any of its friends being harmed.” “Harmed” is somewhat subjective, but if someone is being taken into custody, I think it’s fair to say that the arrest would have to be conducted very gently and respectfully for the arrestee’s friends not to construe that as harm. If it involved, say, an attempt to grapple, I don’t think calm emotions would continue to hold.

Command works on only one creature at once, so its primary use is to prevent a single fugitive from fleeing. Halt, Approach and Grovel might be used on three subsequent turns to prepare a wanted individual for a perp walk. But having the archon cast this spell is kind of like killing a fly with a sledgehammer. It could be doing so much more.

Compelled duel is cinematically cool but somewhat out of character for the archon. Remember, despite its might, the archon primarily plays a support role. Compelled duel is a main-character kind of spell, one used chiefly by front-line brutes. If the archon casts it, it’s basically saying to its allies, “Enough, you numbskulls. I’m taking over this operation myself.”
