Eye of the Law is a ribbon trait, but a narratively useful one: It allows the archon to look at a suspect and know exactly what crimes to charge it with, at least as far as the last 24 hours are concerned. There’s no limit on the use of this trait, and it’s a bonus action, so the archon can roam around, casting its glance at a different individual every 6 seconds, and identify anyone who’s been up to no good since the same time yesterday. It’s like being able to review 4,800 omniscient, up-to-the-minute dossiers in a single 8-hour shift. The good news for lawbreakers is that in a crowd of, say, 150 people in a market square, it will take the archon 15 minutes to look at everybody, which is plenty of time to find someplace else to be.

The archon’s Multiattack lets it make two attacks with its gloriously named Hammer of Justice, each of which packs a CR 14–appropriate wallop of 30 damage per hit and can knock a target prone as well. Although the archon is lawful neutral, not lawful good, it will generally choose to make its finishing blow nonlethal, so that the foe can be hauled before a tribunal to face the judgment of the law. Smite lets the archon spend two of its legendary actions to make a third Hammer of Justice attack, which is necessary for the archon to fulfill the full offensive potential of its CR.

And then there’s Mount. This trait lets the archon use its bonus action to teleport onto the back of its steed and grants it immunity from surprise and advantage on Dexterity saves. It also stipulates that when the archon is reduced to 0 hp while mounted, the mount is, too. By necessity, then, an archon of the Triumvirate has to have an extraordinarily robust mount: If it were riding, say, a griffon, anyone attacking the archon would be wise to target the mount first, because it would have significantly fewer hit points than the archon. A felidar, however, has 93 hp, and those are going to be time-consuming enough to take away that the archon’s foes may as well go ahead and target the archon, the greater threat. Rejoin Mount lets the archon zop to its mount’s back at the end of someone else’s turn if it has to use its bonus action for something else on its own turn, while Smite includes a bonus attack by the mount.

Archons of the Triumvirate “are commonly seen circling above the Azorius guildhall astride their winged felidar mounts, soaring alongside griffon-mounted hussars,” the flavor text declaims. So let’s imagine a company of 50 Azorius soldiers, supported by 15 soldiers mounted on griffons and an archon on a winged felidar, hunting a known threat to the city (or to the Azorius Senate). Initially, before battle is joined, the archon is performing a reconnaissance role, using Eye of the Law to scan the crowd below for malfeasors, while the footsoldiers march through the streets below.

When the archon spies the enemy, it’s time for some crowd control. First, the footsoldiers make their way through the crowd toward the target of their search, while the griffon hussars spread out and cover the exits, preparing to intercept their security threat should they attempt to escape. Once they’re in position, the archon descends and uses Pacifying Presence upon everyone within its line of sight. At an altitude of 15 feet—just 10 feet over most citizens’ heads, plus or minus a foot—the archon’s altitude isn’t going to make a significant dent in the feature’s 120-foot radius, and if the archon wants to cover a smaller area, such as the 50-by-70-foot market square mentioned above, it can do so from a higher altitude, as much as 95 feet up. Eighty-five percent of the crowd will end up charmed and drop whatever they’re holding, another 10 percent is mostly going to either run or freeze in place, and only the last 5 percent will even think about causing any sort of trouble—assuming they aren’t already Azorius-friendly. Any instigators are most likely Rakdos cultists or Gruul warriors.

One false move on the part of its quarry, and the archon can use Detention to try to yoink them out of reality for a moment, giving its footsoldiers time to surround the spot where the threat was and will return to presently. But chances are, Azorius has gathered some intel on its target, and if they’re a cleric or a charisma caster, or some NPC or other beastie with proficiency in Charisma saves, the archon will know that Detention’s chances of success are poor and won’t waste its legendary actions on the attempt. It might choose instead to cast calm emotions on those Rakdos and Gruul troublemakers or command on the threat, ordering them to halt.

Of course, all this assumes that Azorius is on the hunt for just one individual. If it’s after an entire group, there’s a good chance that even a whole company of troops might be unable to stand up to them. In this situation, the archon is more likely to involve itself directly, taking on the most recalcitrant opponents with its Hammer of Justice and, if necessary, compelled duel. But this is only likely when it’s abundantly clear that Azorius’s other troops are overmatched, and that even with Pacifying Presence, they’re not going to be able to keep all their enemies from getting away (or worse, wiping them out) without the archon’s direct intervention. If it still seems like the troops can handle the physical aspects of the operation, the archon confines itself to casting command. (There’s no value in using Pacifying Presence more than once, since a successful save confers immunity to it—unless somebody new arrives on the scene.)

The archon of the triumvirate is a zealot and won’t retreat no matter how much damage it takes. However, it’s also in league with the Azorius Senate, which has an interest in not throwing away resources, including human resources. If 10 percent or more of the guild’s troops are slain, the archon orders the surviving troops to disengage and withdraw. The archon and the griffon hussars delay pursuit while the footsoldiers retreat, then the archon covers the retreat of the hussars. The archon itself is the last to leave.

Next: the Boros Legion.

title: Azorius NPC Tactics
content_text:
The Guildmasters’ Guide to Ravnica (I’ve been miswriting “Guildmaster” in the title as singular; it’s actually plural) is rich in monster and nonplayer character stat blocks. It’s also divided up by guild, making it unreasonable to go through them by creature type as I usually do these days. Instead, I’m going to look at them guild by guild and consider their tactics not just individually but also in combinations in which they might appear, at tier 1 (first missions and neighborhood-level conflicts), tier 2 (strife engulfing whole districts) and tier 3 (struggles for dominance over the entire known world). As a self-contained setting, Ravnica doesn’t lend itself naturally to the interplanar lunacy of tier 4 play.

Ravnica’s 10 guilds are the Azorius Senate (the formal government of Ravnica), the Boros Legion (zealous warriors devoted to “justice, not merely law enforcement”—emphasis mine), House Dimir (spies and shadowy information brokers), the Golgari Swarm (the decomposers in the world-city’s food chain), the Gruul Clans (a confederation of folks who aren’t on board with the whole “civilization” concept), the Izzet League (buncha mad technomancers), the Orzhov Syndicate (loan sharks in ecclesiastical clothing), the Cult of Rakdos (dionysian nutballs with a wide destructive streak), the Selesnya Conclave (environmentalists seeking “to bring nature and the city into balance,” so basically the Ravnica Park District) and the Simic Combine (another buncha mad technomancers, but taking biology instead of chem-phys).

I’ll start off with the Azorius Senate, which is both first alphabetically and the guild I’m most drawn to personally.

The lowest-level Azorius stat block is the soldier, but this stat block isn’t specific to Azorius: Boros and Selesnya make use of it as well. Low-level PCs butting heads with government forces are likely to encounter only groups of soldiers on foot, perhaps using owls as messengers. Mid-level PCs might encounter squads of soldiers backing up a higher-level agent sent out to arrest a wanted lawbreaker, or troops of “hussars”—soldiers mounted on warhorses or griffons, with giant eagles providing reconnaissance and air support. By tier 3, soldiers will appear in companies of 50 to 250, commanded by veteran captains.

Soldier are balanced combatants, above average in all physical abilities but remarkable in none of them. Since they carry shields, they wield their longswords one-handed. They move in tight formation, shoulder-to-shoulder, to take advantage of their Formation Tactics trait. In most instances, they attack to knock out rather than kill, with the aim of hauling their opponents before a tribunal, but their attitude changes quickly when one of their own is killed.

A warhorse or griffon ridden by a soldier functions as an independent mount (see “Mounted Combat,” 2014 Player’s Handbook chapter 8 or 2024 Player’s Handbook chapter 1) so that it can add its own attacks to its rider’s. When hussars on horseback roll higher initiative than their mounts, they Ready their first-round Multiattack actions so that they take their turns immediately afterward, giving their mounts a chance to knock their foes prone with Trampling Charge. Griffons have no comparable ability, so griffon hussars don’t have to bother. Hussars can feel free to spread out and pursue fleeing or backline foes: Their own mounts count as allies for the purpose of Formation Tactics.

Soldiers are more cautious about taking losses than you might think. It takes only one soldier death for a squad to disengage and withdraw from a combat encounter, while a company withdraws once it loses 10 percent or more of its forces. The goal during withdrawal may be to draw the enemy forward to a position more favorable to the soldiers, but most of the time it’s simply to reach a safer, better-defended position, e.g., a fortress or barracks, where there are many more soldiers to back them up. Soldiers will also withdraw when all the soldiers in their unit are seriously wounded (reduced to 6 hp or fewer).

Precognitive mages are “typically employed to anticipate the actions of wanted criminals, thus aiding in their capture,” and they do their best work far from any combat encounter. After obtaining a signed judicial warrant (cough), they patrol a neighborhood or quarter, accompanied by a soldier escort, casting locate object to find stolen items, contraband, clothing that matches scraps left behind at crime scenes and other incriminating stuff. When the spell gets a ping, the precog caster takes careful note of the location, then finds a comfortable spot within 1 mile, settles in and casts clairvoyance to surveil it. They can cast each of these spells only once per day, so if one day’s casting doesn’t turn up useful information, they try again at a different time the next day, then the next day, then the next. They’ll magically snoop on a specific location for about a week before giving up and pursuing some other lead.

If their snooping does reveal suspicious behavior, a precog mage armors up and takes a soldier patrol to the observed location to make an arrest (they have to be present to provide positive identification of the suspect). Based on their exceptional Intelligence and above-average Dexterity and Wisdom, they let the soldiers manhandle the suspect while hanging back 30 feet. They then cast detect thoughts and conduct a psychic interrogation to learn as much as they can from the suspect’s thoughts.

If the suspect fights back or tries to make a break for it, the mage first uses their Precognitive Insight reaction to hinder the attempt, then unleashes Glimpse the Temporal Flood, an awesomely named recharge ability that socks the target with a psionic haymaker, dealing psychic damage and potentially stunning them long enough for the Azorius soldiers to bring them under control. A nasty twist is that by the time the precog uses Glimpse, they have their reaction back and can use Precognitive Insight again to nerf the target’s Intelligence saving throw against being stunned.

The precog’s Quarterstaff hits like a wet noodle; it’s hardly worth using even in self-defense. Consequently, if they get blitzed, they use Precognitive Insight to impose disadvantage on the incoming attack, but they don’t bother to use it to give advantage to any counterattack of their own. Instead, they use Glimpse against their assailant if it’s available; if it’s not, they Disengage and back away, calling on their allies for aid.

The precog calls the shots for the squad, and if they’re even moderately wounded (reduced to 44 hp or fewer) or if one of their soldiers falls, they signal a withdrawal. This job is going to require a special tactical unit.

Maybe one that includes a lawmage, a wizard with very high Intelligence but also high Constitution, so they’re willing to be more hands-on. It’s been a while since I’ve looked at an old-fashioned caster stat block that uses spell slots, so I appreciate your patience as I break it down:

Locate creature is how the lawmage tracks down known suspects.

Stoneskin is a trap option. It may seem like an upgrade to mage armor, but it requires concentration, and when the lawmage is in combat, they’ll need that concentration for other things, like slow, hold person, detect thoughts and possibly expeditious retreat.

Clairvoyance is useful for surveillance of a specific location, but once the lawmage knows whom they’re looking for, it’s time to switch to locate creature.

Dispel magic is worth boosting to 4th level in order to extinguish sustained spells such as confusion, greater invisibility, guardian of nature, shadow of moil and wall of fire, since the lawmage won’t be using that second 4th-level slot for stoneskin. For that matter, it can be used to dispel someone else’s stoneskin.

Slow is handy for thwarting pursuit.

Arcane lock can be slapped on the lock of a prison cell (purpose-built or makeshift) to make it even harder to escape from. (Rather than just slap an extra 10 points onto the Difficulty Class, give some thought to how the spell makes picking the lock more difficult, because you can have a lot of fun with it. It might resist the insertion of lockpicks, provide false tactile and auditory feedback, create “smart wards” that anticipate the lockpicks’ movements and block them, or cause the keyhole to clench shut, twist away or even try to bite a lockpicker’s fingers. To me, the most Azorius effect would be to issue a distracting, droning lecture about the illegality of escaping confinement after a lawful arrest.)

Detect thoughts can be used for interrogation, as above.

Hold person is one of the most useful spells a cop can cast, and since there’s that spare 4th-level spell slot that’s not going to be used for stoneskin, upcasting it to paralyze three targets rather than just one is almost a no-brainer.

Alarm can be used to secure a perimeter, but securing a large perimeter will require casting it many times.

Expeditious retreat requires concentration, as do many of the offensively useful spells above. The lawmage only switches to expeditious retreat when their side is losing.

Shield, bluntly, is going to eat up the lion’s share of the lawmage’s 1st-level spell slots.

Fire bolt is unquestionably superior to the lawmage’s Quarterstaff attack at any distance greater than 5 feet, since it’s more likely to hit and deals an average of 11 fire damage vs. the Quarterstaff’s 4 bludgeoning. (The lawmage is an 8th-level spellcaster, so the cantrip deals 2d10 damage, not 1d10.)

Friends is for asking, “Did you happen to see a [description of suspect] pass by here?”

Light gives the lawmage’s quarterstaff something useful to do.

Message is for coordinating a breach.

If you’re playing 5E24, I’d edit the Spellcasting ability thusly (turning fire bolt into a ranged attack and shield into a reaction):

At will: detect thoughts, dispel magic, expeditious retreat, friends, light, message2/day: alarm, arcane lock1/day: clairvoyance, hold person (4th-level version), locate creature, slow

I might even leave off expeditious retreat.

Unlike the precog, who hangs back 30 feet behind their escort, the lawmage stands just a pace or two behind and doesn’t flee from melee. They’re still better off casting fire bolt than trying to whack someone with their staff, but if they’re rushed, a lawmage stays cool and casts hold person on their assailant with a regular ol’ 2nd-level spell slot, allowing them to deal critical damage on subsequent hits with their staff against the paralyzed target. (About two-thirds of the time, anyway: A spell save DC of 14 is solid but not infallible.) Mostly, though, the lawmage uses locate creature to find perps, then hold person and slow to make them easy for Azorius soldiers to apprehend.

When the situation calls for the involvement of a lawmage, the Azorius Senate doesn’t mess around. If one lawmage, accompanied by, say, 20 soldiers and a veteran captain, isn’t enough to make the encounter Deadly (5E14) or High Difficulty (5E24), the Azorius Senate sends two lawmages, and if two aren’t enough, it sends three. Such jobs are also of the highest priority, so being moderately wounded isn’t enough to deter a lawmage from fighting on. They have to be seriously wounded (reduced to 33 hp or fewer) or deprived of at least half their forces to make them withdraw, and if they have reason to believe that their opponents respect the laws of war, they’ll surrender rather than risk being cut down while fleeing.

Next: felidars.

title: The Monsters Have Gained Some Clarity
content_text:
Hello again! I’d wish you all a happy 2026, except that it seems more like we didn’t do a good enough job of making sure 2025 was properly dead and buried. Get [whack!] back [whack!] in [whack!] there [whack!]

I want to start off by thanking you all for understanding the assignment and leaving your comments about which direction I should take The Monsters Know What They’re Doing in going forward. The leading suggestion was doing more with original monster design, which I have to admit is a tempting option. But there were also two votes for “more of the same, please,” and if I add those to the votes for other sourcebooks for Dungeons & Dragons fifth edition, 2014 version, then The Guildmaster’s Guide to Ravnica and The Explorer’s Guide to Wildemount move into the lead, along with Flee, Mortals! by MCDM. Examining these books seems like the path of least resistance, especially since I own Ravnica already. The Book of Many Things was close behind—it might even have been tied with the aforementioned titles, but one of the possible votes for that book was somewhat ambiguous—and who knows, by the time I get through all of these, maybe we’ll have a second printing of Bigby Presents: Glory of the Giants after all. Probably not. But maybe.

Also in contention is the concept of encounter design. I like this idea, too, but it doesn’t exactly sync up with the name of the blog. Still, I appreciate the suggestion, and maybe I’ll incorporate it into One Foot in Fairyland, which I need to send out more often anyway.

As for r/dndnext subredditors’ reactions to my previous post, they ranged from concurrence with my opinions to reasonable differences of opinion and/or taste to nonsense I won’t dignify here with a response … lots of it. For years, Yoast has warned me that I’m not writing at a sixth-grade level like a blogger ought to, and now I can see why. Let’s just say that the munchkins are very happy that the 2024 revision of 5E is catering to their tastes, would like it to do so even more, and neither understand nor appreciate why changes in that direction might not be to everyone else’s liking.

Eh. Whatever. I’ve always known that my tastes are emphatically not the tastes of the D&D-playing community as a whole—and yet my books sell anyway, which has always struck me as something of a minor miracle for which I’ll always be grateful. Thanks for sticking with me, and I’ll see you next week with my first entry into Ravnica.

title: The Monsters Are Unsure What to Do Next
content_text:
At the end of 2025—the latest of several in a string of disorienting years that have included, among other things, the destruction of Twitter, the OGL kerfuffle, the plague of generative AI, the release of the 50th anniversary revision of fifth edition D&D along with several upstart competitors, and a mass migration to video, especially short-form—your sometimes humble, occasionally entertaining, hopefully informative blogger finds himself at a crossroads with respect to what to do with his blog.

But first, the good news: The Monsters Know What They’re Doing: Revised Edition for 2025, an update to The Monsters Know What They’re Doing: Combat Tactics for Dungeon Masters, is fully written and being edited at this very moment. The original publication date was to be July 21, 2026—in time for Gen Con!—but as a result of some entirely avoidable delays [cough], the pub date is now set at Oct. 6. I’m lobbying as hard as I can to get the pub date reset to July 21, or at least early enough that advance copies will be available for purchase at Gen Con.

If you’ve tried to use The Monsters Know with the 2025 Monster Manual, you know that a number of brand-new monsters have been introduced, and that even for the already existing ones, a lot of my previous tactical suggestions no longer apply. This revised edition contains updated advice for running updated monsters and new advice for running new monsters, along with useful sidebar content boosted from MOAR! Monsters Know What They’re Doing dealing with such topics as dealing with darkness, combining monsters and running hyper-intelligent enemies. But I wanted to give you more than just an update, so for this edition, I’ve also added a special tip or two for each and every monster, regarding modifications, terrain requirements, pairings with other monsters, and the wacky emergent stuff that can happen because of rules interactions, loopholes and unforeseen consequences.

As a preview, here are my tips for the colossus, a divine supergolem:

Modifications: How can we take this Construct and give it a suite of interesting abilities more in line with its hands-on ability contour? For starters, let’s reskin Radiant Ray and imagine instead that the colossus picks up a Large or smaller object and throws it. We can still call it a ranged attack—normal range 100 feet, long range 300 feet—or we can require the target to succeed on a DC 26 Dexterity saving throw to avoid the damage, using the kraken’s Fling action as a model. Either way, keep the amount of damage dealt the same, but change the type to Bludgeoning, and have both the object thrown and the target(s) it impacts take the damage. Also keep the Prone rider; it’s fun to visualize.

Next, since the colossus is chiefly focused on what’s immediately around it, we can change Divine Beam to Divine Emanation. If a 300-foot-long, 10-foot-wide linear area of effect can be expected to pass through twenty-five targets, a comparable circular or spherical area of effect would have a 30-foot radius. Therefore, let’s make the area of effect a 30-foot emanation originating from the colossus. Since it’s a magical effect, however, we should base the save DC on the colossus’s Wisdom, which means reducing it to 16. I don’t think this reduction is a great problem for the colossus, since it still deals half damage on a successful save, and it’s a lot of damage to begin with. We’ll change the saving throw ability to Wisdom as well: You can’t dodge an aura.

What are things a titanic statue should be able to do with its body besides punch things? The Stomp legendary action already extends the Slam attack to include stepping on foes, so we can skip that. It should definitely be able to grab things, though, shouldn’t it? Let’s add a Seize attack with the same reach and to-hit bonus as Slam, dealing less damage on a hit (say, 2d10 + 10 Bludgeoning) but also imposing the Grappled (escape DC 20) and Restrained conditions, and dealing the damage again automatically at the start of each of the colossus’s turns as long as the target remains Grappled by it. (A side effect of this ability is that a Grappled character won’t be able to get out of range of the colossus’s Divine Emanation.) Also, let’s have our reskinned ranged attack allow the colossus to throw one Grappled creature instead of an object.

Finally, let’s give it one more legendary action and make it impressive—something the colossus can use only once between turns. The Earthquake spell offers inspiration, but instead of making it a spell, let’s have the colossus achieve the effect by stamping its foot:

Earth-Shaking Step. Dexterity Saving Throw: DC 26, each creature on a solid surface in a 60-foot Emanation originating from the colossus. Failure: The creature has the Prone condition, and its Concentration is broken. Failure or Success: The ground in the area is Difficult Terrain until the start of the colossus’s next turn. The colossus can’t take this action again until the start of its next turn.

Shenanigans: In case you’re looking for a way for devotees of one deity (or an archdevil or demon lord) to torment devotees of another deity, neither Hallow nor Forbiddance can keep out a Construct.

Now the bad news: All this new and updated material is going to be book-only. There are various reasons for this, but the most important one is that I’m contractually responsible for contributing a certain amount of original material with every book. Given that this is a revision, it’s hard to tease apart what percentage of its content already exists on this blog, what percentage was changed or added for the original The Monsters Know, what percentage of the original The Monsters Know’s content was altered for the revision, and what percentage is entirely new. But whatever the percentage of new content is, every portion of the book that I post online dilutes it. So this blog isn’t going to be a reference source for the MM25 the way it’s been for the MM14, Volo’s Guide to Monsters, Mordenkainen’s Tome of Foes, Ezmerelda’s Guide to Ravenloft, Fizban’s Treasury of Dragons, Spelljammer, Planescape and Mythic Odysseys of Theros.

On top of that, I have certain … feelings … about D&D 5E24. One of the things I love—and I speak in the present tense—about 5E14 is, even as it substantially streamlined D&D’s rules and options, it still both maintained the feeling of playing classic D&D and permitted play in a wide range of styles, from gritty, grimy low fantasy to wild high fantasy and everything in between. But it was clear from the moment the 5E24 Player’s Handbook dropped that D&D was going all in on wild high fantasy, to the exclusion of other styles, and also that it had chosen to fully indulge a decade’s worth of munchkin demands for MOAR POWER!

Knowing that 5E24 was coming, I had fully planned to seize the opportunity to update Live to Tell the Tale: Combat Tactics for Player Characters and in the process improve it as a teaching tool. I was excited to do so, in fact. But when I held the PH24 in my hands and paged through it, the realization came over me that PCs don’t need the help anymore. They’ve been failure-proofed. There’s almost no error a player can make, at this point, that will end their character’s adventuring career prematurely—not unless their DM goes full adversarial, which I don’t condone. And DMs had a hard time posing appropriate challenges to higher-level PCs already! It’s only going to get harder from here. (The restructured combat encounter difficulty guidelines in chapter 4 of the 2024 Dungeon Master’s Guide will help with that somewhat, at least.)

But it’s not just the shift in the balance of power between DMs and PCs that bothers me about 5E24. There’s a palpable change in design approach between 5E14 and 5E24. In 5E14, it seems to me, the designers began with a narrative in mind, then thought about how best to implement that narrative mechanically. The sense I get from 5E24, on the other hand, is that the designers began with mechanics they wanted to implement, then came up with narratives to rationalize the mechanics. Which is how you end up with warlocks signing lifetime contracts with supernatural entities whose identities they don’t even learn until they’re level 3, and Beast Master rangers who form bonds with immortal spirits instead of, you know, wild animals. (The ludicrous overuse of the adjective “spectral” to describe how PCs’ abilities manifest, combined with the fact that you can undo so many decisions you regret having made with just a good night’s sleep, calls to mind a line from the song “Type” by Living Colour: “Everything is possible, and nothing is real.”)

Now, I happen to think that the MM25 is probably the best of the three revised core books of D&D. All three are flawed, but the MM25 is the one whose strengths most outweigh its flaws. I’m running an Icewind Dale: Rime of the Frostmaiden game in which I’m using 5E14 rules and character creation but 5E24 monster stat blocks whenever they’re available, and I’m liking the feel of that particular combination. But at the same time, since the freaking dawn of creation, the normal distribution of human ability scores in D&D has been from 3 to 18. That’s foundational. It’s bedrock. Anything outside that range is either subhuman or superhuman. I’m OK with the fact that 5E has always allowed PCs of high enough level to raise their ability scores above 18, because at that point, we’re talking about heroes of legend—but other humans, in my opinion, should still fall within the 3-to-18 range. Yet the MM25 gives us guard captains who are as strong as Hafþór Júlíus Björnsson, the record-breaking strongman who played Gregor “the Mountain” Clegane on Game of Thrones; bandit crime lords who are more dexterous than Miyamoto Musashi, Lionel Messi, or any present or past member of Cirque du Soleil or the Flying Karamazov Brothers; and cultist hierophants, pirate admirals, performer maestros and noble prodigies—noble prodigies!—who are more charismatic than Martin Luther King Jr. or Beyoncé. (The most bonkers example is the warrior commander, “skilled in both combat and leadership,” who has the Constitution of Rasputin, greater Dexterity than any acrobat, athlete, dancer or swordfighter who’s ever lived, and Strength that surpasses Björnsson’s … together with Charisma that’s merely on par with, say, Justin Trudeau’s.) What are we even doing here? These are clearly instances in which “line go up” took precedence over maintaining internal logical consistency. And I’ve made a career out of creating, and helping others create, imaginary worlds that come alive because they’re internally consistent.

So there’s all that, but there’s also the petty stuff, such as the fact that turning The Monsters Know What They’re Doing into a 5E24 blog would require me to commission new art for the banner and site icon, because liches can’t cast cloudkill anymore. I mean, what the heck, guys.

As you can imagine, then, I’m in no great hurry to rush out to my friendly local game store and start pouring my money into 5E24 supplements. Unfortunately, I’m also running out of suitable 5E14 material. The natural next step, as I’m well aware (and as many people have made sure to remind me), is Bigby Presents: Glory of the Giants. It’s the step I’d most like to take next, actually. But I’ve made my reasons for not having taken this step before now clear. And please believe me when I tell you, I’ve been trying my best to find a print copy of Glory of the Giants without the AI-tainted art in it for more than a year. I check my local game store regularly; at every convention, if I see D&D books for sale, I look for a second-printing Glory of the Giants.Just a couple of weeks ago, I contacted Wizards of the Coast customer service to ask whether Glory of the Giants had ever gone into a second printing and, if so, where I could obtain a second-printing copy. Suffice it to say that the three rounds I went with customer service were uninformative, and that publishing the replies I received would do WotC no credit whatsoever. Posting my query on the D&D Beyond forums proved similarly fruitless.

But beyond Glory of the Giants, there’s not much left. Mythic Odysseys of Theros turned out to be a lot more interesting than I was expecting, but I think the ship has probably sailed on Guildmaster’s Guide to Ravnica at this point. The Book of Many Things, maybe?

A few readers in the past have asked whether I’d ever look at Kobold Press’s Tome of Beasts series. At the time, I always had enough WotC D&D content to keep me going, and I made the decision that sticking with these official releases would provide the greatest benefit to the most readers. I still have doubts about the benefit of delving into third-party products that I don’t already own. It seems like a lot of work for not a lot of benefit to my readers or to me.

In the wake of the OGL controversy, a lot of players have drifted away from D&D into other systems, and I confess, 5E24’s change of direction struck a blow to my own enthusiasm for D&D. It felt something like a bad breakup. However, my wandering eye hasn’t found another system to alight on yet. The leading alternatives to D&D all offer different interpretations of wild high fantasy; none of them offers the anchored high fantasy that I find to be my natural happy medium. A couple of friends have suggested that I check out Dragonbane and Forbidden Lands from Free League Publishing, and I’ve picked them up and plan to give them a good look, but I’m not sure that’s what folks necessarily want me to write about.

Anyway, at its core, The Monsters Know What They’re Doing has always been about one thing: how to run monsters in D&D 5E. For other topics, I have a newsletter. And books. So the question is what to do with this blog at this particular juncture. And it’s a question I haven’t settled on an answer to yet. I have lots of feelings, but no answer. Certainly nothing final.

So I throw the question out to you, my readers: Given [waves arms around] all this, what do you most want to see me cover in this blog? What kind of content will keep you coming back? Should I stick with what I’ve been doing these last—cripes, nine years? Change lanes? Branch out? Retire from blogging? Give things six months or a year to settle and then pose the question again? Please feel free to post your thoughts in the comments.

Wherever I go from here, it’s been an incredible ride, and I’m glad you’ve been along with me on it.

title: Colossus of Akros Tactics
content_text:
From a bunch of cute animated statues to one honking big, not-at-all-cute animated statue: The Colossus of Akros (Theros’s Sparta analogue) is a massive metal golem that defends the polis when its warriors are away picking fights with other city-states. Despite its size, however, its Intelligence is exactly the same as that of those animal constructs. Imagine a giant death robot with the brains of a golden retriever.

This mega-brute, with extraordinary Strength and Constitution, is focused almost entirely on melee. With a 60-foot movement speed and a 15-foot reach, it has little trouble chasing down fleeing foes. Mundane weapons can’t hurt it, nor can poison or psychic damage, and fire has exactly the opposite of the desired effect, softening the Colossus’s substance up just enough for it to magically re-form where it’s been battered out of shape.

The Colossus has a ranged attack in the form of a spear it can throw, but it makes this attack only when it can’t get within reach of a target to make a melee attack or use its Flames of Akros recharge ability against it. The spear magically returns to its hand after the throw, so this is an attack it can make again and again, but since it’s a ranged attack, it can make it only once per turn. In contrast, its Multiattack lets it make two melee attacks per turn, with either its spear or (preferably, since it deals much more damage) its sword.

Flames of Akros is unusual as area-effect abilities go, since it has no defined shape. Instead, it targets three creatures within 90 feet of the Colossus, wherever they may happen to be standing. Thus, the Colossus doesn’t have to concern itself with whether its enemies are grouped up, only with whether there are at least three within range. This fact makes it much less hesitant to use Flames of Akros whenever it has the ability available than it might be otherwise. Also, targets who fail their saving throws against it don’t take damage just once: They catch fire and keep burning until they can roll a successful save. With this additional damage, Flames of Akros stacks up much more favorably against simply making Sword attacks. (If you want to get really cinematic with it, allow Flames of Akros to incinerate up to a dozen NPCs of CR 1 or below as if they were a single target, but don’t group player characters together in this way.)

Finally, the Colossus of Akros is a Siege Monster. When its enemies are behind cover, it attacks the cover first. Since Flames of Akros is explicitly anti-creature, the Colossus uses its sword to destroy objects and structures.

Unfortunately for the Colossus, its low Intelligence and humanoid-average Wisdom prevent it from considering combat encounters from a strategic point of view. It’s indiscriminate in its target selection, with no capacity for assessing its enemies’ weaknesses. Unless a smaller enemy manages to inflict triple-digit damage upon it (it takes at least 35 damage at once for the Colossus even to notice that it’s been hit), it generally focuses its attacks on whichever of its foes is biggest. If there are two mighty heroes both hacking away at its ankles, or perhaps one on the ground and another in the air, it divides its Sword swings between them, but when it faces only one melee attacker, it tends to focus its attention solely on that one foe. When its opponents run, it attacks the one lagging farthest behind.

Crumbling Destruction is a wonderful trait—our heroes’ reward for destroying the Colossus is that they have to dodge its disintegrating pieces as they fall to the ground—but it doesn’t have any impact on the Colossus’s tactics, since it’s perpetually trying to stay within melee reach of its opponents already.

The Colossus of Akros is an automaton built for and by militant fanatics, so the idea of its retreating when badly damaged is wholly preposterous. This thing fights until it crumbles.

Next: The future of The Monsters Know What They’re Doing.

title: Anvilwrought Tactics
content_text:
The Theran analogue of Hephaestus, Purphoros (“Fire-Bearer” or “Fire-Bringer”—again, personally, I’d have chosen to transliterate Πυρφορος as “Pyrphoros,” since we all know that pyr- means “fire”), made all kinds of neat animated creature-bots for his faithful. Four of these have stat blocks in Mythic Odysseys of Theros: the anvilwrought raptor, the bronze sable, the burnished hart and the gold-forged sentinel.

The anvilwrought raptor is an obvious call-out to Bubo in Clash of the Titans (1981), and I’m here for it. Its very high Dexterity and high Constitution suggest either a skirmisher or shock attacker combat role, but it lacks the Flyby trait, so the only way it’s going to withdraw from melee is by either baiting out an opportunity attack that misses or taking an action to Disengage. Still, this little construct packs a punch for a CR 1/2 creature, dealing an average of 11 damage per round; its AC 14 is also a couple of points above par, and while its hit points are at the low end of the range for its CR, they’re still within it. If anything, it’s slightly overpowered.

So I think we can feel free to treat it as a shock attacker, generally engaging in long-range reconnaissance and striving to avoid combat but, when provoked, delivering a couple of rounds’ worth of nasty talon attacks before Disengaging and flying off. (The attack is called Beak, but real-life raptors attack feet-first. You can picture the anvilwrought raptor’s attack as either two talon attacks or a talon strike followed by a beak attack.)

With only “clever animal” Intelligence, the anvilwrought raptor has just this one behavior pattern. If it’s reduced to 7 hp or fewer before it can make its second Multiattack, it withdraws early.

The bronze sable has a nearly identical ability contour to the anvilwrought raptor’s, but it lacks the raptor’s speed and ability to fly. On the other hand, its False Appearance trait lets it pose as an inanimate object until it’s ready to strike, and Surprise Attack gives it a wicked damage bonus—one die greater than a rogue of equivalent level gets from their Sneak Attack—on each of its first-round attacks against a surprised creature. Add Pack Tactics to these traits, granting advantage on attack rolls when more than one bronze sable is on the scene, and you have yourself some nasty little shock attackers.

Sadly, while bronze sables have the Wisdom to choose their battles and their targets, they don’t have the Intelligence to assess their opponents’ weaknesses. Like the anvilwrought raptor, they’re stuck at “clever animal” level; even simple concepts such as “armor” are beyond them. Thus, they mostly attack whoever provokes them—for instance, someone who tries to enter a restricted area in a temple they’re guarding, or who catches them spying and makes an aggressive move against them—rather than make strategic judgments about which targets it’s most important to take out first. Having chosen a target, they typically stick with their choice as long as the target is conscious, since attacks after the first round receive no damage bonus; may as well keep attacking the already wounded rather than start on someone new and leave the first job unfinished. When faced with more than one choice of target, they opt for the smaller one.

A bronze sable on a recon mission flees when seriously damaged (reduced to 12 hp or fewer), normally Dashing but Disengaging when engaged in melee with more than one opponent or when it can’t afford to take even a single hit more. However. a bronze sable on guard duty fights until it’s destroyed.

The burnished hart is a swift brute, with very high Strength and Constitution complemented by high Dexterity and Wisdom. It doesn’t attack unless provoked, but when it is provoked—most likely, by some fool archer trying to bag it—it trots forward until it’s 20 feet from its foe, then stops. Why 20 feet? Because the archer, realizing their error, is most likely going to back away 30 feet, remaining within the burnished hart’s movement distance. Then, on its next turn, it charges forward and rams them with its antlers, dealing both piercing and fire damage and potentially knocking the target prone. It then completes its Multiattack combo by clobbering them with its hooves. (This sequence presumes that the idiot archer shoots at the burnished hart from more than 50 feet away. If the hart can reach its target with 50 feet of movement or less, it wastes no time and charges right away.)

Once a burnished hart is drawn into combat, it keeps fighting until its foe is finished off. Other foes who join the battle are dealt with in the order in which they took hostile action. A seriously wounded burnished hart (reduced to 18 hp or fewer) instinctively Disengages and runs away, switching to Dashing once it’s out of opponents’ melee reach.

The gold-forged sentinel is an enigmatic, peripatetic construct with inscrutable intentions. Its Intelligence is just as limited as that of other anvilwrought creatures, so imagine a dog, but one that’s capable of following detailed instructions—sometimes long past the point where those instructions make sense anymore. It’s capable of thought, but not advanced thought. It creates meaning, but the meanings it assigns may make no sense to us. It wants things, but the reasons it wants those things aren’t necessarily reasonable. It’s lost its script, and while it continues to follow that script to the best of its ability to recall, its ability to write a new one is extremely limited, almost—but not quite—nonexistent.

Fortunately, a gold-forged sentinel is rarely hostile when it’s first encountered. Unfortunately, it’s hard to predict what will turn it hostile. But getting in its way—hindering it from accomplishing whatever it thinks its responsibility is—is one of the more dependable ways to cheese it off.

Fire Breath is a 5–6 recharge ability, making it generally preferable to Multiattack, but a gold-forged sentinel refrains from using this ability unless and until it perceives two or more creatures within the conical area of effect to be enemies (per the Targets in Area[s] of Effect tables in the 2014 and 2024 Dungeon Master’s Guides). If only one target has irked it, or if all other targets are too far away, it sticks with its Multiattack.

However, despite being an out-and-out brute, with exceptional Strength and extraordinary Constitution, the gold-forged sentinel is also highly mobile, with a brisk 40-foot walking speed and an even zippier 60-foot flying speed. It’s as poor as other anvilwrought creatures at assessing weaknesses, so it judges its foes on the basis of size alone—and it doesn’t always consider all its opponents to be foes, only if they’ve done something it doesn’t like. But take that instance in which it’s designated two opponents as enemies, one right nearby, another 40 or 50 feet away: If the farther opponent seems smaller and therefore weaker, the gold-forged sentinel will leap over its nearby foe, potentially provoking an opportunity attack, in order to land next to the distant one and begin whaling on them. In doing so, it gains additional bludgeoning damage from its Charge trait and may even knock its target prone.

Although its Spell Turning trait grants it advantage on any spell that targets it specifically, increasing the likelihood that the spell won’t harm it, the fact that the caster targeted the sentinel provokes it enough to designate the caster as an enemy, even if it takes no damage from the spell. However, support actions—a cleric casting cure wounds, a bard granting Bardic Inspiration to an ally—don’t concern the gold-forged sentinel at all. Only actions that specifically harm or hinder the sentinel, or are clearly meant to, stick in its craw.

The gold-forged sentinel’s inscrutable nature means that whether or not it retreats when damaged is an open question. You can decide for yourself whether it withdraws when moderately damaged (reduced to 53 hp or fewer), when seriously damaged (reduced to 30 hp or fewer) or not at all, or you can decide it randomly by rolling a d6 (1–2: withdraws when moderately damaged, 3–4: withdraws when seriously damaged, 5–6: never withdraws), or you can have it make a Wisdom check each time it passes one of these two damage thresholds, withdrawing on a success. Set the DC at 20 for a Deadly encounter per DMG14 encounter building rules (High Difficulty per DMG24), 15 for a Hard encounter (Moderate Difficulty) or 10 for an Easy or Medium encounter (Low Difficulty). Being faster than its foes, the gold-forged sentinel Disengages from melee before moving away when it decides to withdraw, switching to Dashing when it’s no longer within their reach.

Next: the Colossus of Akros.

title: Theran Satyr Tactics
content_text:
The satyr reveler in Mythic Odysseys of Theros—not to be confused with the much more powerful satyr revelmaster in the 2025 Monster Manual—is similar to the basic 2014 Monster Manual satyr, and one of its similarities is that its distinctive Ram attack is contraindicated. Although its average damage is adequate, its to-hit bonus is +3 versus Shortbow’s +5, making its expected damage nearly a full point lower (2.7 vs. 3.6 against AC 15). The somewhat boring result is that satyr revelers, like regular satyrs, tend to keep their distance and snipe.

What makes the satyr reveler different is the Enthralling Performance trait, which has the power to charm up to four humanoids within 60 feet. The catch is that they have to listen to the satyr reveler’s performance for a 1 minute to be enthralled. Have you ever known a party of adventurers to sit still for anything for a whole minute?

Ah, but they can be tricked into it—by having the performance take place in the background of a social interaction encounter. While the player characters’ attention is fully occupied by conversation with another satyr, a nearby satyr reveler provides diegetic music for the scene. Before the PCs realize it, they’ve been tapping their feet to the music for … how long? Long enough. Time for a Wisdom save.

Disappointingly, the DC on this save is only 13—not quite high enough to offer the satyr reveler even a two-thirds chance of success of charming a commoner, let alone a PC with a positive Wisdom modifier. Out of four low-level adventurers, a satyr reveler might charitably be expected to charm two; out of five, maybe two, maybe three. The most likely outcome of Enthralling Performance is that around half the party is spellbound into joining the satyrs’ revels, while the other half look on in outrage and disbelief, scrambling to think of ways to snap their allies out of it. (Ironically, if they simply sat down and went with the flow, they could gain the benefit of a short rest: The effect wears off after an hour. But have you ever known a party of adventurers to go with the flow for a whole hour?)

In any event, Enthralling Performance takes too long for the satyr reveler to use it in combat, which means that their baseline combat behavior is the same as that of a basic satyr, including when and how they decide to retreat. That said, thanks to their Multiattack, they get to make two attacks per turn rather than just one, so their damage output is higher. Also, since they’re proficient in Acrobatics, you can skin their Dodge actions in entertaining ways, although it offers no additional mechanical effect.

The satyr thornbearer likewise strives to stay out of melee, but before retreating to a distance of 40 to 60 feet, it first uses its Hail of Arrows action, positioning itself to strike at least three (under 2014 rules) or five (under 2024 rules) targets in a 30-foot cone. If there are multiple thornbearers in an encounter, they take turns using this ability, so that roughly the same number of them are using it in each of the first three rounds. That’s partly to avoid an immediate total party kill, but from their point of view, it’s to make sure they don’t spend all their fey ammunition in one opening salvo, only for their foes to heal up the damage and leave them with no recourse but plinking from behind trees. Ideally, they want to be able to keep dealing that big damage round after round.

In other respects, satyr thornbearers also fight like regular satyrs.

Next: Theran constructs.

title: Nymph Tactics
content_text:
Minor deities and personifications of nature in Greek myth, nymphs map neatly onto the fey creature type in Dungeons & Dragons. Mythic Odysseys of Theros includes stat blocks for four types of nymph. Three of these—the alseid, naiad and oread—are straightforward nature spirits, but the lampad (directly related to the modern English word “lamp,” believe it or not) was a torch-bearer and companion of Hecate, goddess of night, crossings and witchcraft. In Theros, the god Athreos, a Charon analogue, supplants Hecate; instead of lighting liminal spaces in the world of the living, Theran lampads light liminal spaces in the realm of the dead, along with aboveground places associated with death.

Greek alseids were grove nymphs, but Theran alseids are associated with meadows, grassy plains and farmlands. They’re magical creatures with exceptional Charisma, high Wisdom and above-average Intelligence. Although their Strength is also high, neither their Dexterity nor their Constitution is particularly impressive, although their Con is slightly higher than their Dex. Having low scores in both defensive abilities makes for a highly unusual ability contour, suggesting that an alseid in a combat encounter seeks neither to absorb damage nor to avoid it but rather to get out of the encounter altogether. However, its Con being slightly greater than its Dex, when it does make offensive use of its Charisma, it does so by touch. Even more than a shock attacker, an alseid is a hit-and-run combatant—when it doesn’t just run, period.

Radiant Touch, a Charisma-based melee spell attack, fits the alseid’s ability contour to a T, but it has the disadvantage that it doesn’t allow the alseid to get away without provoking an opportunity attack. So let’s take a look at its Innate Spellcasting and see what other options we have.

The most obvious choice is sleep, since an unconscious enemy can’t make an opportunity attack, let alone chase after you. But sleep, under 5E14 rules, is notoriously unreliable: It affects whoever has the fewest hit points first, and that might not be whomever the alseid is in reach of. Placement of the spell is no problem, though, since the alseid is immune to sleep by virtue of being immune to the charmed condition; it can include itself in the area of effect, and even center the spell on itself if it likes. The 5E24 version of sleep is a better bet: The alseid isn’t immune, but that’s OK, because the changes to the spell make targeting much easier, and the incapacitated condition gives the alseid a window of opportunity to get away without having to Disengage.

The reliability of charm person depends on the target’s Wisdom saving throw modifier, which means that under 5E14 rules it may be better or worse than sleep, but under 5E24 rules there’s no difference at all charm person is effectively no different as long as fighting hasn’t broken out yet (once it has, charm person is inferior to sleep, since the target gains advantage on their saving throw, as reader Idan bhk points out below). Both spells enjoin the target from attacking the alseid, allowing it to escape without risking an opportunity attack. The 5E14 version of charm person explicitly says, “The charmed creature regards you [i.e., the alseid] as a friendly acquaintance,” whereas the 5E24 charm person merely dictates that the charmed creature’s attitude toward the alseid is Friendly. It’s very unclear to what extent D&D allows a Dungeon Master to tell a player what their character’s attitude toward a monster or NPC is, but we’ll let that slide, since it has no mechanical effect beyond charm person’s injunction against attacking.

However, we can’t ignore attitude when it comes to calm emotions. Both versions of this spell explicitly alter the target’s attitude from Hostile to Indifferent, and neither imposes the charmed condition nor explicitly forbids targets from attacking. We can say that the Indifferent attitude makes targets disinclined to attack, but will players go along with that? We have no guarantee. Thus, this spell may be a good choice for the alseid with your committed story-first home group, but I wouldn’t try it in your friendly local game store’s organized play program. That being said, if you can get your players to accept having an attitude imposed upon them, calm emotions is powerful, because it can affect four (5E14) or 10 (5E24) targets at once and because, once again, the alseid is immune: The spell affects only humanoids, not fey.

An alseid may offer plant growth, using the enrichment option, as a boon, but it can also use the overgrowth option to thwart pursuit. Generally, however, it uses one of the above spells first and saves this one for the following round, when it’s fleeing already. For the greatest possible benefit, it uses its full movement first, then casts the spell right behind it. Cure wounds and lesser restoration are strictly boons, offered during social interaction encounters that go well.

Given the effectiveness of sleep and charm person (as well as, possibly, calm emotions) at enabling an alseid to extricate itself from combat, it becomes clear that Radiant Touch is a last resort—a way for an alseid to defend itself when it has nowhere to run or its pursuer is faster. For a CR 1 creature, this attack delivers a powerful punch, which hopefully will convey the alseid’s “Leave me alone” message clearly enough for its foes to let it depart.

Naiads are water spirits, in Greek myth specifically spirits of running fresh water (sea spirits were either oceanids or nereids, while lake spirits were limnades), but as far as Theros is concerned, water is water. It stands to reason, therefore, that they don’t fight except in water, and they’ll never be encountered farther from the water than a beach, a riverbank or perhaps a sea cave.

Like alseids, naiads have exceptional Charisma, but unlike alseids, they also have very high Dexterity, so they’re willing to stay in a fight as long as they can keep moving and avoid damage. They flee when seriously wounded (reduced to 12 hp or fewer), and they generally don’t need to worry about opportunity attacks, since they’re invisible whenever they’re fully immersed in water. A naiad will risk provoking an opportunity attack if by doing so it can get off land (or off a vessel) and into water.

The naiad has a Psychic Touch attack, similar to the alseid’s Radiant Touch, but for a CR 2 creature, the amount of damage it does isn’t unusually high. Thus, once again, we see a strong preference for Innate Spellcasting first.

The clear go-to is hypnotic pattern. All of the naiad’s spells require concentration, permitting it to cast only one of them at a time. Its only practical use for fly is when it’s been foolish enough to venture more than 120 feet inland, in which case a 60-foot flying speed can get it back to the water faster than Dashing can. Under any other circumstance, fly is a trap choice. The naiad belongs underwater, and fly doesn’t help it there. Phantasmal force can be used creatively in a variety of ways, but it affects only one target at a time, and its damage output falls well short of Psychic Touch. Hypnotic pattern, on the other hand, affects a large group and can be put to equally good use in or out of water.

As long as it’s concentrating on hypnotic pattern, there’s nothing else for a naiad to do offensively than make Psychic Touch attacks. Underwater, it can—and does—exploit its invisibility to dart from target to target without ever provoking an opportunity attack. Its high Intelligence allows it to identify its opponents’ weaknesses with just a bit of observation, but with its merely average Wisdom, it doesn’t give a lot of thought to target selection. Mostly, it tries to finish its action with some movement left so that it can slip away from its last known position, preventing its opponents from fighting back.

Lampads combine their exceptional Charisma with high Constitution, making them melee spellcasters. The old joke about the cantrip chill touch—that it neither deals cold damage nor works at melee range—is partially subverted in the lampad’s stat block, since its Necrotic Touch melee spell attack deals roughly the same amount of damage as its Chill Touch ranged spell attack. However, this attack doesn’t include Chill Touch’s healing suppression (which is disappointing—I might go ahead and add that in for the sake of parity).

Anyway, there really isn’t much to the lampad’s combat routine beyond that. Its only other spell is gentle repose, which is part boon, part interesting quirk—that is, a lampad might continue to attack a foe not just until they’re unconscious but until they’re dead, but then take an action to cast gentle repose on their corpse. There’s also the curious Corpse Stride trait, which is like tree stride but with corpses. It’s only useful when there are lots of corpses lying around, because the lampad must end its movement in an empty space within 5 feet of the second corpse, which doesn’t work if that corpse is buried. Lampads aren’t incorporeal.

A lampad can be driven off by seriously wounding it (reducing it to 20 hp or fewer). It Dashes away, using Corpse Stride to extend its movement if it can.

Traditionally, oreads are mountain nymphs, which Theros embellishes by associating them with active volcanoes. Stronger and less clever than naiads, they otherwise have a similar ability contour, with Charisma as their primary offensive ability and Dexterity as their primary defensive ability. They have no alternative mode of movement, so rather than run rings around their opponents, they try to keep their distance and attack from range.

They’re also chaotic evil, a considerable departure from their mythical origin as companions of Artemis, Dionysus and Pan.

Basically, these oreads are pyromaniacs. They have a Fiery Touch melee attack and a Fire Bolt ranged attack, and they can also cast burning hands, scorching ray and hellish rebuke. Cue the Crazy World of Arthur Brown.

Although Fiery Touch deals more damage, most of the time they rely on Fire Bolt so that they can stay well out of reach of melee attackers. They divide their attacks between two opponents, sussing out whether any of them is resistant to fire damage. Once they’ve established who has no resistance to their flames, they break out with scorching ray, targeting whichever foes are most susceptible. If they’re moderately wounded (reduced to 34 hp or fewer), they’ll release scorching ray ahead of schedule.

An oread casts hellish rebuke the first time it takes damage. It can cast this spell only once per day, but its foes may not know that and may jump to the conclusion that they’ll get burned every time they attack the oread. Let them. As for burning hands, that’s for when at least two enemies approach within 15 feet; the oread backs away after casting it.

Sadly, the oread’s Invisible in Fire trait is nowhere near as useful as the naiad’s Invisible in Water. PCs may gamely try to fight a naiad in the water, but they’ll never march through flames to fight an oread. However, as long as there are large enough open flames about, Invisible in Fire does allow a retreating oread to “disappear” by walking into them. Their enemies will never be sure whether they’re still around or not.

Next: Theran satyrs.

title: Theran Archon Tactics
content_text:
Archons, in Theros, are celestials, and they’re lawful, but they’re most definitely not angels; they’re more akin to titans, but titans who ruled over humankind as tyrants in some bygone era. They were deposed by champions of the gods, and divided into two camps: archons of falling stars, which repented their wrongs and turned to the side of good, and ashen riders, who still bear an eternal grudge.

An archon of falling stars has the ability contour of a war priest: Extraordinary Wisdom on top, followed by only slightly less extraordinary Strength, Charisma and Constitution. This contour means it uses magic first, weapons afterward, but is unafraid to wade into the fray.

Contrary to our usual mental image of celestial champions, it’s not larger than human-size, and it doesn’t fly. However, according to the flavor text in Mythic Odysseys of Theros, it’s usually seen riding a Large winged mount. Two of these, the winged bull and winged lion, are given stat blocks in Theros, but there’s no need to delve deeply into their tactics, because aside from their supernatural ability scores (which include ape-level Intelligence), their behavior is simple: They act just like an ordinary bull or lion, but one that can fly to get where it’s going and doesn’t turn tail unless and until its rider instructs it to. According to the rules for mounted combat (chapter 9 in the 2014 Player’s Handboook, chapter 1 in the 2024 Player’s Handbook), the mount must be independent to use its Charge or Pounce trait and its melee attack(s). Remember that if the mount takes the Disengage action on its turn, this action exempts not only the mount but also its rider from opportunity attacks.

Combat begins, therefore, with the mount zooming in and using Charge or Pounce to deal extra damage to or knock down the archon’s target. It doesn’t really matter whether the archon rolls higher or lower for initiative than its mount, because its first action in combat is always to cast spirit guardians, exempting its mount and any other ally it may have from the spell’s effect. (Given that it’s called the “archon of falling stars,” a cool way to skin this spell is to surround the archon with swirling motes of starlight.) Chances are excellent that the archon will be able to maintain this spell for the full duration of combat, because it has proficiency on Constitution saving throws and no particular need to do anything else with its concentration. (Yes, there’s crusader’s mantle, but that’s inferior to spirit guardians; I’ll get into the reasons why presently.)

Before it moves on to anything else, the archon will have the opportunity to use its legendary actions. Most of the time, it will choose Attack, which simply and straightforwardly increases its damage output. If there’s anyone within its 10-foot reach, it makes a Radiant Spear attack. If not, since it can’t relocate on someone else’s turn, it casts guiding bolt.

Return to Nyx is an awesome ability—detonate a corpse, preventing it from being raised or revivified while also dealing damage to anyone who stands too close to it—but one that the archon probably won’t have the opportunity to use, unless it’s fighting a party of evil player characters. Since the archon is lawful good, it’s only going to try to kill its foes when they’re incorrigibly evil, or at least incorrigibly rebellious; most of the time, instead, it attacks to subdue. Also, Return to Nyx costs all three of the archon’s legendary actions. The time to use it, if it does use it, is at the end of the turn immediately after it slays an evil or chaotic neutral foe, before anyone else can intervene.

To evaluate Coordinated Assault, which costs two legendary actions, we have to weigh the mount’s damage against another use of the archon’s Radiant Spear or guiding bolt. Radiant Spear deals an average total of 22 damage. The winged bull’s Gore deals an average of 18 (it will never get to deal extra Charge damage in this context); the winged lion’s Bite, 16. Guiding bolt deals only 14, but we can disregard that, because if the archon is engaged in melee along with its mount, it will have disadvantage on ranged attacks, and guiding bolt is a ranged spell attack.

Generally, then, two uses of the archon’s Attack legendary action are superior to one use of Coordinated Assault, except if the value of taking two attacks right now exceeds that of taking one now and one at the end of the next turn, which is only the case if the target of the archon’s attacks is up next in the initiative order. Note also that this legendary action consumes the mount’s reaction, meaning it’s unavailable if the opponent should try to withdraw without Disengaging. If the archon simply uses Attack and the opponent does just that, both the archon and the mount get to make opportunity attacks against them, and the archon can use Attack at the end of the opponent’s turn to fling a guiding bolt at them as they retreat.

With that settled, back to the archon’s other spells.

Crusader’s mantle has some value when the archon of falling stars is fighting as an ally of the PCs, since it will enhance the damage output of combatants who stand alongside it (but only if they wield weapons or make unarmed strikes). However, if the archon is fighting on its own against the PCs, the only one this spell helps, aside from the archon itself, is its mount—and under 2024 rules, it doesn’t even help the mount, since the mount neither wields a weapon nor makes unarmed strikes (as defined in PH24 appendix C). In every other instance I can think of, spirit guardians is better.

Guiding bolt is a strong go-to when the archon of falling stars isn’t engaged in melee, but it’s made for melee. Rather than cast this spell on its own turn, the archon will most often cast it as a legendary action after it knocks a melee opponent unconscious and has no one else within reach to fight.

Command is situationally useful, but it depends on the target’s damage output and Armor Class, how many melee attacks the archon can make against them, and whether or not the archon’s mount is joining in. If the target is capable of putting out more damage per action than the archon’s Multiattack (45 damage total, on average), it’s worthwhile for the archon to spend that action on a Haltcommand to nullify it. If the target is between 15 and 60 feet away and the archon’s mount has already used its movement for the turn, the archon can use an Approachcommand to bring them within melee reach, then whale on them with its legendary actions. (Three Radiant Spear attacks deal more damage than four guiding bolts.) However, the Grovel command, forcing the foe prone, is worthwhile only against a target that the archon has a 30 percent or lower chance of hitting with Radiant Spear, i.e., one with AC 24 or higher. Against lower ACs, the archon is better off simply taking the extra two swings.

Spare the dying’s use case is obvious. It’s only for an archon of falling stars that’s fighting as an ally of the PCs.

The Mount trait allows the archon of falling stars to teleport back to its mount as a bonus action whenever it’s on its feet, but there’s never a good reason for the archon to voluntarily dismount. The only time it will be unmounted is when it’s forced off by its mount’s being knocked prone, and as soon as its mount has gotten up again, the archon uses this bonus action at the start of its turn to get back on it.

The archon of falling stars is a clever and prudent combatant. It’s good at spotting weaknesses (though not preternaturally so), it’s thoughtful about its target selection, and it never willingly picks a fight it can’t win. Between its Magic Resistance trait, its four saving throw proficiencies, and the advantage on Dexterity saves it receives when mounted and not incapacitated, the archon of falling stars has very little to fear from spellcasters of any stripe. At the same time, spirit guardians fortifies it against melee opponents, who have to wade through a swarm of angry little meteors to be able to hit it. It’s opponents with ranged weapons who can actually give it trouble while staying out of its reach of its own defenses. But thanks to its mount’s mobility, it can make its defenses their problem. Let the front-line fighters try to keep up with it; it’s going to chase down Philoctetes.

Any fight against an archon of falling stars should be of Deadly difficulty under 5E14 encounter building rules or High Difficulty under 5E24 rules. Otherwise, it tries to talk its way out of a conflict. However, despite its extraordinary Charisma, it lacks proficiency in any social skill other than Insight. In other words, it’s probably not going to be able to talk a determined enemy out of fighting—but it will be able to read their demeanor and discern how determined they actually are. If the enemy can’t be placated, the archon asks itself, Is this a fight I really want to get into?—basing its final determination on the loathsomeness of its foe(s). It may decide that this particular enemy needs to be resisted, whatever the cost. But it’s more likely to simply Disengage, with some biting parting words, and depart.

When it does commit to a fight, nothing stops it. If it’s reduced to 0 hp, its Radiant Rebirth trait brings it right back in a blaze of glory. This stunt is available to it only once per day, but you don’t need to tell your players that. It’s perfectly fine to let them witness this phoenix-like revival and jump to the conclusion that the archon can’t be killed. Certainly, the revived archon behaves as if that were the case. (It may even be amusing to have the archon presage combat with Obi-wan Kenobi’s warning, “If you strike me down, I shall become more powerful than you can possibly imagine,” to increase the freak-out power of Radiant Rebirth. In fact, the archon is no more powerful than it was before, aside from the burst of damage that nearby creatures take from it, and it has only one-fifth of its former hit points. But again, let your PCs either fall for the kayfabe or test it at their own peril.)

The ashen rider, Theros’s evil variety of archon, has the same ability contour as the archon of falling stars but a higher Challenge Rating. Lacking spirit guardians, it casts blade barrier upon arrival to divide its foes (if any of them are more than 30 feet away) and prevent melee opponents from getting away. This maneuver makes sense only after the ashen rider arrives on the battlefield, so if its combat turn precedes its mount’s, it Readies the spell rather than cast it right away.

The chief use of command for the ashen rider is to force an opponent who can dish out an average of 81 damage per round to Halt. Once engaged in melee, the ashen rider must give up three melee attacks to cast command rather than just two, so the Grovel option becomes advantageous only when the chance to hit without advantage is 15 percent or lower, i.e., against AC 28 or higher. Inside a circle of whirling blades, there’s never any need to use the Approach option. It can be used against someone standing just outside the circle, forcing them to march right through the wall of razors, but this trick—while nasty—deals only 33 damage on average, not much more than one of the ashen rider’s Ashen Blade attacks, and its Multiattack gives it three. Use it only against the insufferably smug.

Compelled duel is cool, but not as impressive as blade barrier, and it competes against blade barrier for the ashen rider’s concentration. So does banishment, which for the ashen rider is a desperation move. Being low on hit points for its Challenge Rating, and not having any resistances or tricks like Radiant Rebirth up its sleeve, the ashen rider depends heavily on its prodigious damage-dealing ability to defeat its opponents. The opportunity cost of these two spells is too great for the ashen rider to cast them routinely.

Like the archon of falling stars, the ashen rider relies on its melee attack, Ashen Blade, switching to Bolt of Ash only when it has no opponent within its melee reach. After other creatures’ turns, it also relies chiefly on its Attack legendary action. Coordinated Assault has the same drawback for the ashen rider that it does for the archon of falling stars and is thus only useful when the ashen rider needs to make two attacks immediately to forestall its target’s action.

The ashen rider’s Reduce to Ash legendary action is much more broadly useful than the archon’s Return to Nyx. Although it costs three legendary actions and deals only as much damage as a single Ashen Blade attack, Reduce to Ash has the additional benefit of subtracting from the target’s maximum hit points—and slaying them instantly if that subtraction brings their maximum hit points to zero. But note that this effect only occurs when the hit point maximum is reduced to zero; if the target has taken damage from other sources, including from the ashen rider’s own mount, it doesn’t count toward cinification. If the PCs are of a level high enough for them to battle an ashen rider, it will probably take three or four uses of Reduce to Ash to actually reduce one of them to ash. When the alternative is three Ashen Blade attacks, it’s hard to argue in favor of Reduce to Ash.

That being said, both the Bolt of Ash attack and the Reduce to Ash legendary action prevent their targets from regaining their lost hit points. If an ashen rider has it in for one opponent in particular, it might use its mount’s flight to keep it airborne, out of reach of enemy weapons, and hew them down with Bolt, Bolt, Reduce, or—if they’re acing their Con saves—two Bolts on their own turn plus three more as legendary actions. Taking one cheap potshot after another isn’t the ashen rider’s style, though. (Speaking of Con saves, it’s important to point out that an opponent who succeeds on their save against Reduce to Ash takes no damage at all, which means the ashen rider has to be very confident in its target’s frailty. It can gauge weakness only by observation, and while its observations are accurate, it’s not going to risk using Reduce to Ash until it’s sure the target is overwhelmingly likely to fail their saves.)

Like the archon of falling stars, the ashen rider doesn’t start a fight it can’t finish. Deadly (5E14) or High Difficulty (5E24) encounters only, please. Ashen riders are bent on destruction and don’t flee, regardless of how much damage they’ve taken.

Next: nymphs.

title: Nightmare Shepherd Tactics
content_text:
The third and final demon—and the third and final fiend—in Mythic Odysseys of Theros is the nightmare shepherd, a Nyxborn creature that guides deceased souls to their final destinations and punishes turnstile-jumpers severely. It shares its mobility, social skill proficiencies, damage resistances and Magic Resistance with the eater of hope and abhorrent overlord, but the similarities end there.

The nightmare shepherd has a distinctive ability contour: With its extraordinary Strength and exceptional Constitution, it looks like a brute … until you notice its even more extraordinary Charisma. This beast is a war caster—a tank that nonetheless favors spellcasting over melee. Its proficiencies in Constitution and Wisdom saving throws are thematically consistent with this contour.

With that in mind, let’s start by looking at the traits and abilities that the nightmare shepherd’s Charisma powers.

First, there’s Aura of Nightmares, which has two functions: It punishes anyone who gets too close to the nightmare shepherd who isn’t either an undead or a construct with a jolt of psychic damage, but it also pumps up the attacks and damage of undead creatures within it. So not only is the nightmare shepherd a nasty enemy, it’s also a leader, commanding up to six undead minions under 2014 area-of-effect rules and up to 20 under 2024 rules (per their respective Dungeon Master’s Guides’ Targets in Area[s] of Effect tables).

According to the rules of thumb I introduced in How to Defend Your Lair and elaborate on in the upcoming Making Enemies, the CR 11 nightmare shepherd, being a leader of troops, is an appropriate enemy for parties of level 11 to 15, while a group of six minions appropriate to this tier of play should be around CR 2 to 4, but a mob of 20 should be only CR 1. Pulling from the Monster Manual, Monsters of the Multiverse and (why not?) Ezmerelda’s Guide to Ravenloft—and looking specifically for front-line brutes that can fight in formation around their commander—excellent choices for 5E14 include bone nagas, sword wraith warriors, priests of Osybus and good old wights. In 5E24, we’re largely limited to ghouls and specters, unless we want to give the nightmare shepherd far fewer minions than it’s entitled to. (There’s one other option as well, which I’ll get to in a moment.)

Second, there’s the nightmare shepherd’s Innate Spellcasting. With minions at the ready, hold person can be devastating, as they pile on an affected foe with both a +5 bonus from Aura of Nightmares and advantage from the foe’s paralyzed condition. But confusion is also strong, since it can potentially affect an entire group of enemies if they’re packed too closely together (minimum of two under 5E14 rules, three under 5E24 rules). Either way, the nightmare shepherd’s concentration is protected somewhat by its undead defensive line.
