Dispel magic is handy for snuffing inconvenient spells of 3rd level or lower, most notably haste and bless (but also, malevolently, spider climb—waiting until the target is all the way up on the ceiling to snatch away the power to do whatever a spider can). Suggestion is extremely situational and competes with hold person and confusion for the nightmare shepherd’s concentration; the opportunity cost is generally too high to cast it in combat.

Third, there’s the once-per-encounter ability Herd the Underworld, which summons up to six shadows, conveniently inside the nightmare shepherd’s Aura of Nightmares. At first glance, it seems like a gimme, but there are actually two problems with it. The first is that the number of summoned shadows is random: You could easily end up with only one or two. The second is that shadows have only CR 1/2, too weak for the Masters of the Realm they’ll be up against. As written, I almost wouldn’t bother.

So let’s rewrite it a bit. First, just give the nightmare shepherd six shadows, or perhaps 1d4 + 2 instead of 1d6. Second, let’s juice them up. Give them AC 13 and 10 hit dice, for an average of 55 hp, and increase their attack bonus from +4 to +5 and their damage (if using the 5E24 version) from 1d6 + 2 to 2d6 + 2, making them CR 2. Alternatively, keep the shadows as written, but have the nightmare shepherd summon them in addition to whatever other minions accompany it. These CR 1/2 shadows will be easily popped distractions, but they will distract. In this case, if you’re using 5E14 rules, you can let the shadows bask in the nightmare shepherd’s Aura of Nightmares while other minions roam around the battlefield or attack from range, expanding the range of minion options to include banshees, deathlocks and deathlock wights, and flameskulls.

The nightmare shepherd needs its minions to run interference, so it’s not only advantageous but essential to use Herd the Underworld immediately at the outset of combat, if not before combat actually begins. Any later in the encounter, especially if you’re rolling for the number of shadows that appear, it could turn out to be a big waste of an action. In fact, if you’re using juiced-up shadows as the nightmare shepherd’s only minions, you may want them to be present already when the PCs first encounter it—although the act of summoning them is pretty cool and worth doing in front of the PCs, dramatically illustrating where the nightmare shepherd’s name comes from.

Whenever a nightmare shepherd has Herd the Underworld to use or a spell to cast, that’s exactly what it does. Only when it’s used Herd the Underworld already, is concentrating on confusion or hold person, and doesn’t need to cast dispel magic for any reason does it use its Multiattack, which packs respectable clout for a CR 11 creature but still plays second fiddle to its magical powers. (Since it carries no shield, it always wields its staff two-handed.)

While its Strength, Constitution and Charisma are impressive indeed, the nightmare shepherd’s Intelligence and Wisdom aren’t to be overlooked. It picks its battles with care, never attacking unless the encounter is Hard or Deadly (5E14) or of High Difficulty (5E24). With just a bit of observation, it can accurately assess its enemies’ weaknesses, and it targets them accordingly—by directing its minions’ attacks as much as with its own abilities.

A combat encounter with a nightmare shepherd—which takes place in darkness whenever possible—has no neatly definable front line. The nightmare shepherd loves mess; it wants to get right in the middle of its foes’ formation, exploiting its flying movement to float above them, out of their reach, unless and until it descends to engage one of them in melee. Meanwhile, its Aura of Nightmares emanates in all directions, buffing allies and tormenting enemies.

But whom, if anyone, does the nightmare shepherd fear? Magic Resistance dampens spellcasters’ power to harm it; Aura of Nightmares makes it difficult to engage at close range. Ranged attackers, however, pose a problem. The nightmare shepherd’s Armor Class is solid but not prohibitively high. Its flying speed allows it to catch those pests easily enough, but if it does that, its undead minions are likely to lose their buffs from Aura of Nightmares. Here’s where it’s good for the nightmare shepherd to have some minions capable of ranged attacks or fast flight themselves, which it can send against enemy marksmen or skirmishers with ranged weapons, two against one. In the absence of such minions, the nightmare shepherd can hobble such opponents with confusion, although it’s not going to blow that spell to undercut just one opponent; there must be a second foe in range, and if you’re using 5E24 rules, there really ought to be a third foe as well.(If it strikes you as appropriate, you can limit PC marksmen’s ability to make mischief by having them encounter the nightmare shepherd and its underlings in an indoor location.)

When ranged attackers are taken care of, support casters buffing their allies are the nightmare shepherd’s next priority, followed by its front-line enemies. By “next priority,” though, I mean “target the nightmare shepherd orders its minions to overrun while it does its own thing up in the air.” If it doesn’t have anything better to do, then sure, it will drift down and make melee attacks with its staff and claws, but remember, its first preference is to use its magic.

Commenters have persuaded me that being Nyxborn overrides creatures’ usual propensity to save their own skin, so despite its very high Wisdom, the nightmare shepherd doesn’t flee from combat, even when it’s seriously wounded (reduced to 53 hp or fewer). You can have it fight to the death, but given its social skill proficiencies, I think the more interesting choice at that point is for it to float down to earth, lay its staff upon the ground and ask what its opponents want from it. Being lawful evil, it won’t be flexible or kind, and any sort of upending of the supernatural order of things is anathema to it. It may grant a single, limited-time-only favor that’s within its power to grant, but there’s a good chance that it will expect something in return. If it can’t get that, its opponents will be put on notice that the next time they meet, the circumstances will be far less favorable to them. On the other hand, if some silver-tongued hero—maybe a College of Eloquence bard—can somehow sketch out a scenario that serves both the PCs’ interests and the nightmare shepherd’s, the fiend will give it due consideration, then agree to it. Should talks break down, however, it’s back to the fray.

Next: Theran celestials.

title: Making Enemies, One Week Away!
content_text:
It’s almost here! Making Enemies: Monster Design Inspiration for Tabletop Roleplaying Games, my foray into creating monsters from the ground up, comes out next Tuesday, Oct. 7. Including not only a treasury of ideas for creating your own homegrown abominations but also interviews with brilliant RPG and creature designers including Willy Abeel, Banana Chan, Kelsey Dionne and Chris S. Sims and 20 original monsters statted out for multiple roleplaying game systems, Making Enemies gives you the tools to surprise and delight your players—and terrify their characters—again and again.

Check out some of these illustrations, produced my by spouse and collaborator Jen C. Mars:

I’ll be up-front with you: Probably owing to inflation, preorders for Making Enemies aren’t coming in as strongly as they did for my previous books. Publishers care about preorders a great deal; they often use them as a metric for whether they should pick up an author’s next book. So if you’ve been deliberating over whether to buy Making Enemies now or later …

https://y.yarn.co/93f3ae20-3666-497a-a848-1d0064d532b3.mp4

title: Abhorrent Overlord Tactics
content_text:
Like eaters of hope, abhorrent overlords are driven by greed, but to them, treasure isn’t a tool they use to manipulate others; it’s an end in itself. Running a close second to their avarice is their cruelty: They relish watching others suffer. They gladly accept other creatures’ loyalty, but they don’t return it. (Again, disturbingly familiar.)

Their ability contour is very similar to eaters of hope’s, with Strength as their primary offensive ability and Dexterity as their primary defensive ability, but their ability scores are high across the board. They also possess the same social skill proficiencies—the trifecta of Deception, Intimidation and Persuasion—which readers Idan bhk and shikomekidomi correctly pointed out that eaters of hope can make use of in social interaction encounters when they’re trying to lure characters into making poor decisions, but this kind of enticement is out of character for abhorrent overlords. More likely, if they employ these proficiencies, it will be to deceive characters into walking into traps (either literal or figurative) that will maim or kill them (either literally or figuratively). Which, by extension, means that they’re most likely to lean on Deception, then Intimidation, and least likely to employ Persuasion.

Their combat package includes a melee attack, Claws; an area-effect recharge ability, Storm of Crows; and the ability to cast confusion, crown of madness and suggestion once per day each. All these spells require concentration, and Storm of Crows also requires concentration, so we can think of it as effectively being a unique spell that only the abhorrent overlord can cast. Because it recharges only on a 6, it’s most likely (but not always!) a once-per-combat ability, and it’s very potent, dealing damage equivalent to a “5th-and-a-half-level” spell. In contrast, confusion is only 4th-level, while crown of madness and suggestion are both 2nd-level. Plus, being unique to the abhorrent overlord, it’s something we want to show off, and since it lasts 1 minute with concentration, it offers more value the longer it’s sustained. So the abhorrent overlord is almost always going to use it right out of the gate.

I say “almost always” because of its area of effect, a 20-foot-radius sphere. Under 2014 rules, per the Targets in Areas of Effect table in chapter 8 of the Dungeon Master’s Guide, the abhorrent overlord should use it only when it can place the sphere to target at least four opponents. Under 2024 rules, that number increases to 10; since there usually aren’t 10 player characters in a party, you can read that as “all of them.” If the fiend’s opponents are inconveniently spread out, it’s better off waiting until its foes are more grouped up. They won’t do that willingly, of course, but the abhorrent overlord is clever; it has its ways. More on that in a moment.

For now, let’s assume that it opens with Storm of Crows. This ability deals a lot of damage: an average of 31 on a failed save, 16 on a successful save, for an overall average of 23 damage per target if we assume a 50/50 chance of success. It deals this damage on the targets’ turns, not on its own, and the targets will naturally try to get the heck out of it. However, the area of effect is difficult terrain, which means that fast-moving targets and those near the periphery may be able to get out of it easily, but those of low-to-average speed who are smack in the middle of it can’t get out without Dashing, leaving them with no action to use on anything else.

In the second round, the abhorrent overlord targets enemies who’ve escaped the storm with its claws—and unlike the eater of hope’s Claws attack, the abhorrent overlord’s deals quite appropriate damage for its CR. It can’t venture into its own Storm of Crows without getting hurt itself, but it can attack those who’ve fled it. Spellcasting is off the table for now, because it needs to maintain concentration on Storm of Crows.

The abhorrent overlord’s Intelligence and Wisdom are both high. It can zero in on any perceivable weakness in its enemies, and it chooses its targets wisely. Its Magic Resistance makes it more vulnerable to conventional attacks than to save-required spells, and its Armor Class is CR-appropriate but not unusually high, so it wants to take out martial foes first. Its 60-foot flying speed equips it well to chase down mobile and ranged combatants, and since finishing an enemy off adjusts the numbers in its favor, it tries to take out the less robust ones first: monks, rogues, bards from the College of Valor or Swords, followed by rangers, clerics and fighters in light armor, and Wild Shaping druids. Barbarians and warriors in medium or heavy armor are more work to bring down and thus better saved for later, while dedicated spellcasters pose less of a threat. Plus, a lower AC means a better chance of getting back lost hit points, since Claws allows the fiend to steal some of its target’s vitality on a hit.

When the abhorrent overlord knocks an opponent unconscious, it flies up into the air, keeping itself out of reach of its other foes’ melee reach. It’s not mechanistic about it: If enemy warriors wield only normal-reach melee weapons, it stays 15 feet in the air between turns, but if any of them has a polearm, it knows to stay 20 feet up, where the longer weapon can’t hit it.

It maintains concentration on Storm of Crows, if it can, until all its opponents have escaped from it. At that point, it has to make a choice: Cast a new spell or continue attacking with Claws? The answer to this question depends on the terrain, what enemies it still faces, and whether it’s backed up by minions such as harpies, giant vultures or swarms of ravens. If its allies can keep up the melee assault, the abhorrent overlord can drop back a bit and plop a confusion spell on two or more opponents (in 2014—three or more in 2024) within a 10-foot-radius sphere around the midpoint of the spell, or crown of madness on an opponent whose behavior and evident class bespeak a low Wisdom save modifier. But if it doesn’t have minions to do its melee fighting for it, a strong second choice is to make a tactical retreat through a choke point where its foes will have to move closer to one another if they want to pursue. In that case, it Readies a confusion spell, with the trigger condition of having the necessary minimum number of foes in the area of effect at the end of the turn before its own.

If this spell goes off, the abhorrent overlord can continue to make Claws attacks against unaffected enemies while the affected targets mill around aimlessly. If it’s never triggered and fizzles, the fiend casts crown of madness and keeps its distance, counting on the target to do its work for it. There’s also the remote chance that Storm of Crows recharges at the start of its turn, in which case it can reuse this ability instead of casting a spell. It only returns to making melee attacks, however, if the opposition is in obvious disarray. When the abhorrent overlord is seriously wounded (reduced to 54 hp or fewer), it flies away, using the Dash action to double its speed.

What about suggestion? Well, it’s such a challenging spell to make good use of, and it targets only one opponent, and it requires concentration. It’s better used in social interaction situations in which the abhorrent overlord’s goal is simply to mess with somebody to their detriment. In combat, it’s not worth the opportunity cost.

Abhorrent overlords’ Wisdom is such that they never willingly bite off more than they can chew. Under 2014 rules, any encounter with an abhorrent overlord—with or without minions—must be Hard, at a minimum, and really should be Deadly. Under 2024 rules, it should always be High Difficulty.

Next: nightmare shepherds.

title: Eater of Hope Tactics
content_text:
Unlike in most Dungeons & Dragons cosmology, fiends in Mythic Odysseys of Theros aren’t begotten as incarnations of evil. Instead, they’re something more like undead: entities that form out of the corrupted souls of deceased mortals who were “despicable in life.” The sourcebook refers to all its fiends as “demons”—the lawful devil–vs.–chaotic demon dichotomy doesn’t exist in Theros.

The eater of hope is one such “demon,” uncomfortably reminiscent of certain members of our own decadent society, “obsessed with wealth and dominating lesser beings.” It uses the promise of riches as enticement, but it’s incapable of creating the treasure it uses as bait; it has to steal it from others. Which means that heroes on their way back from a successful adventure, laden with gains as well as glory, are top targets for an eater of hope attack. Not only do they have the stuff it wants and needs, it also gets to enjoy robbing them of their sense of accomplishment.

With extraordinary Strength and very high Dexterity, the eater of hope is a shock attacker, but it doesn’t have the proficiencies in Perception and Stealth to function as an ambush attacker. Instead, it must rely on its 60-foot flying speed to dive-bomb its targets, perhaps as they’re passing through ruins, gloomy forests or similar places where it can perch someplace high up. It has 120 feet of darkvision, so it also takes advantage of targets’ need to stop and rest at night, striking after the sun has set and before they’re fully refreshed.

It opens with its Breath of Hopelessness. Not only is this a 5–6 recharge ability, which nearly always indicates a high-power ability that a monster always takes advantage of when it has the chance, it’s also necessary in order for the eater of hope to achieve its full assault power, since its Claws attack deals decent damage for a CR 6 attacker but its Multiattack doesn’t give it enough of that attack.

Breath of Hopelessness covers a 30-foot cone, which in 2014 terms means the eater of hope wants to make sure to use it against at least three opponents and in 2024 terms means at least five. This threshold is easy to meet if a party is on the march, or if its members are sleeping on bedrolls under the stars. It’s harder if the player characters have tents, but no starting equipment pack contains a tent, and players often imagine that their characters are sleeping in tents but never get around to actually buying them. In any event, the eater of hope is careful to position itself where its Breath of Hopelessness will hit as many targets as possible, and if it can’t meet the minimum threshold, it waits to launch its attack until it can.

The eater of hope’s goal is to relieve its foes of their treasure and, in the process, make them feel rotten about themselves; if it gets to gobble them up afterward, so much the better. Unfortunately, however, it’s hampered by an unfortunate Intelligence-Wisdom contour. It’s smart enough to have a cogent plan and flexibility in its methods, but it doesn’t quite possess the discipline to choose its targets with care. Thus, while it goes into combat with a respectable strategy—wear them down with Breath of Hopelessness, then Multiattack whoever presents the greatest obstacle to its goal until they can no longer resist—it falls apart more or less instantly, as the eater of hope reacts to every change in the situation without thinking about what it means in the big picture.

Thus, let’s say it unleashes its Breath of Hopelessness against a party of five level 5 PCs. Two succeed on their Charisma saves, while the other three fail. The fiend is hovering up in the air out of reach, so no front-line melee opponent can get at it just yet; only ranged attackers and spellcasters can, and its Magic Resistance gives it a measure of protection against casters. You’d naturally conclude that a marksman, or a skirmisher with a ranged weapon, would be the chief obstacle that the eater of hope had to overcome.

Ultimately, though, it doesn’t matter. To get the full value out of Breath of Hopelessness, the eater of hope has to attack one of the targets who’s failed their save against it, because each hit against them deals the extra necrotic damage that the fiend needs in order to deal enough damage from the get-go to perform its combat role as a shock attacker. Which of these targets does it choose? Probably, whichever one has managed to hurt it the most since its previous turn, even though it might be wiser to choose the one who looks most frail (to take them out of the battle ASAP) or one who’s capable of healing (undoing its hard work).

Most healthy level 5 PCs will have between 32 and 48 hp, with an average around 39—but the eater of hope doesn’t attack when they’re at full health. It attacks when they’re banged up and in need of convalescence. One hit from its claws, delivering extra damage from its curse, deals an average of 22 damage. Since it only chooses a target that’s already taken an average of 26 damage from the intial blast of its breath, that’s a total of 48 damage, giving it a solid chance of knocking out two enemies in the second round of combat. And once its latter foe is unconscious, it can fly back up into the air without having to worry about provoking an opportunity attack.

Once it’s brought down everyone affected by its curse, however, it starts to lose its momentum. Now each Claws attack deals only 15 damage, and if it can’t bring its foe down, it risks an OA if it tries to fly back out of reach. (Despite being Large in size, the eater of hope has only a 5-foot reach on its melee attack.) Its Armor Class is high for its Challenge Rating, but not so high that it can’t worry about OAs at all, and that AC is paired with hit points that are on the low side, balancing it out. If an enemy can land a hit on an eater of hope, it’s going to feel that hit. The eater of hope uses Breath of Hopelessness against its enemies again if it should happen to recharge, and maybe one who succeeded on their first save will fail their second, but that’s not something it can count on.

Note what Theros fiends don’t have: any trait that allows them to re-form in the Underworld if their physical body is destroyed. A dead Theran demon is dead dead. And while eaters of hope’s Wisdom isn’t quite enough for them to be prudent about target selection, it is enough to give them a normal survival instinct. While “resentful of all life and joy,” they’re sufficiently attached to their present existence to want to keep doing the nasty things they do. Consequently, when they’re seriously wounded (reduced to 36 hp or fewer), they snarl, Disengage if necessary, Dash otherwise, and fly away in search of easier prey.

I haven’t mentioned the eater of hope’s Insatiable Greed trait, because it’s merely a ribbon. Since the demon’s Intelligence and Wisdom are what they are, this trait doesn’t influence its tactics in any way; it simply gives us an idea about when, why and whom the eater of hope attacks in the first place.

Next: abhorrent overlords.

title: Woe Strider Tactics
content_text:
The only aberrations in Mythic Odysseys of Theros, woe striders are onetime mortals who severed themselves from destiny—and are now cursed by the god Klothys to wander the Underworld in search of discarded scraps of destiny to replace it. They slaughter whatever living beings they encounter, searching for directives in those beings’ entrails.

Sadly, their stat block doesn’t measure up to their intriguing backstory. In essence, they’re nothing more than magic-suppressing brutes. Their primary offensive ability is Strength; their primary defensive ability, Constitution. They have proficiency in Intimidation and can use telepathy to engage in it, but it’s not clear what they want that they could bully anyone else into giving them. (Intimidation has never had any mechanical effect when applied to player characters.)

Their Antimagic Cone trait projects the effects of the antimagic field spell over a 60-foot cone, enough to cover up to six opponents when properly positioned and aimed. A woe strider can turn this feature on or off at the start of its turn, but there’s no good reason for it ever to turn it off: Unlike, say, the beholder, it has no magical ability of its own that Antimagic Cone might interfere with. Its Multiattack always comprises two Claw attacks and one Bite attack, and while two hits with Claw impose the grappled condition on a target, that condition isn’t enough to grant advantage on any follow-up attack; it merely makes it difficult for the target to get away.

If we’re going to find anything interesting in this monster, it’s in the juxtaposition of its below-average Intelligence and its high Wisdom. Intelligence 8 is just barely enough for it to deviate from instinct when it senses that things aren’t going its way, but not enough for it to make plans or identify enemies’ weaknesses. Wisdom 14, however, is enough for it to tell when a battle is unwinnable and steer clear of it. It also implies that the woe strider is careful in its target selection—but the woe strider’s Intelligence means it’s not good at it. The funny upshot of this combination of abilities is that it chooses its targets for demented reasons—which actually fits well with its being an aberration.

Here’s one completely arbitrary yet plausible target selection criterion: The woe strider thinks it can learn what its next move should be by reading clues in its victims’ entrails. Wiser targets will carry that wisdom in their entrails, naturally! Therefore, it targets those who seem like they might possess metaphysical knowledge—clerics, of course, but also druids, wizards, maybe warlocks, but also any other character that superficially looks like they might have such knowledge, such as a College of Lore bard. It doesn’t matter whether a character actually has high Wisdom, only whether they look as if they understand things that others wouldn’t.

Woe striders are pretty fast—their speed is 40 feet, either walking or climbing—but they don’t have the Spider Climb trait, so they’re not going to be dropping down on their victim from the ceiling. Instead, whenever possible, they use their extra movement to get them around the front line without provoking opportunity attacks. (Those armored meatheads’ entrails never have anything interesting to say.) They make both their Claw attacks against the same target in order to gain the benefit of the grappled condition: They chose this target, for whatever goofy reason, and don’t want to let them go. Plus, they can’t make their Bite attack unless they have a grappled, incapacitated or restrained target to use it against. If either Claw attack misses, or if they foolishly divide their attacks, they forfeit that part of their Multiattack.

Sadly, a woe strider lacks the wits to pay attention to which, if any, of its opponents is carrying a magic weapon or other magic item, which a hit with Bite would temporarily deactivate. Since its mouth faces its target, its Antimagic Cone will do the job, not only against that target but also against anyone behind them (because of how a cone is defined in fifth edition Dungeons & Dragons, it covers an arc of about 53 degrees, which you can round to 60 degrees for convenience), but if the woe strider must turn to Bite someone else, their Antimagic Cone rotates with them.

Any encounter with one or more woe striders must be at least Hard under 2014 encounter building rules, and preferably Deadly; under 2024 encounter building rules, it must be at least Moderate Difficulty and really should be High Difficulty. When a woe strider is moderately wounded (reduced to 77 hp or fewer), it stops focusing its attacks on the target it initially chose and starts defending itself against whoever’s dealing the most damage to it. When it’s seriously wounded (reduced to 44 hp or fewer), it starts to reconsider its choices. But it’s not a simple consideration: Yes, it has a self-preservation instinct, but it’s also weird and desperate and disordered in its thinking. At the start of each of its turns, have it make a morale check—a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw, which it makes at +2—and flee if it succeeds on the save, which is the opposite of how morale checks usually work but in this case is the actually wise thing to do. If it fails, it makes a last-ditch attempt to disembowel the target it came for … and if it succeeds, i.e., if it not only knocks the target unconscious but kills them, it willingly fails its next morale check and takes the Search action (or the Study action, under 2024 rules), spending its turn neither attacking or fleeing but scanning the deceased target’s entrails for the signs it seeks. If by some miracle it survives this turn of inaction, it Disengages and flees at last.

Next: eaters of hope.

title: Phylaskia Tactics
content_text:
Guardians of the Underworld, phylaskia (singular and plural, a slightly garbled derivation of Ancient Greek φῠ́λᾰξ phýlax, “guard, sentry”) are lawful neutral undead, indifferent to all except those who trespass. Theirs is a brute ability contour, with extraordinary Strength and exceptional Constitution, along with very high Wisdom and high Dexterity and Charisma. They have proficiency in Constitution and Wisdom saving throws, making them resilient against being turned, among other things. They’re flat-out immune to being charmed or frightened; they also can’t be blinded, deafened or poisoned, and they never tire. Direct damage from a Dex-save spell is the best way to bypass their plate armor, but they can also be fooled by illusions or simply banished.

Phylaskia are aided in their task by the Undead Fortitude trait, which they share with zombies—they get knocked down, but they get up again—and the Vigilant trait, which guarantees that they’re never surprised. However, it’s unclear how much they’re aided by their Gatekeeper’s Aura trait, which forces creatures who start their turn within 10 feet of them to make a Wisdom save; on a failure, until the start of their next turn, they have disadvantage on saving throws, and their speed is halved.

Significantly, this doesn’t impose disadvantage on their next save against Gatekeeper’s Aura, and just one success immunizes them against this trait for 24 hours. What’s it for, then? Phylaskia don’t have any other ability that forces creatures to make saving throws.

If this isn’t simply an oversight, then what it tells us is that phylaskia don’t work alone. Maybe they’re accompanied by cerberi, which do have an ability that forces saves: their breath weapon. Each phylaskia might be accompanied by a single Underworld cerberus, say, or a handful of two-headed cerberi.

When it comes to their actions, there’s no much to say. Their Multiattack grants them two Longsword attacks and one Strength Drain attack, and neither imposes any debilitating condition that would dictate using it before the other. Since they don’t carry shields, there’s no reason for them not to wield their swords in both hands for the larger damage die.

Despite their high Wisdom, whose sole purpose seems to be to fuel their Perception skill, they don’t shy away from fights they can’t win; they take on anyone who tries to pass without authorization, no matter how powerful. Being undead, driven by compulsion, they’re undeterred by any amount of damage. They are, however, capable of recognizing when a fight isn’t going their way, and they do choose their targets carefully—within the range of possibility. That is, while they may know they need to focus their blows on an enemy spellcaster, they may not be able to get at that spellcaster without, say, letting a rogue slip past them. This is where having cerberi fighting alongside them is helpful: They can order those more mobile creatures to take down the back-liners for them. For this purpose, the intelligent Underworld cerberus, which can’t speak but does understand language, makes a better ally than the more or less feral two-headed cerberus.

Next: woe striders.

title: Eidolon Tactics (Theros)
content_text:
In Mordenkainen’s Tome of Foes and Monsters of the Multiverse, an eidolon is an undead spirit divinely honored by its god(s) for its zealous devotion by being assigned to protect a sacred place from defilers. However, in Mythic Odysseys of Theros, when someone escapes the Underworld, their identity is ripped away from them; they become one of the Returned, while their lost identity becomes an eidolon. The Returned goes through the motions of its former life without any memory of that life. The eidolon remembers its life, but that life no longer holds any meaning for it. It’s personality without continuity.

As long as its personality isn’t violent or neurotic, there’s not much to fear from a flitterstep eidolon. Those with which a party of player characters might find themselves in conflict are unhealthy personalities, ones that gave in to their fears and acted out in ways that harmed others. Incorporeal beings with low Strength and middle-of-the-road mental abilities, flitterstep eidolons stand out for their exceptional Dexterity, their primary ability in both offense and defense. When they choose to fight or are forced to, they’re mobile hit-and-run attackers, often making their first strikes from ambush, as indicated by their proficiency in Perception and expertise in Stealth.

Flitterstep eidolons are quick and hard to hit. Although their Armor Class is only 14—all of that coming from their Dex—their Blurred Form trait imposes disadvantage on all attack rolls against them, their Evasion trait lets them take half damage on failed Dexterity saving throws against area effects and no damage at all on successes, and they’re resistant to physical damage from nonmagical weapons. They also have advantage on saves against being turned by clerics and paladins. Constantly on the move, they never let themselves be pinned down.

Flitterstep eidolons are most vulnerable to opponents who rely on magic weapons or non-evocation spells, so it targets these opponents first, especially the latter. It’s pretty good at staying out of melee reach, but staying out of spellcasting range is another matter, so that problem needs to be dealt with preemptively. While focusing down spellcasters, it makes both its attacks against the same high-priority target; when it’s no longer particularly worried about any of its opponents, it divides its attacks.

Whenever it can, it uses Flitterstep before its first attack to give itself advantage on the roll. After attacking, it zips away, not worrying about opportunity attacks because of its Blurred Form. It always tries to finish its turn at least 20 feet away from its nearest opponent, farther if it can.

Having escaped from the Underworld once already, they’re subconsciously aware that they don’t want to go back—or maybe subconsciously fearful of having their last vestige of self destroyed forever. Either way, they flee when seriously wounded (reduced to 17 hp or fewer).

Ghostblade eidolons are the souls of former warriors, and they retain their erstwhile belligerence, wrongly believing that they’re still fighting for gods and country. They have lower Dexterity and higher Strength than flitterstep eidolons; interestingly, they also have higher Intelligence and Charisma. That Intelligence is enough to give them a bit more tactical insight, while the Strength makes them want to charge into melee.

They share flitterstep eidolon’s Blurred Form and Turn Resistance, but they lack the Evasion trait, so they’ve got a problem with all spellcasters and generally try to take them out first. But because they’re undead, there’s a bug in their programming: When confronted by an opponent who challenges them with a magic weapon, they must accept the challenge, for glory’s sake. It’s a compulsion. They can’t help it.

Once engaged in melee with an opponent, a ghostblade eidolon typically remains engaged until it can subdue its foe, but if it’s targeted by a spellcaster and its current opponent is wielding a nonmagical weapon, it breaks off to go after the spellcaster. If it’s trying to take down a spellcaster and a melee opponent approaches to intervene, it divides its Ghostblade attacks between the two of them, but it still stays put.

To flee from battle would be shameful, so ghostblade eidolons fight until they’re destroyed.

Next: phylaskia.

title: Returned Tactics
content_text:
Hades was one hell of a video game, wasn’t it? I absolutely loved it. I’ve also been a massive fan of the musical Hadestown since long before it ever reached Broadway, back when Anaïs Mitchell was still workshopping it and released an album on which she and her friends recorded early versions of the songs. Both of these works reference the myth of Orpheus and Eurydice, in which the bard ventures into the Underworld to bring his lover back to the world of the living—assuming, as we might, that once they get back, they’ll live happily ever after. Of course, in the myth, she doesn’t make it back to the surface. But what if she did make it back … and … wasn’t … quite … right … anymore?

That’s the problem with the Returned in Mythic Odysseys of Theros. They’ve done one of the most awesome, incredible things imaginable: They’ve busted out of the afterlife. The problem is, you can’t do that and take your personality with you—or even your identity. Those get ripped away from you, becoming eidolons (which I’ll look at next). What’s left of you is an empty, blank-faced husk, going through the motions of living without actually experiencing it.

The Returned generally aren’t aggressive, and they rarely want anything badly enough to fight for it, so why would player characters find themselves in a combat encounter with one?

Because the golden masks they wear to hide their featureless visages are worth a lot of money.

The Returned drifter is your basic Returned, a placid slacker dwelling far from the bustle of town, where its uncanny existence won’t bother anybody. It fights only in self-defense, which means it usually employs its Scimitar melee attack rather than its Sling ranged attack, unless someone’s shooting at it from afar. But even then, it lacks the gumption to take cover; it shoots back because that’s what you do when someone shoots at you. Despite having enough Intelligence to come up with tactics and enough Wisdom to care about defending itself, its manner of fighting is as uninspired as the rest of its existence. It goes through the motions of fighting until it’s destroyed, and then it’s over. When it takes damage, it doesn’t flee.

The Returned sentry is a bit different from ordinary Returned, because the responsibility to guard something has been impressed upon it, and having nothing else to live for, it takes that responsibility seriously. With very high Strength and high Dexterity, they fall upon their foes swiftly and use their Pack Tactics trait to form a unified front. However, despite being higher than their merely above-average Constitution, Dex is not their primary defensive ability, as it would be for an evolved creature, but rather a secondary offensive ability. They don’t try to dodge incoming attacks or rely on mobility for defense. Those that are near enough charge forth and attack with their spears, while those that are too far to both move and make melee attacks in a single action attack with their slings.

The one respect in which their Intelligence and Wisdom serve them is that they know how to position themselves to minimize their enemies’ ability to slip past them. They’re there to guard, and they do it well. Rather than form a solid wall, they space themselves out 10 feet apart, allowing them to cover more area while still conferring the benefits of Pack Tactics upon one another. Also, any sentries not positioned to engage in melee with the opposing front line, rather than join the scrum (and possibly concentrate their forces too much to provide broad coverage), use their slings to take shots at foes in the back.

Returned sentries don’t flee when they take damage, either; they fight until they fall. If that leaves a gap in their line, another Returned sentry moves into the gap, if one is available to do so.

I’ve done my darnedest to find out what “palamnite” refers to in the name Returned palamnite, and every reference I can find is circular, referring back to this particular entity. The name sounds kind of Greekish, but it doesn’t seem to come from actual Greek, either modern or ancient. The closest I can get is “Palamite,” referring to a doctrine of Eastern Orthodox Christian theology, or the Greek word παλάμη palámē, meaning “palm” or “hand”; in Ancient Greek, it carries the additional figurative meanings of “cunning” or “handiwork.” None of these meanings seems to apply to the Returned palamnite, which is simply a rampaging murderer, compulsively acting out the pervasive violence of its former life.

The Returned palamnite stat block contains a couple of curious elements. It includes proficiency in Acrobatics, Athletics and Stealth, implying aptitude for escaping grapples and ambushing victims. It seems to treat the palamnite’s very high Dex as both its primary offensive ability and its primary defensive ability, although it has no mobility trait that would mark it further as a shock attacker; if we construe Constitution as its primary defensive ability instead, it still doesn’t have any mobility trait that would mark it as a skirmisher. Like the Returned sentry, the palamnite seems to be all offense, at least once it emerges from hiding to attack.

Since the palamnite has no way of employing its Stealth proficiency during combat, and since the behavior of all Returned is dictated by their compulsions, I think we have to conclude that whenever it’s not engaged in combat, it hides itself (or tries to) as it moves from place to place. It attempts to attack with surprise, which has different effects depending on whether you’re using 2014 or 2024 fifth edition rules: If it succeeds in gaining surprise under 5E14 rules, it gets a round of free attacks while its foes stand around gawping, but since they take no actions, they won’t activate its Fleeting Anger trait, which grants it advantage on attack rolls whenever it’s just taken damage. Under 5E24 rules, on the other hand, all it gains from surprise is a strong likelihood of attacking first—but its enemies’ counterattacks will trigger Fleeting Anger immediately. I think the 5E14 rules favor it slightly more—a head start on damage dealing is worth a lot—but Fleeting Anger is the Returned palamnite’s most interesting feature, so I can’t complain about its getting to show it off sooner.

OK, so we have an entity that prowls around committing murders whenever it can get away with them and that is going to keep attacking relentlessly once it starts. How does it choose its victims? Its Intelligence and Wisdom are both above humanoid average, which is enough for it to care about choosing its targets wisely but not enough for it to be a particularly accurate judge of their weaknesses. To answer this question, then, we turn to its alignment—chaotic evil—and conclude that it simply preys on the weak, or at least those whom it perceives to be weak. In other words, targets who are smaller, less armored and less visibly robust. Beyond that, it chooses largely at random.

Just like the other Returned, the palamnite is undeterred by damage and fights until it’s destroyed.

ETA: As reader Lionheart261 pointed out, I forgot to include the Returned kakomantis (fr. Gk. κάκο- káko-, “bad, evil,” and μάντης mántēs, “seer, fortune teller”), a variant of the Returned palamnite. Since it has only one attack action, the ranged spell attack Underworld Bolt, we’ll consider Charisma to be its primary offensive ability. Instead of charging and engaging its victims in melee, it simply snipes at them from a distance of 40 to 120 feet, focusing down already wounded targets rather than switching from foe to foe. If an opponent charges them, they move away, attempting to maintain this distance. Other than that, it behaves the same as any other Returned palamnite.

Next: eidolons (the Theros version, as opposed to the version in Mordenkainen’s Tome of Foes and Monsters of the Multiverse).

title: Theran Giant Tactics
content_text:
Mythic Odysseys of Theros contains stat blocks for two giants, which are both lumped together under the heading “giants”: the doomwake giant and the hundred-handed one.

Doomwake giants are Nyxborn creatures conjured into existence by people’s anxieties: If enough people come to believe that their annihilation is nigh, a doomwake giant shows up to make their fears come true. Like most giants, they’re Huge creatures with extraordinary Strength and Constitution, making them partial to brute melee combat. But they aren’t truly weak in any ability score, and in fact they enjoy high Wisdom and very high Charisma. They speak only Giant, but I imagine that player characters who speak that language could have very ominous conversations with them.

They have proficiency in Constitution and Wisdom saving throws, but not Dexterity, and since their Dex and Intelligence modifiers are both merely +1, these are the nearest things they have to vulnerabilities. Logically, you’d expect them to try to neutralize back-line spellcasters, who are most likely to target these two abilities, before taking on other foes. However, doomwake giants also have Magic Resistance, so they’re actually more vulnerable to conventional weapons than to spells, since their Armor Class is a modest 15.

Doomwake giants’ Aura of Erebos trait makes getting within 10 feet of them unhealthy, but this effect is resisted with a Con save, and who’s most likely to have excellent Con save modifiers? That’s right: the front-liners, especially fighters and barbarians. All it takes is one successful save to acquire immunity to the aura for 24 hours. So these warriors, along with foes using ranged weapons (who aren’t subjecting themselves to the Aura of Erebos), are prime targets of a doomwake giant’s wrath.

Most of the time, a doomwake giant relies on its dual-Slam Multiattack, focusing on the aforementioned enemies. This melee attack has a 15-foot reach, and the giant is a Huge creature, two features that combine to allow it to stride boldly over the front line and attack other enemies behind it without leaving the front-liners’ melee reach. Since the giant takes up a 15-by-15 space, a target must be standing more than 30 feet behind the front line to be safely out of reach of its fists.

However, when Noxious Gust is available, and when the giant can position itself so as to have at least four targets (per the Targets in Areas of Effect table in the Dungeon Master’s Guide, chapter 8) in a 10-foot-wide, 60-foot-long swath in front of it, it uses this breath weapon to deal necrotic damage and knock its enemies down. It won’t provoke opportunity attacks against itself just for the sake of getting into position, though. The targets must be suitably arrayed already, or the giant must be out of reach of any opponent who might make a melee attack against it, or it must be able to maneuver into position without leaving that opponent’s reach. Remember, though, because it’s Huge, it can move through the space of any humanoid opponent, making that last option relatively easy for it.

Because of their Nyxborn nature, doomwake giants don’t retreat from combat, regardless of how much damage they take. But if they happen to be taking an unexpected amount of damage from an unexpected source, they do turn their attention to the source of the damage; they don’t keep mechanistically attacking the same targets that they originally picked out as likely threats.

The hundred-handed one is inspired by three beings from Greek myth, the Hecatoncheires, whose name literally means “Hundred-handed.” There were three of them, named Briareos (sometimes called by the byname Aigion, “Son of the Sea”), Gyges and Kottos, and they aided Zeus and the other Olympian deities in their war against the Titans. In addition to their numerous arms, they had fifty heads apiece. The hundred-handed ones of Theros are single-headed, and their extra arms revolve around their bodies like muscly ioun stones.

Like doomwake giants, hundred-handed ones are blessed not only with extraordinary Strength and Constitution but also with high Dexterity and Intelligence and very high Wisdom and Charisma, and they’re proficient in Con and Wisdom saves. Unlike doomwake giants, they don’t have Magic Resistance, so they do place a premium on taking out those pesky spellcasters. Their Multiattack lets them make four Longsword attacks or two Rock attacks, but as written, it doesn’t allow them to mix and match. Personally, I don’t see why a creature with that many arms can’t flex a bit, so I’d also entertain the combination of two Longsword attacks plus one Rock attack. All other things being equal, however, a hundred-handed one would rather spend its turn making those four melee attacks. If it wants to cut down a bothersome wizard, it would much rather do so by walking right up to them, if it can, than by chucking a boulder.

Doesn’t it have to worry about opportunity attacks, though? After all, its AC is no higher than a doomwake giant’s. Ah, but it has the Deflect Attack reaction, which lets it pop its AC up to 20 for the duration of one weapon attack. Thus, on its own turn, it can feel free to provoke one opportunity attack with reasonable confidence that it can parry the blow. Meanwhile, its Reactive trait lets it take a reaction on every turn in a combat, which means it can parry one weapon attack from each foe—or make a separate opportunity attack against them if they try to get away from its merciless blades.

Getting back to Longsword vs. Rock, the latter attack deals more damage than the former—but not more than twice as much, which is the opportunity cost of not making two Longsword attacks instead. Rock does also knock the target prone on a failed Strength save with a hard-to-beat DC, but because ranged attacks are rolled with disadvantage against prone opponents, this condition makes it unwise to follow up with another Rock.

But what about knocking an enemy down with a Rock attack, then walking up to them, then attacking with Longsword? Again, not permitted as written, but if you bend the Multiattack to make one Rock attack interchangeable with two Longsword attacks, there is an inflection point at which one Rock attack made straight followed by two Longsword attacks with advantage deals more expected damage than four Longsword attacks. It’s not against that lily-livered wizard, though—it’s against a front-line tank. With the hundred-handed one’s +13 attack modifier, an opponent with AC 19 or lower is best dealt with using nothing but its Longsword. Only against AC 20 or higher does it make sense to knock an enemy flat with Rock before moving in to stab ’em while they’re down. (A hundred-handed one is pretty smart, but not so smart that it can read an opponent’s AC just by looking at them. “Plate plus shield” is its minimum criterion for this combination, with no accounting for bonuses from magic armor.)

Hundred-handed ones are lawful neutral, so by default, they’re indifferent to passers-by who don’t disturb the peace. If they’re provoked to the point of hostility, they’ll attack, but when moderately wounded (reduced to 170 hp or fewer), they’ll become amenable to parley, if anyone thinks to address them in Giant. (They don’t speak any other language.) When seriously wounded (reduced to 97 hp or fewer), they withdraw, throwing rocks behind them to deter pursuit.

Next: the Returned.

title: Typhon Tactics
content_text:
The typhon has some of the best flavor text I’ve read in any fifth edition Dungeons & Dragons monster sourcebook—so good, it’s difficult for me to paraphrase, as I often do, because it nails the creature’s vibe perfectly. If you have Mythic Odysseys of Theros, go read it (it’s on page 246), then come back.

OK, down to business. The typhon is a Huge monstrosity with extraordinary Strength and Constitution—an unambiguous melee brute—whose other ability scores pale in comparison. It’s not mindless, but it’s not a bright bulb, either: Its Intelligence 7 is on par with a caveman’s, and while its Wisdom and Charisma are higher than humanoid average, their influence on its combat behavior is modest.

Its Multiattack comprises one use each of its three melee attacks: Flurry of Bites, Constrict and Maw. The key thing here is to use Constrict first, because it grapples and restrains the target on a hit, conferring advantage on its follow-up Flurry and Maw attack rolls. It also has the longest reach of the three: 15 feet vs. 10 feet.

Combined with the typhon’s 40-foot speed, what we have here is a creature that scrambles forth from its lair, or wherever else it happens to be lurking, snatches a victim with whatever limb it uses to Constrict with (a detail that’s omitted from both the stat block and the flavor text—I guess it’s part of the typhon’s “writhing flesh”) when it comes within 15 feet, then comes 5 feet closer and bites the heck out of them. To ensure that it’s able to make those last two attacks, it chooses a target that’s no more than 50 feet from its initial position.

So how does it choose a target? As it happens, target selection is one cognitive task it’s good at. Its Armor Class is a respectable 17, and it’s got plenty of hit points, so it’s not particularly afraid of front-line warriors. On the other hand, of the big three saving throws, the only one it has particularly robust saving throws in is Constitution. In fact, despite having a high Constitution modifier already, it’s also proficient in Con saves, giving it a whopping +10 Con save modifier. In contrast, its Dexterity and Wisdom save modifiers are +0 and +1. Sad! It’s enough to produce a dim but present awareness that spellcasters are very dangerous and that it should devour them first.

Being a Huge creature, it’s unimpeded by merely Medium-size humanoids in its path, and it will storm right over them, heedless of opportunity attacks, in order to get at a spellcaster—preferably, one in the back, because they tend to be the tenderest morsels. (Clerics often have hard shells.) That said, unless they’re dressed in a stereotypical fashion, a typhon may not immediately recognize a spellcaster as a spellcaster—but it recognizes spellcasting immediately, and promptly rectifies its oversight.

A moderately wounded typhon (reduced to 136 hit points or fewer) is willing to consider the possibility that it may be outmatched. At this point, the difficulty of the encounter (see Dungeon Master’s Guide chapter 3, “Creating a Combat Encounter”) becomes relevant. If the encounter is Deadly, it keeps fighting. If the encounter is Hard, it keeps fighting until it’s seriously wounded (reduced to 78 hit points or fewer). At that point, or if the encounter is Easy or Moderate, the typhon balks, and it makes use of the fact that it’s capable of speaking and understanding Common, asking its foes what it is they want. It’s not going to accept “Your death!” as an answer, but it’s open to other very simple accommodations that permit it to live. Remember, we’re talking caveman Intelligence here. It can strike a concrete deal consisting of basic action verbs and common nouns (“Leave this place,” “Shun that place,” “Spare that person”), but it can’t negotiate a treaty.

Next: Theran giants.

title: Theran Hydra Tactics
content_text:
The ironscale hydra is fundamentally no different from an ordinary hydra. It has a higher Armor Class, it’s faster on land and in the water, and it’s stronger and tougher (but also less dexterous). It also has one trait that the basic hydra doesn’t have: Acidic Blood, which splashes every creature in its immediate vicinity with acid anytime it takes piercing or slashing damage. None of these differences, however, is great enough to change its tactics from those of the basic hydra. So let’s skip forward to Polukranos, the World-Eater, a legendary ironscale hydra with Intelligence on par with a monkey’s—just enough to solve problems and exhibit malice.

With Polukranos (“Many-Heads”—personally, I’d have spelled it “Polykranos,” since we’re used to seeing both Ancient Greek and modern Greek υ transliterated as y, and the stem poly- is in, well, basically everything), we have not only higher Intelligence but higher Wisdom, enough to make a major difference in its willingness to pick a fight, plus the Siege Monster trait and some legendary actions. It has a higher Charisma, too, but the only thing this ability really affects is its susceptibility to banishment, and since it’s a monstrosity, it comes right back after 1 minute anyway. Its added Stomp and Tail attacks come into play only as part of its legendary actions; on its turn, it does nothing but Bite with each of its heads.

A basic hydra positions itself wherever it can attack as many targets as possible, up to the number of heads it has, and attacks every one of them once, then uses any leftover heads to Bite the biggest, then the next-biggest and so on—unless someone has just cut off one of its heads, in which case both of the replacement heads attack them. Polukranos is a little smarter than that. It has excellent senses (expertise in Perception) and will often notice when its enemies are hiding behind cover or inside a structure, and it relentlessly strikes at the cover or structure until it’s destroyed before turning its attention to its thus-exposed targets. (It’s mechanistic in this tendency, and its foes can exploit it by running from cover to cover—if enough different sources of cover are available—in order to direct more of its damage output toward inanimate objects and less of it at themselves.) The reach of its Bite attack is 15 feet, and it doesn’t come closer to its targets than that unless it has to. If they want to run up and expose themselves to its Acidic Blood every time they land a hit, that’s up to them.

The big gun among Polukranos’s legendary actions is Trampling Charge, which costs its entire legendary action budget for the turn; if it uses Trampling Charge, it’s not using either of its other legendary actions, and vice versa. My initial thought was that, since Stomp deals slightly less damage than Tail, for Trampling Charge to be a better choice than simply using Tail Swipe three times there must be at least four foes in its 50-foot-long, straight-line path. (Since Polukranos occupies a space 20 feet wide, it’s easier to get foes lined up in front of it for Trampling Charge than for, say, a lightning bolt spell, whose area of effect is only 5 feet wide.)

But that’s assuming that it has at least one Tail Swipe target within reach (15 feet). The tricky thing about legendary actions that don’t include movement is that a monster has to use them where it is. If Polukranos is too particular about how many opponents Trampling Charge will affect, it may find itself without any targets in reach to make Tail attacks against. So let’s give it some grace and say that it’s enough to have three opponents in its path, because the alternative is waiting for those opponents to run up to it and make attacks of their own, and generally only a couple of front-line warriors will be bold enough to do something like that.

There’s another catch to Trampling Charge, however: None of the foes in its path can be behind cover. Trampling Charge explicitly deals damage only to creatures, and while Polukranos can move through the space of any Huge or smaller creature, it can’t move through objects or structures it can’t budge. Now, given its size and Strength, it can budge a lot: With a carrying capacity of 3,000 pounds, it can push, drag, or lift twice that weight. A chariot might not take damage from Polukranos’s Trampling Charge, since it’s not a creature, but it would definitely be sent flying. But a tree, rooted in the ground, would stop Polukranos in its tracks; ditto a stone wall.

Thus, the prerequisites for Trampling Charge are still rather steep: three or more targets, arrayed within 50 feet of Polukranos in one direction, in a band 20 feet wide, with no other obstacle to block Polukranos’s movement. If these prerequisites are met anytime after Polukranos’s turn ends and it hasn’t yet taken any other legendary action, this one is its automatic choice. Alas, it doesn’t have the Intelligence to recognize when its enemies are almost in position, nor does it have the patience to wait and see whether they’ll go ahead and line up for it. If the prerequisites aren’t met, it goes straight to one of its other legendary actions.

Like a dragon, Polukranos uses its Detect legendary action anytime an opponent attempts to move stealthily within its line of sight. Tail Swipe is simply a free attack, a default option to use when there’s an enemy within reach and neither other legendary action applies.

Even with Intelligence 4 instead of 2, Polukranos is still instinct-bound, unable to vary its combat behavior. But with Wisdom 14, it knows better than to tangle with anyone who poses a real danger to it. It lacks the ability to communicate, so it can’t talk its way out of a confrontation, but if the encounter with Polukranos is of Deadly difficulty (see Dungeon Master’s Guide chapter 3, “Creating a Combat Encounter”), it attacks without hesitation, whereas if it’s of Hard difficulty, Polukranos balks, waiting to see what its foes will do. If the encounter is Easy or Medium, Polukranos doesn’t even wait around; it runs away or dives underwater, and heroes who want to slay it will have to chase after it to get the chance. Only if its pursuers catch up to it and engage it in combat does it fight back, its monstrous nature getting the better of its judgment.

Regardless of encounter difficulty, Polukranos attempts to flee when seriously wounded (reduced to 84 hp or fewer), instinctively Disengaging if it’s within more than one enemy’s reach and Dashing otherwise.

Next: Typhon.

title: Making Enemies, Available for Preorder
content_text:
As roleplaying gamers, we make up stories—and as gamemasters, we select many of the elements out of which these stories are made. We come up with a theme, we set the scenes and the mood, we populate the world, and perhaps most important of all, we create the antagonists against which the player characters must struggle. We decide how powerful they’ll be, what motivates them, how they can be overcome, and what effects victory and defeat will have upon the world around them.

For this purpose, the games we play provide an abundance of fantastic and horrific creatures that can fill the antagonist role, from mindless to hyperintelligent, laughably hapless to devastating, indifferent or oblivious to single-mindedly malicious. There are so many choices, in fact, that it can sometimes be hard to find the right one among them.

And yet sometimes, despite having all these choices, we find that none of them is quite right for the particular story we want to tell. In which case, we have to invent monsters of our own.

In The Monsters Know What They’re Doing, the essential tactics guide for Dungeon Masters, and its sequel, MOAR! Monsters Know What They’re Doing, I reverse-engineered hundreds of fifth edition Dungeons & Dragons monsters to help DMs prepare battle plans for combat encounters before their game sessions. Now, in Making Enemies—coming Oct. 7 from Saga Press—I explore everything that goes into creating monsters from the ground up:

Size, number, and level of challenge

Monster habitats

Monster motivations

Monsters as metaphors

Monsters and magic

The monstrous anatomy possessed by real-world organisms

How to customize monsters for your own tabletop roleplaying game adventuring party to confront 

No longer limited to one game system, Making Enemies shows you how to build out your creations not just for D&D 5E but also for Pathfinder 2E, Shadowdark, the Cypher System, and Call of Cthulhu 7E. Including interviews with some of the most brilliant names in RPG and creature design, Making Enemies gives you the tools to surprise and delight your players—and terrify their characters—again and again.

Click here to preorder today from your favorite independent bookseller, or follow one of these links:

BookshopAmazonBarnes & NobleIndigoKoboGoogle PlayApple Books

If you’d prefer to support your friendly local game store with your purchase, ask the buyer to contact Simon & Schuster Distribution or ACD Distribution to order wholesale.

title: Theran Chimera Tactics
content_text:
The chimera in the 2014 Monster Manual is a chaotic evil monstrosity with a challenge rating of 6; you can read my previous thoughts on it here. The Theran chimera in Mythic Odysseys of Theros has a CR of 7, reflecting a handful of tweaks to the basic chimera’s stat block: a slightly higher Dexterity, giving it a slightly higher Armor Class; slightly greater damage from its claws; proficiency in Constitution and Wisdom saving throws; and the Spell Turning trait we saw previously in the fleecemane lion. (Also, its breath weapon damage is calculated differently, but the average is roughly the same; it loses the chimera’s double proficiency in Perception; and it actually has 17 percent fewer hit points.) Finally, its Bite attack is replaced by a Tail attack with a 10-foot reach but the same damage as before, and the damage types of Head (previously Horns) and Tail (previously Bite) are swapped, with Tail dealing bludgeoning damage and Head dealing piercing.

Except for the save proficiencies, the hit points and Spell Turning, these are all fairly superficial changes. The key thing is, its ability contour doesn’t change at all, and therefore its combat role doesn’t change. It’s still a brute melee fighter. All the tactics of the basic MM chimera still apply, with one addition: The increased range on its Tail attack lets it make an opportunity attack against a foe who never got within 5 feet of it but did get within 10 and is now trying to move away.

However, all that being said, the Theran chimera isn’t just its basic stat block. It’s a Chimera Construction Set, with tables that allow you to customize its body composition, breath weapon, and head and tail attacks, and some of these changes do make a difference.

If a Theran chimera’s body is that of a plains creature, for instance, it can’t fly, so difficult terrain doesn’t favor it the way it favors a basic chimera. This kind of chimera will avoid mountains, swamps, ice and other troublesome ground. If its body is “that of a coastal or aquatic creature, such as a heron or a shark” (fly-ing shark, doo doo doo-doo doo-doo!), it gains a swimming speed and the ability to breathe water as well as air, but this modification is only advantageous if the fight is actually in or on water. This kind of chimera is still strong on land, but not as strong as it is in the water, so it instinctively prefers to initiate combat far from shore. A Theran chimera with the body of a mountain creature doesn’t provoke opportunity attacks when it flies out of an enemy’s reach—effectively granting it the Flyby trait—and thus it prefers to remain airborne at all times, ending its turn where its sword-wielding foes can’t hit it. And if it has the body of a swamp or subterranean creature, it effectively gains the Spider Climb trait, allowing it to drop down on its prey or aim its breath weapon from tree branches or cavern ceilings.

Lightning Breath changes the area of effect of the Theran chimera’s breath weapon from a 15-foot cone to a 60-foot line. It’s pretty easy to strike the two opponents necessary to make this ability worthwhile under 2014 rules, but under 2024 rules, a 60-foot linear effect is expected to strike three opponents (Dungeon Master’s Guide chapter 3, Targets in Areas of Effect), which may not always be feasible. Here I’ll emphasize that chimeras, including Theran chimeras, will reposition themselves without leaving their enemies’ reach to optimize their breath weapon attacks if they can, but they’re not above soaking up an opportunity attack or two if they have to move out of reach to get a better angle. Lightning Breath deals more damage than the baseline chimera breath weapon, so if a Theran chimera can hit three opponents with it, it’s a big deal—big enough, perhaps, to hold off using it until its enemies are properly positioned.

Tidal Wave breath has the same area of effect as Lightning Breath; it deals less damage but can knock targets prone. I would absolutely say that if a target is standing on a boat or ship with their back to the water, being knocked prone means being knocked overboard. Venom Spray and Necrotic Breath come with nasty riders but don’t offer any specific tactical advantages.

A Theran chimera with the head of a bull can execute a charge attack to deal some extra piercing damage, making it a no-brainer to initiate combat this way (logically, this head should be the chimera’s middle one, or that’s going to be one weird-looking charge). A shark head that smells blood has advantage on its attacks, incentivizing it to attack the same target over and over. A cockatrice head can petrify a target it pecks; during the petrifaction process, the target is temporarily restrained, priming follow-up attacks. A unicorn head deals extra damage but offers no tactical advantage.

A Shark Tail pushes a target it hits up to 10 feet, an even bett er method of knocking foes off their vessels and into the water. A Constricting Tail grapples and restrains, priming follow-up attacks against targets its hits. Venomous Tail has no particular tactical advantage. A Theran chimera with a Perplexing Tail loses the benefit of its 10-foot reach, and it’s advantageous only to the extent that the new head (which, according to the rules as written, must come from the Head Attacks table) has something to offer, which in my opinion excludes Bull Horns. This thing isn’t going to charge an enemy by running backward. In fact, once you have a creature with a rear attack that doesn’t come with extra reach, facing becomes a major consideration, and given fifth edition Dungeons & Dragons players’ distaste for anything involving facing, I’d simply steer clear of Perplexing Tail.

Theran chimeras might still fight to the death, but since they’re unaligned rather than chaotic evil, I’d argue that they’re more likely to behave like natural predators, with their monstrous rather than bestial nature goading them to keep attacking past the point of being merely moderately wounded. Their lower hit point maximum means that a seriously wounded chimera will withdraw when it’s reduced to 38 hp or fewer.

Next: Theran hydras.

title: Fleecemane Lion Tactics
content_text:
When they’re not shooting shampoo commercials, fleecemane lions guard places where the power of Nyx seeps into the material world of Theros. Although their challenge rating is only 3, they’re considered legendary creatures, which can take two (rather than the usual three) legendary actions per round.

Fleecemane lions are extraordinarily strong. You might expect them to be brutes, but with very high Dexterity—higher than their Constitution—they’re actually shock attackers. They’re also ambushers, with proficiency in Perception and Stealth. However, while they can make their first attack from hiding in order to gain unseen-attacker advantage, they usually don’t, because their Pounce ability is both more powerful and what makes them memorable.

In fact, although it’s not explicitly laid out in the fleecemane’s stat block, its traits dictate a fairly rigid script for it to follow. To tease out the sequence, let’s look at each of its traits.

First, Keen Smell: This trait helps the fleecemane detect its opponents before they detect it. (Note that since Monsters of the Multiverse, and in the 2024 revised rules, Dungeons & Dragons has deprecated the Keen Sense traits and switched to giving creatures double proficiency in Perception instead. That would make the fleecemane’s Perception modifier +6 rather than +4.)

Second, Spell Turning: This trait is purely passive, a nasty surprise for spellcasters but not something that’s going to influence its behavior except to make it bolder around them.

Third, Running Leap: This trait lets it make a 25-foot running long jump, which seems pretty great until you realize that its Strength alone allows it to make a 19-foot running long jump anyway. Also, all the fleecemane’s movement on its turn has to take place within the limit dictated by its 50-foot speed. If it were to jump beyond this limit, it would simply hang in midair while all its opponents took their turns, completing its movement when its turn came back around. In the first round of combat, when the fleecemane wants to deliver as much damage as it can, that would be a pretty big mistake. Instead, the main value of Running Leap is in bypassing difficult terrain (which gives you, the Dungeon Master, an incentive to include difficult terrain in your encounter location).

The essential trait is Pounce, which grants the fleecemane the ability to knock its target prone and deliver an extra attack as a bonus action provided it has a 20-foot, straight-line running start. (Running Leap makes its Pounce more dramatic, but mechanically, it doesn’t add anything. It’s all flavor.) To gain these benefits, the fleecemane lion must initiate combat when its chosen target is 25 to 55 feet away from it (25 to 40 feet if the terrain is difficult). That’s the condition that causes it to strike.

Since the fleecemane lion can’t count on an unseen attack, it needs to take advantage of surprise instead. An interesting—and poorly understood—aspect of 5E14’s surprise rules allows the fleecemane to gain a benefit even before it leaps into battle. Initiative is rolled when any creature decides to take hostile action. The fleecemane’s Dexterity modifier (and therefore initiative modifier) is +3, decent but by no means unbeatable. There’s a good chance that another creature will roll higher on initiative than the fleecemane. If they do, the fleecemane can use its two legendary actions to Roar at the end of their turn. That means that even before it shows itself, one or more of its opponents may already be stricken with the frightened condition.

But the fleecemane lion does not want to do that. Not only does the effect have a fairly low, easily resisted DC, it wears off at the end of the fleecemane’s next turn—that is, before the fleecemane’s opponents can even collect their wits enough to know what’s going on. The condition is wasted: If they can’t act, it doesn’t matter whether they have disadvantage on their attack rolls and ability checks. The fleecemane needs to save the Roar for when its opponents are about to start doing stuff. Even then, there’s no perfect moment for it: Because it ends as soon as the fleecemane finishes its turn, and because legendary actions must always be taken at the end of another creature’s turn, whoever takes their turn immediately after the fleecemane can never be frightened by its Roar. If it Roars at the end of that character’s turn, however, it at least has a chance to frighten everyone else. The only way around this restriction is to throw in a second fleecemane and to have it take its turn on a different initiative account from the first, which isn’t really comme il faut.

Having examined this tactic and ruled it out, we’re back to the simple, straightforward approach of kicking off combat with a run-and-pounce. When the target comes within range, the fleecemane lion bursts out from its hiding spot and Multiattacks (one Claw, one Bite). If the terrain is difficult, it uses the first 20 feet of its movement to cover 10 feet of ground, then uses Running Leap to clear the remaining distance, up to 25 feet more. If it succeeds in knocking its target down, it takes a second Bite as a bonus action.

Because the fleecemane is a shock attacker, it wants to maximize the damage it deals out of the gate. Thus, it uses both of its legendary actions to Claw at its target. By the time it finishes its second turn, its foes will have rallied and be ready to fight back, but Roar is still premature; the fleecemane still has important damage to do.

As guardian creatures, fleecemane lions are undeterred by injury and will fight to the death. However, if after two rounds of combat they still haven’t brought down their prey, their shock attacker nature kicks in: They make one more Multiattack, then use their full movement to back off (potentially provoking one or more opportunity attacks—they don’t have the Intelligence or the instinct to Disengage). From this distance, at the end of the turn of the next creature to act, they finally take the Roar legendary action. If the fight remains unfinished, they engage any foe who runs up to them but otherwise circle their foes with a wary eye, looking for a good opportunity to rush in and Pounce again, Roaring each round to keep their enemies frightened. It won’t keep a spellcaster from firing off a spell that requires its target to make a saving throw, but that’s OK—that’s what Spell Turning is for.

Fleecemane lions are about as smart as an ape, but that’s not enough for them to be able to size up an opponent’s weaknesses; to these Large creatures, all PCs look weak, especially the Small ones. But they do have good intuition for whether a group of potential enemies outclasses them. If an encounter with a fleecemane lion would be Easy or Medium difficulty, then rather than pounce on trespassers like prey, it emerges slowly from its hiding place, growling in warning—trying to deter them without having to fight. In this case, as soon as an opponent makes like they’re going to fight regardless, the fleecemane replies with a Roar, saving the Claw legendary actions until battle is joined. If it’s left room to Pounce on its turn, it does. But it doesn’t run; it’s a guardian, and it guards, as long as it can hold out. And it’s a monstrosity, indicating that it’s more brutal than a common beast. When it brings a foe down, it doesn’t stop attacking them—it kills them dead before moving on to other prey.

In 5E24, being surprised merely imposes disadvantage on opponents’ initiative rolls, which means a fleecemane lion’s opponents will begin fighting back immediately. Rather than have it resort to a premature Roar—it is still a shock attacker, after all, and needs to deal as much damage as it can, as fast as it can, at the outset of combat—I suggest following the example of the 5E24 stat blocks we’ve already seen and removing the saving throw from Pounce, making the prone condition automatic. You might even do the same with Roar. What’s the point of a DC of 12, anyway?

Next: Theran chimeras.

title: Cerberus Tactics
content_text:
Modern roleplaying games often include minotaurs as a species, but in Greek myth, there was only one: the Minotaur. Our krakens descend from the Kraken of Nordic lore; our medusas, from Medusa. “Leviathan” and “behemoth” likewise both began as Hebrew proper nouns. Yet for some reason, Mythic Odysseys of Theros’s inclusion of “cerberi” as a genus of monstrosities hits me as being particularly goofy. It’s as if Van Richten’s Guide to Ravenloft had included a listing for “draculas.”

It’s commonly believed that the Cerberus of myth had three heads, but in fact, he was most often depicted in art as having only two, and in a few cases with only one. Theros presents a lesser, two-headed species of cerberus and a more formidable, three-headed species. In other respects, however, the two-headed cerberus and the Underworld cerberus are essentially the same:

They’re fast-moving ambush predators, with proficiency in Stealth and double proficiency in Perception.

They’re immune to fire and necrotic damage and can’t be surprised, charmed, frightened, blinded, deafened, exhausted or stunned.
